syllabus_desc: Describe the weather sequence and general flying conditions associated with: cold fronts
concept: A cold front is where a wedge of cold, denser air shoves under warmer air and lifts it sharply. Because the lift is abrupt and the warm air is often unstable, you get vigorous vertical development — towering cumulus and Cb along a narrow band, heavy showers, thunderstorms, gusty winds, hail, and severe turbulence in the active zone. Behind the front the air clears rapidly, visibility improves dramatically, and you're often left with cool southerlies and scattered showers. For an IFR flight, a cold front is one of the bigger hazards you'll plan around — the frontal band can be narrow but violent, with embedded Cb that radar doesn't always paint cleanly. A classic NZ example is a southerly change pushing up the country — Christchurch to Wellington can go from calm to wild in under an hour.
exam_tip: Cold front = unstable, narrow band of violent weather, clears quickly behind. Think Cb, turbulence, heavy showers, windshear, then rapid improvement. In the Southern Hemisphere the wind backs across the front.
key_facts: Cold dense air undercuts warm air and forces it sharply upward
Unstable lifting produces Cu, TCu and Cb in a narrow band
Hazards: heavy showers, thunderstorms, hail, severe turbulence, windshear, icing
Clearance is usually rapid once the front passes
In the Southern Hemisphere wind backs across a cold front (e.g., NW to SW)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather sequence and general flying conditions associated with: warm fronts
concept: A warm front is the opposite picture — warm, lighter air slides up and over a retreating wedge of colder air. The slope is shallow (typically 1:150 or so), which means the cloud system stretches hundreds of miles ahead of the surface position. You'll see cirrus first, then cirrostratus, altostratus, and finally nimbostratus right at the front, with steady rain or drizzle, low ceilings, poor visibility, and a high chance of embedded stratiform icing if you're flying in cloud above the freezing level. For an IFR pilot, warm fronts are less violent than cold fronts but more insidious — the weather closes in slowly, your destination quietly goes below minima, and the icing can be persistent because you're in cloud for a long time.
exam_tip: Warm front = stable, widespread stratiform cloud, steady rain, poor visibility, big area of IFR conditions. Cloud sequence: Ci → Cs → As → Ns. Main IFR concerns are ceiling, visibility, and persistent airframe icing.
key_facts: Warm air rides up over colder air on a shallow slope
Cloud sequence ahead of front: Ci, Cs, As, Ns
Produces steady rain/drizzle, low ceilings, and poor visibility
Extensive area of IMC well ahead of the surface position
Persistent stratiform icing is a major IFR hazard above the freezing level
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService training

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather sequence and general flying conditions associated with: occluded fronts
concept: An occluded front is what happens when a faster cold front catches up to the warm front ahead of it and lifts the warm sector clear of the surface. You effectively end up with two cold air masses meeting, with the old warm air squeezed aloft. On the chart it's drawn with alternating cold-front barbs and warm-front bumps on the same line. Weather-wise you get a messy combination of both parent fronts — layered stratiform cloud from the warm front mixed with embedded convective cells from the cold front, widespread rain, poor visibility, icing, and patches of turbulence. Occlusions are common in NZ, particularly as depressions mature over the Tasman and track across the country.
exam_tip: Occluded front = cold front has caught the warm front. Expect mixed warm-front and cold-front weather: widespread rain plus embedded convection, poor visibility, icing. Two types: cold occlusion (coldest air behind) and warm occlusion (coldest air ahead).
key_facts: Forms when the cold front overtakes the warm front ahead of it
Warm sector is lifted clear of the surface
Weather combines warm-front stratiform cloud and cold-front convective showers
Cold occlusion: coldest air is behind; warm occlusion: coldest air is ahead
Common in mature NZ depressions crossing the country
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService training

syllabus_desc: Describe the weather sequence and general flying conditions associated with: stationary fronts
concept: A stationary front is a boundary between two air masses where neither one is really winning — the front isn't moving much in either direction. The weather associated with it depends on the moisture and stability of the two air masses, but you'll typically see persistent cloud, drizzle or rain, and low visibility sitting over the same area for a long time. For IFR operations the main issue is persistence — a stationary front parked across your route or destination can keep conditions below minima for many hours or even days. The front is drawn with alternating cold-front barbs on one side and warm-front bumps on the other, pointing in opposite directions.
exam_tip: Stationary front = neither air mass advancing. Persistent cloud and precipitation, potentially for days. For IFR planning the worry is duration, not intensity — you may not be able to wait it out.
key_facts: Boundary where neither air mass is advancing
Weather similar to a weak warm front — stratiform cloud, drizzle/rain, low visibility
Persists over the same area for extended periods
Depicted with alternating cold/warm front symbols pointing in opposite directions
Main IFR issue is duration of below-minima conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService training

syllabus_desc: Describe typical wind speeds and directions ahead of and behind these fronts in mid-latitudes
concept: Ahead of a cold front in the mid-latitudes of the Southern Hemisphere you'll typically find winds from the NW to N around 15-30 kt, sometimes stronger as the front approaches. Behind the cold front the wind backs (remember, in the SH the wind backs across a cold front — it's the mirror of the NH) to SW or S, often strengthening to 20-35 kt with gusts. Ahead of a warm front the wind is usually N to NE at moderate strength. After the warm front passes the wind veers to NW and can ease slightly. These are typical numbers — actual values depend on the pressure gradient and strength of the system. The NZ westerly belt means these patterns repeat week after week.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere rule: wind BACKS across a cold front (e.g., NW to SW), VEERS across a warm front. This is the opposite of what you'd read in a FAA textbook. Know it cold for the exam.
key_facts: Ahead of a cold front: NW-N winds, 15-30 kt typical in NZ
Behind a cold front: SW-S winds, 20-35 kt, often gusty
Ahead of a warm front: N-NE winds at moderate strength
In the Southern Hemisphere wind BACKS across a cold front and VEERS across a warm front
Actual strength depends on the pressure gradient
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Explain how subsidence and ascent of air influences the type of weather commonly associated with pressure systems
concept: Subsidence means sinking air. Ascent means rising air. That simple distinction drives most of the weather on a synoptic chart. In a high, the air is subsiding — as it sinks it warms adiabatically, the relative humidity drops, and cloud evaporates. That's why anticyclones give you clear skies, light winds, good visibility in the middle of the high, and often a subsidence inversion capping anything that's trying to form lower down. In a low, the air is rising — as it cools adiabatically the moisture condenses, clouds form, and you get the precipitation and poor weather you'd expect. Troughs are essentially bands of ascent; ridges are bands of subsidence. Once you have this picture in your head, a synoptic chart tells you immediately where the good and bad weather is.
exam_tip: Subsidence (sinking air) → warming, drying, clear skies, good visibility → highs and ridges. Ascent (rising air) → cooling, condensation, cloud, rain → lows and troughs. This is the single biggest takeaway from synoptic charts.
key_facts: Subsiding air warms adiabatically and dries out — produces clear skies and good visibility
Rising air cools adiabatically, condenses moisture — produces cloud and precipitation
Highs and ridges = subsidence; lows and troughs = ascent
Subsidence inversions cap convection and trap haze/pollution below
Explains why the centre of a high is usually fine and the centre of a low is usually wet
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService training

syllabus_desc: Identify the general direction of movement of pressure systems in the mid-latitudes of the Southern Hemisphere
concept: In the mid-latitudes of the Southern Hemisphere, pressure systems generally move from west to east. That's because the prevailing upper-level westerlies (the same ones that give NZ its 'Roaring Forties' reputation) steer the surface systems along with them. Highs tend to track more slowly — often drifting at 15-25 kt — and can stall for days over the Tasman or eastern Australia. Lows and fronts move quicker, typically 25-40 kt, sometimes faster when they're well embedded in a strong westerly flow. For an IFR flight planner this means you can usually get a rough idea of where the weather will be in 12 or 24 hours by just shifting the chart east at those speeds, though the official forecasts are obviously much more refined than that.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere mid-latitudes: pressure systems move west to east. Highs slower (~15-25 kt), lows and fronts faster (~25-40 kt). The prevailing westerlies do the steering.
key_facts: Mid-latitude Southern Hemisphere systems move west to east
Steered by the upper-level westerly flow
Highs typically move at 15-25 kt and can stall
Lows and fronts typically move at 25-40 kt
NZ lies right in the westerly belt — hence the constant procession of weather
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Define the 'westerly index' over New Zealand
concept: The westerly index is a MetService term that measures how strong the prevailing westerly flow is over NZ at a given time. Technically it's the difference in mean sea level pressure between the Auckland/Northland area and the southern South Island — a bigger pressure difference means a tighter gradient and stronger westerlies. A 'high' westerly index means strong west-to-east flow across the country; a 'low' westerly index means the flow is weak or even reversed (often with blocking highs or lows dominating instead). It's a really useful shorthand for describing the synoptic regime in NZ because our weather is so dominated by how strong the westerly stream is at any given time.
exam_tip: Westerly index = strength of the west-to-east flow across NZ, measured by the pressure difference between the north and south of the country. High index = strong westerlies, low index = weak or blocked flow. This is an NZ-specific concept you won't see in overseas textbooks.
key_facts: NZ MetService concept measuring the strength of the prevailing westerly flow
Based on the MSL pressure difference between northern and southern NZ
High westerly index = strong westerlies across NZ
Low westerly index = weak, blocked or reversed flow
Useful shorthand for describing the NZ synoptic regime
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Identify 'high' and 'low' westerly indices on weather maps
concept: You identify high and low westerly indices on a weather map by looking at the MSL isobars across NZ. A high westerly index shows up as tight, closely-spaced isobars running roughly west-east across the whole country, often with a strong high north of NZ and a deep low or trough to the south. The pressure difference between the top of the North Island and the bottom of the South Island will be large — that's the tight gradient driving strong westerlies. A low westerly index shows up as isobars that are widely spaced, wavy, or running in some other direction — often because a big anticyclone is sitting right over NZ, or because a blocking pattern has set up with a low to the north and a high to the south (a reversed gradient). On the NZ synoptic chart you can usually tell within a few seconds which regime you're looking at.
exam_tip: Look at isobar spacing across NZ. Tight, west-east isobars with high north/low south = high westerly index. Widely spaced, wavy, or reversed isobars = low westerly index. Practice on a few MetService charts and it becomes obvious quickly.
key_facts: Tight west-east isobars across NZ = high westerly index
Widely spaced or wavy isobars = low westerly index
Pattern of high north / low south produces strongest westerlies
Blocking high over NZ or reversed gradient = low westerly index
Read it straight off a MetService MSL analysis chart
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Explain the weather distribution across New Zealand in high and low westerly index situations
concept: In a high westerly index situation the western side of both islands (west coast South Island, Taranaki, Waikato west) gets hammered — orographic cloud, rain, strong NW winds, poor visibility, low ceilings. The eastern side meanwhile often sees the Föhn effect: dry warm NW winds roaring down the lee of the ranges, little cloud, very good visibility but gusty mechanical turbulence. That's the classic 'nor'wester' regime on the Canterbury Plains. In a low westerly index situation, with a blocking high sitting over or near NZ, weather is generally more settled and variable — individual weather features like sea breezes, local convection, light variable winds, and overnight fog can dominate rather than the big westerly flow. Low index can also mean a reversed gradient with easterlies bringing moisture and cloud onto the east coast instead.
exam_tip: High index = wet/windy west, Föhn nor'wester on the east (Canterbury Plains classic). Low index = settled or easterly regimes, more localised weather. Know the Canterbury nor'wester cold.
key_facts: High index: wet and windy on the west coast; Föhn effect on the east (Canterbury nor'wester)
West coast South Island gets heavy orographic rain in high index flows
Low index: more settled weather, or reversed easterly flow
Blocking highs with a low index can bring foggy calm mornings over much of the country
The westerly index sets the overall character of NZ weather day by day
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: Describe the significance of high and low westerly index situations across New Zealand to aviation
concept: For aviation, the westerly index is basically a quick summary of where the hazards will be. A high westerly index regime means strong turbulence over and downwind of the ranges (mountain waves, rotor zones, severe mechanical turbulence in the nor'wester), icing in the orographic cloud on the windward side, low ceilings and poor visibility on the west coast, and high crosswinds at exposed aerodromes like Wellington, Christchurch and Queenstown. A low westerly index is usually less hazardous in terms of wind and turbulence but brings its own problems — radiation fog at morning and evening, sea breeze convergence zones, and blocked systems that can sit below minima for days. In IFR planning it's one of the first things you should check because it immediately tells you what kind of day you're dealing with.
exam_tip: High index hazards = mountain waves, severe turbulence, icing on the west, nor'wester crosswinds. Low index hazards = fog, stagnant below-minima conditions. Both have their traps — don't assume low index means safe.
key_facts: High index: mountain waves, rotor turbulence, icing on west coast, big crosswinds at exposed aerodromes
Low index: fog, persistent below-minima conditions, local convergence weather
High westerly index is when NZ aerodromes see the most mountain wave activity
The index tells you the type of hazard to expect on a given day
IFR planners use it as a quick synoptic overview
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in New Zealand

syllabus_desc: Define 'pressure gradient'
concept: Pressure gradient is the rate at which pressure changes with horizontal distance across a chart. Think of it as the slope of the pressure field — the steeper the slope, the more 'push' the air feels, and the stronger the wind. On a surface chart you read it straight off the isobar spacing. Close spacing = steep gradient = strong wind. Wide spacing = weak gradient = light wind. That's why the first thing an experienced pilot does when they pick up a MetService chart is eyeball the isobar spacing over their route.
exam_tip: Pressure gradient = horizontal rate of change of pressure. Tight isobars = strong gradient = strong wind. It's literally a slope on the pressure field.
key_facts: Pressure gradient is the rate of change of pressure with horizontal distance
Measured in hPa per unit distance
Drives the pressure gradient force, which is what makes wind blow
Shown by isobar spacing on a synoptic chart
Close isobars = strong gradient; wide isobars = weak gradient
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify strong and weak pressure gradients on a weather map
concept: Strong pressure gradients show up as tightly packed isobars — often just 2 or 4 hPa apart crammed close together. You'll see this around deep lows or where a vigorous front is pushing through. Expect strong winds, potentially 30 kt+ at the surface and more aloft. Weak pressure gradients show up as isobars spaced widely apart, sometimes almost absent — typical in the middle of a large high. Expect light and variable surface winds. As a rule of thumb on the NZ MSL analysis: if you can fit your thumb between two isobars the wind is probably light, if you can't fit a finger between them it's going to be rough.
exam_tip: Strong gradient = tight isobars = strong wind. Weak gradient = widely spaced isobars = light wind. Simple inspection of the chart.
key_facts: Strong gradient = closely spaced isobars (strong winds)
Weak gradient = widely spaced isobars (light winds)
NZ MetService charts typically use 4 hPa isobar intervals
Strong gradients often found around deep lows and active fronts
Weak gradients typical in the core of large anticyclones
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Given examples of ambient temperature at a stated altitude, calculate: the ISA temperature at that altitude
concept: ISA temperature at altitude is straightforward: start with 15°C at sea level and lose 2°C for every 1,000 ft up to 36,090 ft (where it stabilises at -56.5°C). So if you're given an ambient temperature at, say, 8,000 ft, the ISA at that altitude is 15 - (8 × 2) = -1°C. The difference between actual and ISA is often expressed as ISA+X or ISA-X (e.g., -1°C at 8,000 ft is ISA standard; if the actual was +5°C, that's ISA+6). You'll use this constantly for performance calculations and for estimating density altitude on the fly.
exam_tip: ISA temp = 15 - (altitude in thousands × 2). ISA tropopause -56.5°C at 36,090 ft. Learn the formula and practice a few calculations.
key_facts: ISA sea level = 15°C, 1013.25 hPa
Lapse rate 2°C per 1,000 ft (standard)
Tropopause at 36,090 ft, -56.5°C
Formula: ISA temp = 15 - (alt_ft/1000 × 2)
Used for performance, density altitude, and TAS calculations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Given examples of ambient temperature at a stated altitude, calculate: the ISA height at that temperature
concept: The reverse calculation — given an ambient temperature, what altitude would have that temperature in ISA? Rearrange the standard formula: ISA height = ((15 - temp) / 2) × 1,000 ft. For example, -5°C is ISA at 10,000 ft. It's a handy way to back into the relationship between temperature and altitude when you're thinking about performance or icing levels in ISA terms.
exam_tip: ISA height = ((15 - temp) / 2) × 1000 ft. Rearranged from the basic lapse rate formula. Practice a few worked examples.
key_facts: Reverse of the ISA temperature calculation
Formula: ISA height = ((15 - temp) / 2) × 1,000 ft
For +1°C, ISA height is 7,000 ft
For -5°C, ISA height is 10,000 ft
Valid up to the tropopause at 36,090 ft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define: QFE
concept: QFE is the pressure setting on your altimeter that reads zero when you're sitting on the aerodrome. It refers to the actual atmospheric pressure at aerodrome level. Used a lot in circuit work in some parts of the world (notably the UK) because your altimeter reads height above the field, not altitude. Not commonly used in NZ IFR operations — here we run QNH for the terminal area and standard pressure (1013) above the transition altitude.
exam_tip: QFE = altimeter zeros on the aerodrome surface. Reads height AGL, not altitude AMSL. Rare in NZ but you need to know the definition.
key_facts: QFE is the actual pressure at aerodrome elevation
Altimeter set to QFE reads zero on the runway
Gives height above aerodrome (AGL)
Not routinely used in NZ
Still asked about in theory exams
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define: QNH
concept: QNH is the altimeter setting that makes your altimeter read altitude above mean sea level when you're on the aerodrome. It's derived by taking the station pressure and adjusting it to sea level using ISA. This is the standard setting for NZ low-level operations — you fly QNH from the ground up to the transition altitude (13,000 ft in NZ). QNH changes as you fly across the country because it's tied to the local pressure — that's why ATIS gives you a fresh one and controllers pass it to you on approach.
exam_tip: QNH = altimeter reads altitude AMSL. Standard for all NZ operations below the transition altitude (13,000 ft). Gets updated as you cross regions because pressure changes.
key_facts: QNH is the MSL-equivalent pressure, derived from station pressure and ISA
Altimeter set to QNH reads altitude AMSL
Transition altitude in NZ is 13,000 ft
Below the transition altitude you fly QNH
QNH changes with position because MSL pressure varies geographically
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define: QNE
concept: QNE is the standard pressure setting of 1013.25 hPa (or 29.92 inHg). It's what you set on your altimeter in the cruise above the transition altitude — that's when your altimeter becomes a flight level indicator rather than an altitude indicator. Everybody at FL200 is using the same reference datum, so vertical separation works even though the underlying MSL pressure varies across the country. QNE gives you flight levels; QNH gives you altitudes.
exam_tip: QNE = 1013.25 hPa (standard). Above the transition altitude you fly on QNE and your height is reported as a flight level (e.g., FL180). Provides a common datum for all traffic.
key_facts: QNE = 1013.25 hPa / 29.92 inHg (ISA sea level pressure)
Used above the NZ transition altitude (13,000 ft)
Gives flight levels rather than altitudes
Ensures all traffic at high level uses the same reference datum
Vertical separation works even when regional QNH varies
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define: pressure altitude
concept: Pressure altitude is the altitude your altimeter reads when you set 1013.25 hPa (QNE). It's the altitude you'd be at in a standard atmosphere for the pressure you're actually experiencing. Pressure altitude is the input you need for most performance calculations — TAS, density altitude, fuel burn — because engines and wings respond to pressure, not the number written on a navigation chart. Easy to get from the aircraft altimeter by simply winding 1013 into the subscale and reading the result.
exam_tip: Pressure altitude = altimeter reading with 1013 set. The altitude that corresponds to the actual pressure in the ISA. Used for performance calculations.
key_facts: Pressure altitude = altitude reading when altimeter is set to 1013.25 hPa
Equals the indicated altitude in a standard atmosphere
Used as the input for density altitude and TAS calculations
Obtained by setting QNE on the subscale
Differs from true altitude when the atmosphere is non-standard
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define: flight levels (FL)
concept: Flight levels are simply pressure altitudes in hundreds of feet, flown with 1013 set on the altimeter. FL180 = 18,000 ft pressure altitude. In NZ, flight levels are used above the transition altitude of 13,000 ft. The point of flight levels is that everyone in the upper airspace is referenced to the same datum (standard pressure), so vertical separation works regardless of local pressure. You transition from altitude to flight level climbing through the transition altitude, and from flight level back to altitude descending through the transition level.
exam_tip: Flight level = pressure altitude in hundreds of feet, on 1013. FL180 = 18,000 ft PA. NZ transition altitude is 13,000 ft. Know the NZ-specific value.
key_facts: Flight levels are pressure altitudes in hundreds of feet on standard pressure (1013.25 hPa)
Written as FL followed by the hundreds-of-feet number (e.g., FL180)
Used above the NZ transition altitude of 13,000 ft
Provides a common reference for separation in the upper airspace
Climbing through the transition altitude, pilots set 1013
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe how localised pressure changes occur in association with: lee troughs
concept: Lee troughs are shallow low-pressure features that form in the lee of a mountain range when a strong flow crosses the range. As the air descends the lee slope it compresses and warms, which effectively 'stretches' the column vertically and reduces the surface pressure locally. You'll see them on NZ charts as little dashed lines running downstream of the Southern Alps in a strong westerly flow. They're not usually big weather producers on their own but they affect wind patterns and can enhance local convergence.
exam_tip: Lee trough = shallow low in the lee of mountains caused by descending, compressing, warming air. Common downstream of the Southern Alps in westerlies.
key_facts: Shallow surface low in the lee of a mountain range
Forms because descending air warms and column stretches, reducing surface pressure
Common in NZ downstream of the Southern Alps in strong westerlies
Often enhances low-level convergence and wind shear
Shown as a dashed trough line on synoptic charts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe how localised pressure changes occur in association with: thermal (or 'heat') lows
concept: Thermal or heat lows form when intense surface heating warms the air through a deep layer, making it less dense and so lowering the surface pressure. They're common in summer over large land masses (inland Australia is the classic example) or over dry heated areas. In NZ you'll sometimes see weak heat lows over the central South Island in summer. They're usually shallow — they disappear aloft within a few thousand feet — but at the surface they can generate gusty winds and trigger convection where there's any available moisture.
exam_tip: Thermal/heat low = shallow surface low from intense surface heating. Summer continental feature. Shallow — doesn't extend much above a few thousand feet.
key_facts: Caused by strong surface heating warming the air column
Warm air is less dense, reduces surface pressure
Common over large continental interiors in summer
Shallow — usually confined to low levels
Can trigger convection if moisture is available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe how localised pressure changes occur in association with: thunderstorms
concept: Thunderstorms create their own local pressure patterns. Underneath and just ahead of a mature Cb you'll find a 'meso-high' caused by the cold downdraught air spreading out at the surface. Just behind the storm, as the outflow decays, there's often a mesoscale low where the warm moist inflow is feeding the next updraught. The pressure changes aren't huge in magnitude but they happen fast and produce strong outflow winds, gust fronts, and sudden wind shifts — all the hazards that make thunderstorms dangerous to fly near.
exam_tip: Thunderstorms create a meso-high under the downdraught (cold outflow) and a meso-low near the updraught inflow. Small scale but rapid changes mean gust fronts and windshear.
key_facts: Downdraught outflow creates a local high-pressure 'bubble' under the storm
Updraught inflow zone has locally reduced pressure
Drives gust fronts and first-gust hazards
Very localised and short-lived but intense
Generates severe low-level wind shear
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe 'diurnal' pressure variations
concept: Diurnal pressure variation is the regular daily rise-and-fall of atmospheric pressure that happens on a 12-hour cycle. It's caused by solar heating of the atmosphere and by atmospheric tides. The pressure peaks roughly at 10am and 10pm local time and dips at around 4am and 4pm. The amplitude is small — typically about 1 hPa in the mid-latitudes — but in the tropics it can reach 2-3 hPa, large enough to be very obvious on a barograph.
exam_tip: Diurnal pressure variation = twice-daily rise/fall of pressure, peaks near 10am/10pm, dips near 4am/4pm. Caused by solar heating and atmospheric tides.
key_facts: Twice-daily (semi-diurnal) pressure cycle
Peaks around 10am and 10pm local time
Dips around 4am and 4pm local time
Driven by solar heating and atmospheric tides
Amplitude ~1 hPa in mid-latitudes, 2-3 hPa in the tropics
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State the latitudes where diurnal pressure variation is most significant
concept: Diurnal pressure variation is most significant in the tropics — between about 30°N and 30°S. That's where the amplitude of the semi-diurnal cycle is greatest (2-3 hPa), and it can actually mask or exaggerate the real synoptic changes. In the mid-latitudes like NZ, the signal is smaller (around 1 hPa) and usually swamped by everyday weather-driven pressure changes.
exam_tip: Diurnal pressure variation is biggest in the tropics (roughly 30°N to 30°S), smallest at high latitudes.
key_facts: Most significant between 30°N and 30°S (the tropics)
Tropical amplitude is around 2-3 hPa
Mid-latitude amplitude is around 1 hPa
Polar regions show minimal diurnal pressure variation
In the tropics it can dominate over synoptic changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of changes in the following elements on air density: pressure
concept: Pressure and density are directly proportional when temperature is held constant. If pressure increases, density increases. If pressure decreases, density decreases. That's why air density drops off as you climb — the pressure is dropping. At the surface you've got ~1,225 g/m³ at ISA, and by 18,000 ft pressure altitude you're down to about half that.
exam_tip: Pressure and density are directly proportional (hold temp constant). Lower pressure = lower density. Why performance degrades with altitude.
key_facts: Direct proportion: higher pressure = higher density (temperature constant)
Air density decreases with altitude because pressure drops
ISA density at sea level is 1.225 kg/m³
Halved by around 18,000 ft
Key input for performance calculations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of changes in the following elements on air density: temperature
concept: Temperature and density are inversely proportional. Hotter air expands and is less dense; colder air contracts and is denser. A hot summer's afternoon at Hamilton with an OAT of 30°C gives significantly lower air density than a chilly winter morning at the same field at 2°C. Hot and high is the classic performance trap — fewer molecules under the wing, less lift, longer takeoff roll, worse climb gradient.
exam_tip: Temperature and density are inversely proportional. Hot air = less dense = worse performance. 'Hot and high' kills take-off performance.
key_facts: Inverse proportion: higher temp = lower density
Cold dense air gives better lift, thrust, and climb performance
Hot days reduce engine output, lift, and propeller efficiency
Classic 'hot and high' performance trap in NZ summer ops
One of the three key inputs for density altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of changes in the following elements on air density: altitude
concept: Altitude affects density because it affects pressure and temperature together. As you climb, pressure drops (reducing density) and temperature drops (increasing density). But the pressure effect dominates by a long way, so the net result is that density decreases with altitude. That's the whole reason aeroplanes have a service ceiling — eventually there's not enough air left to produce lift or thrust.
exam_tip: As altitude increases, density decreases overall (pressure effect dominates over cooling effect). Sets the service ceiling of an aircraft.
key_facts: Altitude increases → pressure decreases → density decreases
Cooling with altitude partially offsets but pressure drop dominates
Net effect: density falls with height
Density at 18,000 ft is about half that at sea level
Service ceiling limited by insufficient density
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of changes in the following elements on air density: moisture content of the air
concept: Moisture content reduces air density. Water vapour is lighter than dry air (a water molecule weighs less than the N2 or O2 it displaces), so the more humid the air, the less dense it is. The effect is smaller than temperature or pressure but it's real — a hot, humid day at Tauranga will give worse performance than the same temperature on a dry day. Density altitude calculations usually account for it through a humidity correction if you need to be precise.
exam_tip: Moist air is LESS dense than dry air — counter-intuitive but true. Water vapour molecules are lighter than the nitrogen/oxygen they replace. Small but real performance penalty.
key_facts: Humid air is less dense than dry air at the same temperature
Water vapour is lighter than N2 or O2
Effect is smaller than temperature or pressure but measurable
Worsens performance on hot humid days
Accounted for in precise density altitude calculations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define 'density altitude' (DA)
concept: Density altitude is the pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature — it tells you the altitude in a standard atmosphere at which the current air density actually exists. It's the number that matters for aeroplane performance. A hot summer's day at Queenstown (3,100 ft elevation, OAT maybe +28°C) can give a density altitude of 6,500 ft or more — your aeroplane thinks it's taking off from a much higher aerodrome than the charts show.
exam_tip: Density altitude = pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature. The altitude the aircraft 'thinks' it's at. Queenstown in summer is a classic DA trap.
key_facts: DA is pressure altitude adjusted for temperature deviation from ISA
Represents the actual density the aircraft is flying in
High DA = worse performance (thinner air)
Key input for take-off and climb performance calculations
Always higher than elevation on a hot day
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Calculate 'density altitude'
concept: To calculate density altitude: find pressure altitude first (set 1013, read altimeter), then add 120 ft for every degree Celsius the OAT exceeds ISA. Or use the slide-rule side of the flight computer — pressure altitude on one scale, OAT on another, read DA off the window. Example: Queenstown pressure altitude 3,100 ft, OAT +28°C, ISA at 3,100 ft = 15 - 6.2 = 8.8°C, deviation = +19°C, DA = 3,100 + (19 × 120) = 5,380 ft.
exam_tip: DA ≈ PA + 120 ft per °C above ISA. Memorise the 120 ft/°C rule of thumb. Flight computer is more precise but the approximation is close enough for quick checks.
key_facts: Formula: DA ≈ PA + 120 × (OAT - ISA temp)
Each degree above ISA adds roughly 120 ft of DA
Flight computer provides a precise answer
Use PA (altimeter on 1013) as the starting point
Essential for calculating take-off distance and climb performance in hot conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'gradient wind' in the Southern Hemisphere with respect to: anticyclonically curved isobars
concept: Gradient wind is the wind that results when the pressure gradient force, Coriolis force, and centripetal acceleration all balance around curved isobars. In the Southern Hemisphere around anticyclonically curved isobars (flowing around a high), the flow is anticlockwise (counter-clockwise looking down from above). The curvature means the wind is actually STRONGER than the geostrophic wind for the same pressure gradient — because the centripetal term adds to the pressure gradient force. Small effect but real.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere gradient wind: anticlockwise around a high (anticyclonically curved), STRONGER than geostrophic for the same gradient. Don't mix up SH and NH.
key_facts: Gradient wind balances PGF, Coriolis, and centripetal terms
Southern Hemisphere anticyclone: flow is anticlockwise
Around anticyclonically curved isobars, gradient wind is stronger than geostrophic
Pressure gradient force is augmented by centripetal contribution
Still parallel to the isobars
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'gradient wind' in the Southern Hemisphere with respect to: cyclonically curved isobars
concept: Around cyclonically curved isobars (flowing around a low), the Southern Hemisphere gradient wind is clockwise. In this case the centripetal force acts AGAINST the pressure gradient force, so the gradient wind is WEAKER than the geostrophic wind for the same isobar spacing. Again, the effect is small but measurable. Remember the direction — SH lows are clockwise, NH lows are anticlockwise.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere gradient wind around a low: clockwise, WEAKER than geostrophic. Cyclonic curvature reduces the wind strength compared to straight isobars.
key_facts: Southern Hemisphere cyclone: flow is clockwise
Around cyclonically curved isobars, gradient wind is weaker than geostrophic
Centripetal force opposes the pressure gradient force
Still parallel to the isobars
Opposite direction to the Northern Hemisphere
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the 'frictional wind balance'
concept: The frictional wind balance is what happens near the ground where surface friction slows the wind down and tilts it across the isobars toward low pressure. In free air the wind is parallel to the isobars, but friction throws off the Coriolis/PGF balance, so the wind ends up flowing at an angle to the isobars — typically 10-30° toward the low. The magnitude is also reduced. Above about 2,000 ft AGL friction drops off and the wind lifts back to parallel the isobars (the classic 'wind shear' you feel climbing out).
exam_tip: Friction slows the wind AND turns it across isobars toward low pressure. Stops acting above about 2,000 ft AGL. That's why surface and gradient winds differ in both strength and direction.
key_facts: Friction unbalances the geostrophic balance near the ground
Wind slows and turns across the isobars toward low pressure
Typical deflection 10-30° depending on surface roughness
Friction layer typically extends to about 2,000 ft AGL
Above the friction layer, wind returns to being parallel to isobars
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: the sea
concept: Over the sea, the surface is relatively smooth, so friction is low. The surface wind typically backs about 10° from the free-air direction (in the Southern Hemisphere) and loses only a small amount of speed — surface wind is around 80-90% of the gradient wind strength.
exam_tip: Sea surface: ~10° deflection, wind speed ~80-90% of gradient wind. Smoothest surface = smallest friction effect.
key_facts: Sea has the lowest surface friction
Typical deflection about 10° across the isobars toward low pressure
Surface wind around 80-90% of gradient wind speed
Smoother than land so friction effect is smallest
Important for coastal and oceanic operations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: flat to undulating ground
concept: Over flat to undulating ground the friction effect is larger. Surface wind is typically deflected about 20° across the isobars toward low pressure and slowed to roughly 60-70% of the gradient wind speed.
exam_tip: Flat land: ~20° deflection, speed ~60-70% of gradient wind. Moderate friction.
key_facts: Moderate friction compared with sea or mountains
Deflection about 20° across the isobars toward low pressure
Surface wind around 60-70% of gradient wind speed
Typical for inland NZ plains
Friction layer deeper than over the sea
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State typical wind direction deflections due to friction over: mountainous regions
concept: Over mountainous regions, friction is very high and the surface wind behaviour is messy. Deflection can reach 30° or more, and wind speed can drop to 40-50% of the gradient wind — but that's only the mean picture. In reality, mountains create channeled flow, rotor zones, mountain waves, and highly localised gusts that bear almost no resemblance to the smooth picture the friction model gives you. NZ alpine regions are a classic example.
exam_tip: Mountainous: ~30° or more deflection, speed ~40-50% of gradient wind on average, but local effects (channeling, rotors, waves) dominate. The simple model breaks down.
key_facts: Highest friction of the three surface types
Average deflection 30° or more across the isobars
Average surface wind 40-50% of gradient wind
Local effects — channeling, rotors, waves — dominate over the average
NZ alpine terrain is a textbook example
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: atmospheric stability
concept: Atmospheric stability is the biggest single factor affecting the depth of the friction layer. In unstable conditions, vertical mixing is vigorous — surface turbulence is carried upward, spreading the frictional effect through a deeper layer. In stable conditions, vertical motion is suppressed, so friction is confined close to the surface. That's why a stable morning has a shallow friction layer and a hot unstable afternoon has a deep one.
exam_tip: Unstable air = deeper friction layer (more vertical mixing). Stable air = shallower friction layer.
key_facts: Unstable air encourages vertical mixing and deepens the friction layer
Stable air suppresses vertical motion and keeps friction close to the surface
Friction layer depth varies through the day with heating and stability changes
Morning friction layer often shallow; afternoon deeper
Affects how quickly surface wind becomes gradient wind with height
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: wind strength
concept: Stronger winds create more mechanical turbulence as they flow over surface obstacles, and that turbulence mixes momentum upward through a deeper layer. So a strong wind day has a deeper friction layer than a light wind day with the same surface. It's why on windy days you can still feel turbulence climbing out at 2,500 ft, and on light wind days you're in smooth air by 1,000 ft.
exam_tip: Stronger wind = deeper friction layer (more mechanical turbulence). Light wind = shallow friction layer.
key_facts: Stronger winds produce more mechanical turbulence at the surface
Turbulence mixes momentum upward, deepening the friction layer
Light winds have shallow friction layers
Strong winds can carry surface friction effects up to 3,000 ft or more
Explains variable turbulence altitudes on departure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain how the following affect the depth of the friction layer: surface roughness
concept: Surface roughness is the third factor. Rougher surfaces — forests, built-up areas, mountainous terrain — generate more turbulence and deepen the friction layer. Smoother surfaces — open water, flat snowfields — generate less and keep the friction layer shallow. That's partly why the wind over the Canterbury Plains feels different from the wind over the Cook Strait even when the synoptic wind is the same.
exam_tip: Rougher surface = deeper friction layer. Forests and mountains = deep; sea and snow = shallow.
key_facts: Rougher surface = more mechanical turbulence = deeper friction layer
Urban areas and forests generate significant roughness
Sea and snowfields are relatively smooth
Mountains produce extreme roughness effects
Combines with stability and wind speed to set overall depth
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: wavelengths
concept: Mountain wave wavelengths depend on the wind speed and atmospheric stability. Typical wavelengths range from about 5 to 30 km. Stronger winds and more stable layers tend to produce longer wavelengths. In the NZ Southern Alps you'll commonly see lee waves with wavelengths of 10-20 km stretching well downwind of the ranges when a strong westerly is flowing.
exam_tip: Typical mountain wave wavelength 5-30 km. Stronger wind and more stable atmosphere = longer wavelength.
key_facts: Typical wavelengths 5-30 km
Longer wavelengths with stronger winds and greater stability
Visible on satellite imagery as bands of wave cloud downstream of ranges
Common downwind of the Southern Alps in westerlies
Wavelength determines the spacing of wave crests and rotors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: position and rotation of any possible rotor zones
concept: Rotor zones sit underneath the crests of the lee waves, roughly at ridge-top height or slightly below. They're zones of intense, turbulent rolling motion with a horizontal axis parallel to the ridge. The rotor rotates in the opposite direction to the main airflow on its lower side, and it can contain severe to extreme turbulence. For small aircraft, rotor zones are genuinely dangerous — they've broken airframes and killed pilots in NZ over the Southern Alps.
exam_tip: Rotor zones sit under the wave crests at roughly ridge-top level. Extreme turbulence. Deadly to small aircraft. Rotation is OPPOSITE to the primary flow on the lower edge.
key_facts: Sit beneath the wave crests at about ridge-top height
Horizontal axis of rotation parallel to the ridge
Rotation opposite to main flow on the lower side
Contain severe to extreme turbulence
Dangerous to all aircraft, particularly light GA
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: position and type of any possible cloud development
concept: Cloud development in a mountain wave depends on moisture. If the air is moist enough, you'll see three signature cloud types: cap cloud (sitting on top of the ridge, looking like a stationary cap), lenticular cloud (smooth, lens-shaped altocumulus standing at the crest of each wave, Altocumulus Lenticularis), and rotor cloud (ragged cumulus-looking cloud in the rotor zone). All three are stationary relative to the ground even though the air is streaming through them at speed — a visual clue that wave activity is present. In dry air you may get no cloud at all, which is even more dangerous because the waves are invisible.
exam_tip: Three mountain wave clouds: cap cloud on the ridge, lenticular (ACSL) at the wave crests, rotor cloud in the rotor. All stationary. No cloud doesn't mean no waves.
key_facts: Cap cloud sits on top of the ridge
Lenticular cloud (ACSL) marks the wave crests aloft
Rotor cloud marks the rotor zone underneath
All wave clouds are stationary relative to the ground
Dry air gives invisible waves — more dangerous
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: the heights of the friction layer
concept: The friction layer over mountains is highly variable because of the irregular surface. In mountain wave situations the effective friction layer can extend much higher than normal — surface-induced turbulence can reach several thousand feet above the ridge. Add rotor zones on top of that and you've got turbulent air potentially extending up through the wave region itself.
exam_tip: Mountain wave friction layer is deep and variable. Turbulence extends well above the ridge, especially in the rotor zone.
key_facts: Friction layer over mountains is deeper than over flat terrain
Turbulent motion can extend thousands of feet above the ridge
Combines with rotor turbulence in wave situations
Makes it hard to find smooth air at low level in the lee of ranges
Critical for route and altitude planning in NZ alpine regions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: areas of probable severe turbulence
concept: Severe turbulence in a mountain wave setup is most likely in two places: the rotor zones under the wave crests, and in the lee of the main ridge within the wave region. Aircraft have been lost in both. The rotor is typically the worst — turbulence there can exceed aircraft structural limits. Occasionally, severe turbulence extends well upwards through the wave if the flow becomes especially unstable.
exam_tip: Rotor zones = severe to extreme turbulence. Also in the lee of the ridge near ridge-top level. Avoid the whole system if possible.
key_facts: Rotor zone contains the worst turbulence in a mountain wave system
Severe to extreme turbulence may exceed aircraft structural limits
Also present just in the lee of the ridge at ridge-top level
Known to have caused in-flight breakups
Avoidance is the only reliable option for light aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the general characteristics of a mountain wave set-up with reference to: areas of possible severe airframe icing
concept: Severe airframe icing is likely in the wave clouds themselves, particularly in the lenticular cloud at wave crests. Why? Because the air in a wave crest is rapidly being lifted and cooled, generating supercooled water droplets at just the right size for aggressive ice accretion. The cap cloud on the ridge can also be icy, especially in cold winter conditions. Combined with the severe turbulence already present, mountain waves are one of the most hazardous weather phenomena an IFR pilot faces.
exam_tip: Mountain wave clouds (cap, lenticular) can produce severe icing, especially at wave crests where rapid lifting generates supercooled droplets.
key_facts: Supercooled droplets form readily in wave-crest lifting
Lenticular clouds can produce severe icing
Cap cloud on the ridge also a significant icing risk
Icing combines with severe turbulence — a double hazard
A major reason to avoid wave clouds in a non-icing-certified aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: explain the factors that affect the wave amplitude
concept: The amplitude of mountain waves (how high and low the air oscillates vertically) depends on the strength of the flow, the shape of the ridge, and the stability of the atmosphere. Stronger winds produce bigger amplitudes. A more abrupt ridge (like a sharp lee-facing edge) also gives larger amplitudes. And an atmospheric stability profile with a stable layer just above ridge-top height tends to trap and amplify the wave energy.
exam_tip: Wave amplitude increases with wind strength, sharpness of the ridge, and a stable layer just above ridge height. All three amplify vertical motion.
key_facts: Amplitude is the vertical size of the wave oscillation
Stronger winds produce larger amplitudes
Sharp, abrupt ridges produce larger amplitudes
Stable layer above the ridge traps and amplifies wave energy
Typical NZ amplitudes can exceed 1,000 ft vertical motion
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: explain the factors that affect the wave-length
concept: Wavelength — the horizontal distance between successive wave crests — increases with wind speed and stability. A stronger, more stable flow stretches the waves out. Shorter wavelengths occur in weaker or less stable flows. Knowing this lets you estimate the likely downstream extent of wave activity: in a strong NW westerly over the Southern Alps you can see wave cloud stretching 100+ km downwind, with successive wavelengths of 15-25 km.
exam_tip: Wavelength increases with wind speed and stability. Stronger, more stable = longer waves.
key_facts: Wavelength is the horizontal distance between successive crests
Increases with wind speed
Increases with atmospheric stability
Typical NZ values 5-30 km
Determines how far downstream wave effects will extend
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: With reference to mountain waves: describe the flight conditions associated with mountain waves
concept: Flight conditions in mountain waves are distinctive and treacherous. Smooth, powerful up- and downdraughts on the order of hundreds to thousands of feet per minute aloft — you can find yourself unable to hold altitude even with full power. Extreme vertical currents at the wave crests. Severe turbulence in the rotor. Severe icing in the wave clouds. Altimeter errors from the local pressure changes. And the whole system is invisible if the air's dry. The classic advice: if you have to fly near waves, stay well above the terrain, fly perpendicular to the ridge if you can't go around, and don't try to maintain altitude against a strong downdraught — ride it down, get clear, then climb in smoother air.
exam_tip: Mountain wave flight conditions: smooth but strong vertical currents aloft, rotor turbulence below, icing in wave clouds, altimeter errors, sometimes invisible in dry air. Avoid if possible.
key_facts: Strong smooth up- and downdraughts aloft (hundreds to thousands of ft/min)
Severe to extreme turbulence in rotor zones
Severe airframe icing in wave clouds
Altimeter errors due to local pressure changes
Can be invisible in dry air
Give the whole system a wide berth if possible
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the rotor streaming process
concept: Rotor streaming is a specific mountain wave configuration where a low-level inversion keeps the wave energy confined to a thin layer just above the ridge, producing a single row (or streaming line) of rotors downstream. Instead of a nicely ordered wave train aloft, you get a layer of chaotic horizontal rotor circulations flowing downwind from the ridge. Extremely turbulent at low levels. Often present in NZ when a strong wind is flowing over the ranges with a capping inversion not far above ridge height.
exam_tip: Rotor streaming = low-level cap on the wave energy creates a line of rotors flowing downstream of the ridge. Very turbulent. Often no wave cloud aloft to give it away.
key_facts: Occurs when a capping inversion confines wave energy to low levels
Produces a streaming line of rotors downwind of the ridge
Very turbulent at low levels
Often no upper wave cloud to warn you
Common in NZ when strong winds meet a shallow stable layer over the ranges
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions associated with rotor streaming
concept: Flying in rotor streaming is as rough as it gets at low level downwind of a mountain range — extreme turbulence, sudden changes in wind direction and strength, and significant vertical currents. Light aircraft should not be at low level in rotor streaming conditions. You'll often see ragged, fast-moving cloud fragments marking the rotor circulations. The safest option is to climb well above the affected layer or route completely around the affected area.
exam_tip: Rotor streaming = extreme low-level turbulence, sudden wind changes, vertical currents. Light aircraft should avoid at low level.
key_facts: Extreme low-level turbulence downwind of the ridge
Sudden changes in wind speed and direction
Vertical currents in the rotors
Light aircraft should avoid the affected airspace
Climb well above the capping inversion or route around
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define the Föhn wind
concept: A Föhn wind is a warm, dry wind that blows down the lee slope of a mountain range when moist air is forced over the range. As the moist air rises on the windward side it cools at the slower saturated lapse rate (about 1.5°C per 1,000 ft), condenses, and drops most of its moisture as precipitation. On the lee side, the now-dry air descends and warms at the faster dry adiabatic rate (3°C per 1,000 ft). Result: the lee-side air arrives significantly warmer and drier than it started. In NZ the Canterbury 'nor'wester' is the textbook Föhn wind — warm, dry, gusty, and famous for making everyone grumpy.
exam_tip: Föhn wind = warm dry lee-side wind. Caused by moisture drop-out on windward side and dry descent on lee side. Canterbury nor'wester is the classic NZ example.
key_facts: Warm, dry wind blowing down the lee slope of a mountain range
Forms when moist air is forced over a mountain range
Windward side loses moisture as precipitation (cools at SALR)
Lee side warms at DALR — drier and warmer than upwind
Canterbury nor'wester is the best-known NZ Föhn wind
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State the requirements for the development of a Föhn wind
concept: For a Föhn wind to develop you need: (1) moist air on the windward side, (2) a strong wind component perpendicular to the range driving that air upslope, (3) sufficient ridge height to force significant lifting and precipitation, and (4) air descending on the lee side that warms at the dry adiabatic rate. The NZ Southern Alps meet all four conditions regularly, which is why Canterbury gets hammered by nor'westers so often.
exam_tip: Föhn requirements: moist windward air, wind blowing perpendicular to the range, enough ridge height to force precipitation, dry descent on the lee side. NZ Southern Alps + westerly flow = classic setup.
key_facts: Moist air on the windward side
Wind component perpendicular to the range
Sufficient ridge height to force lifting and precipitation
Air descending on the lee side at DALR
NZ Southern Alps with westerly flow is the textbook case
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: to windward of the mountain range
concept: To windward of the range in a Föhn setup, conditions are wet and miserable — orographic cloud, rain or drizzle, low ceilings, poor visibility, and often moderate to severe turbulence from the forced lifting. West Coast South Island in a nor'wester is the classic example: heavy rain, Cb embedded in the cloud mass, and wind right at the limits.
exam_tip: Föhn windward side: low cloud, rain, poor visibility, turbulence. West Coast in a nor'wester.
key_facts: Orographic cloud and rain on the windward slope
Low ceilings and poor visibility
Moderate to severe turbulence from lifting
Possible embedded Cb
West Coast South Island in a nor'wester = classic picture
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: over the mountain range
concept: Over the range in a Föhn setup you've got the summit cloud itself (the cap cloud), often with freezing levels and icing, along with severe mechanical turbulence and possible mountain wave activity. Wind is strong. This is usually the worst place to be flying. IFR aircraft crossing the Alps in strong westerlies plan carefully to minimise time over the main ridges.
exam_tip: Föhn over the range: cap cloud, icing, severe turbulence, strong wind, possible mountain waves. The hardest part of the crossing.
key_facts: Cap cloud sits on the ridge
Icing likely in the cap cloud
Severe mechanical turbulence
Possible mountain wave activity initiating
Strong wind at ridge height
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the flight conditions when flying in Föhn conditions in the following positions: on the lee side of the mountain range
concept: On the lee side — Canterbury in a nor'wester — the picture is completely different. Clear skies or scattered cloud, very good visibility, warm and dry air, but strong gusty winds and significant mechanical turbulence. Possible mountain waves and rotor cloud downstream of the ranges. Temperatures can rise dramatically in a matter of hours. Crosswinds at Canterbury aerodromes can be extreme during a strong nor'wester — Christchurch has seen gusts well above 60 kt in big events.
exam_tip: Föhn lee side: clear, warm, dry, very good visibility, but strong gusty winds, mechanical turbulence, possible mountain waves. Canterbury nor'wester — looks beautiful, lands like a rodeo.
key_facts: Clear skies or scattered cloud on the lee side
Excellent visibility
Warm and dry (the 'Föhn' effect)
Strong, gusty winds at the surface
Mechanical turbulence and possible mountain waves downstream
Severe crosswinds possible at Canterbury aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the relationship between stability of air and cloud type
concept: The stability of the air controls what type of cloud forms. Unstable air produces cloud with strong vertical development — cumulus, towering cumulus, cumulonimbus — because rising parcels keep accelerating upwards. Stable air produces layered cloud — stratus, stratocumulus, altostratus, nimbostratus — because the rising parcels are suppressed and spread horizontally instead. Neutral stability gives you mixed or transitional cloud. When you look at a sky full of puffy Cu, the atmosphere is telling you it's unstable; a grey layered overcast is telling you it's stable.
exam_tip: Unstable = vertical development (Cu, TCu, Cb). Stable = layered cloud (St, Sc, As, Ns). Read the sky to read the stability.
key_facts: Stability determines whether clouds are layered or towering
Unstable air → cumuliform clouds (vertical development)
Stable air → stratiform clouds (layered)
Cb is the ultimate unstable cloud; Ns the ultimate stable precipitation cloud
Neutral stability gives transitional cloud types
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the 10 main cloud types as defined by the WMO
concept: The World Meteorological Organisation (WMO) defines 10 main cloud genera, grouped by altitude: HIGH (bases above ~16,500 ft): cirrus (Ci), cirrocumulus (Cc), cirrostratus (Cs). MIDDLE (6,500-16,500 ft): altocumulus (Ac), altostratus (As), nimbostratus (Ns, often spans heights). LOW (below ~6,500 ft): stratocumulus (Sc), stratus (St). VERTICAL DEVELOPMENT: cumulus (Cu), cumulonimbus (Cb). Every cloud you see in the sky belongs to one of these ten families.
exam_tip: Ten WMO cloud genera across three levels plus two vertical-development types. Memorise the list and the level groupings.
key_facts: High: Ci, Cc, Cs (above 16,500 ft)
Middle: Ac, As, Ns (6,500-16,500 ft; Ns often spans levels)
Low: Sc, St (below 6,500 ft)
Vertical development: Cu, Cb
Ten genera total; each has sub-species
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: turbulence
concept: Turbulence by cloud type: Cb is the worst — severe to extreme turbulence throughout. TCu and Cu are moderate to severe inside. Sc and Ac mostly light to moderate. Stratus and St at low level are smooth apart from surface friction. Cirrus, Cs, Cc up high are usually smooth unless there's jet stream turbulence nearby. Ns can surprise you — mostly smooth stratiform but with embedded convective cells in some cases.
exam_tip: Cb = severe/extreme. Cu/TCu = moderate/severe. Ac/Sc = light/moderate. Stratus = smooth. Cirrus = smooth (unless jet stream). Ns = mostly smooth but watch for embedded Cb.
key_facts: Cb: severe to extreme turbulence
TCu/Cu: moderate to severe inside
Ac/Sc: light to moderate
St and Cs/Ci: generally smooth
Ns: generally smooth but embedded Cb possible
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: icing
concept: Icing by cloud type: the worst is in Cb (clear ice, moderate to severe) and in stratiform clouds near the freezing level (As, Ns — rime or mixed ice, often persistent). Cu and TCu produce icing in their upper portions where supercooled droplets exist. Sc and Ac also give rime ice at temperatures below 0°C. Ci, Cs, Cc — all ice crystal clouds — produce minimal icing because the water is already frozen. Stratus at low level is rarely cold enough in NZ to be icy but can be.
exam_tip: Worst icing: Cb (clear/severe), and As/Ns (persistent rime/mixed). Ice-crystal high clouds (Ci/Cs/Cc) give minimal icing. Match cloud type to icing type.
key_facts: Cb: clear ice, moderate to severe
As/Ns: persistent rime or mixed ice near freezing level
Cu/TCu: icing in upper portions above the freezing level
Sc/Ac: rime ice below 0°C
Ci/Cs/Cc: ice crystal clouds, minimal icing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe typical conditions for each of the 10 main cloud types with respect to: precipitation
concept: Precipitation by cloud type: heaviest and most intense from Cb — thunderstorm rain, hail, snow in strong convection. Cu and TCu give showery rain. Ns is the classic steady-rain cloud of warm fronts — long-duration moderate rain or snow. As can give light steady rain. Sc gives light showers or drizzle. St can give drizzle. Ac, Cc, Cs, Ci rarely produce precipitation that reaches the ground (virga is possible).
exam_tip: Cb = thunderstorms/hail. Ns = steady rain. Cu/TCu = showers. St/Sc = drizzle. High clouds = usually no precipitation. Match cloud type to precipitation type.
key_facts: Cb: thunderstorm rain, hail, strong showers
Ns: continuous moderate rain/snow
Cu/TCu: showers
Sc: light showers or drizzle
St: drizzle
Ac/Ci/Cs/Cc: usually no surface precipitation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Asperitas
concept: Asperitas (formerly Undulatus asperatus) is a recently named sub-type — dramatic, wave-like undulations on the underside of a cloud layer, often looking like a turbulent sea seen from below. It's usually associated with altostratus or stratocumulus after a stormy period. Indicates turbulent but not necessarily convective conditions aloft.
exam_tip: Asperitas = dramatic wave patterns on the underside of a cloud layer. Indicates turbulent conditions after a storm. Recently named WMO sub-type.
key_facts: Wave-like underside patterns on a stratiform cloud layer
Associated with Ac and Sc after disturbed weather
Indicates turbulent flow underneath the cloud
Recently added to the WMO cloud atlas (2017)
Not necessarily a severe weather indicator
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Mammatus
concept: Mammatus are the pouch- or bubble-shaped protrusions on the underside of a cloud — usually the anvil of a cumulonimbus. They form when cold, heavy air sinks into warmer air below the cloud base. Mammatus always indicate a mature or decaying thunderstorm nearby, and the storm is usually significant. Seeing them means there's big convection in the area — take them seriously as a hazard marker.
exam_tip: Mammatus = pouches on the underside of a Cb anvil. Always means serious thunderstorm activity in the area. A warning sign.
key_facts: Pouch/bubble-shaped protrusions on the underside of a cloud
Usually on the anvil of a mature or decaying Cb
Caused by cold air sinking into warmer air below
Always indicate significant convection nearby
A recognised thunderstorm hazard marker
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Altocumulus Lenticularis
concept: Altocumulus Lenticularis (ACSL) are the lens-shaped or almond-shaped clouds that mark mountain wave activity. They form at the crests of standing waves downwind of a ridge and remain stationary even in strong wind because the air is streaming through them while the wave shape itself is fixed. Seeing lenticular cloud is the clearest visual clue that mountain waves are active — and that turbulence and icing are very likely in the area.
exam_tip: Altocumulus Lenticularis (ACSL) = stationary lens-shaped cloud at mountain wave crests. The visual signature of wave activity. Severe turbulence and icing nearby.
key_facts: Lens- or almond-shaped clouds
Form at the crests of standing mountain waves
Stationary relative to the ground despite strong flow through them
Visual signature of mountain wave activity
Severe turbulence and icing likely
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Rotor Cloud
concept: Rotor cloud is the ragged, cumulus-like cloud that forms in the turbulent rotor zone under a lee wave, usually at or just below ridge-top height. Unlike the smooth lenticular clouds above, rotor clouds look torn and chaotic — a visual warning that extreme turbulence is happening inside them. Never fly through or near a rotor cloud in a light aircraft.
exam_tip: Rotor cloud = ragged cumulus-looking cloud in the rotor zone under a lee wave. Extreme turbulence. Never fly into one.
key_facts: Ragged, chaotic-looking cloud beneath a lee wave
Forms in the rotor zone at or below ridge-top height
Visually distinct from the smooth lenticular cloud above
Marks extreme turbulence
Unsafe for flight at any size of aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Kelvin Helmholtz waves
concept: Kelvin-Helmholtz waves (KH waves) look like breaking ocean waves frozen in the sky — a row of curling wave shapes on top of a cloud layer. They form at the boundary between two layers of air moving at different speeds (a wind shear layer). KH waves are the visual signature of significant vertical wind shear and indicate likely turbulence at that level. They're usually short-lived — you catch them as they form and break.
exam_tip: Kelvin-Helmholtz waves look like breaking ocean waves in the sky. Mark a shear layer. Indicate turbulence there. Short-lived.
key_facts: Row of curling wave shapes resembling breaking ocean waves
Form at the boundary of two wind layers with different speeds
Visual signature of vertical wind shear
Indicate turbulence at that level
Short-lived — form and decay quickly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Altocumulus Castellanus
concept: Altocumulus Castellanus (ACC) are altocumulus with little turrets or castle-like tops, showing that the middle atmosphere is unstable at that level. Morning ACC is a classic warning sign — it tells you that the middle levels are already unstable and that afternoon thunderstorms are likely once surface heating gets going. A strong hint to bring forward or re-think any VFR plans.
exam_tip: Altocumulus Castellanus (ACC) = altocumulus with turrets. Sign of mid-level instability. Morning ACC = afternoon thunderstorms likely.
key_facts: Altocumulus with distinct turret or castle-like tops
Indicates mid-level atmospheric instability
Morning ACC often precedes afternoon thunderstorms
Classic warning sign for thunderstorm-prone days
Common pre-storm indicator in summer NZ weather
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Identify the following cloud sub-sets and outline the atmospheric conditions indicated by each: Banner cloud
concept: Banner cloud is a cloud that forms and remains attached to the lee side of a sharp mountain peak or ridge, trailing downwind like a banner. It forms when the flow accelerates over the peak, causing a local pressure drop and condensation on the lee side. You'll see it on some of the sharper peaks in the Southern Alps and on Mount Taranaki in strong winds. Indicates strong flow over the peak and often turbulence downstream.
exam_tip: Banner cloud = streamer of cloud flowing downwind from the lee of a sharp peak. Marks strong flow and turbulence near the summit.
key_facts: Streamer of cloud attached to the lee side of a sharp peak
Caused by local pressure drop from accelerated flow
Visible on sharper NZ peaks in strong winds
Indicates strong flow over the summit
Turbulence likely downstream
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define runway visual range (RVR)
concept: Runway Visual Range (RVR) is the horizontal distance a pilot in a landing aircraft can see down the runway — specifically, the distance at which they can identify the runway edge lights or centreline markings. It's measured at actual aerodromes by transmissometers or forward-scatter meters near the touchdown zone. RVR is usually quoted in metres and is the key visibility reference for instrument approaches, because it's what you actually need to see in order to land.
exam_tip: RVR = horizontal distance you can see down the runway (to runway lights or markings). Measured at the aerodrome, quoted in metres. Key visibility reference for IFR approach minima.
key_facts: Horizontal distance a pilot can see down the runway
Identifies runway lights and markings
Measured by transmissometer or forward-scatter equipment
Quoted in metres (NZ convention)
Used to determine whether an instrument approach can be completed to land
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: State the difference between fog, mist and haze
concept: Fog is cloud at the surface, reducing visibility to less than 1 km (or 1,000 m for aviation purposes). Mist is the same phenomenon but with visibility between 1 km and 5 km — thinner, still water droplets in the air. Haze is dry — tiny particles (dust, pollution, smoke) rather than water — and it also reduces visibility, though it can co-exist with very low humidity. So fog and mist are wet, haze is dry. The distinction matters because they have different causes and different behaviours.
exam_tip: Fog = water, vis <1 km. Mist = water, 1-5 km. Haze = dry (dust/smoke/pollution). Fog and mist are water, haze is particles.
key_facts: Fog: water droplets at the surface, visibility <1 km
Mist: same phenomenon with visibility 1-5 km
Haze: dry particulates (dust, smoke, pollution)
Fog and mist are moisture-related; haze is not
Different formation processes and different implications for flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: their formation processes
concept: Anticyclones (highs) form where there's large-scale subsidence in the atmosphere — air is sinking from upper levels, converging at altitude and diverging at the surface. The subsiding air warms adiabatically, which is why highs are associated with clear skies and light winds. They form in two main ways: thermodynamically (over warm areas), and dynamically (as part of the mid-latitude wave pattern on the upper westerlies). NZ gets both types — the Australian high drifting across the Tasman is the most common visitor.
exam_tip: Highs form by subsidence (sinking air). Two types: thermodynamic (warm areas) and dynamic (upper wave pattern). NZ sees both.
key_facts: Form where air is subsiding on a large scale
Converging at altitude, diverging at the surface
Subsiding air warms adiabatically, drying out
Two main formation types: thermodynamic and dynamic
Australian high moving across Tasman is the most common NZ visitor
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: pressure patterns and wind flow
concept: The pressure pattern in a high is a closed set of roughly circular isobars, with the highest pressure at the centre and progressively lower pressure outwards.
