In the Southern Hemisphere the surface wind flows anticlockwise around a high (because of the direction of Coriolis). The flow is also outward from the centre due to the divergence at the surface — the air is actively spreading out. Deep in the middle of a large high the isobar spacing can become very wide, giving light or calm winds.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere high: isobars closed around the centre, flow anticlockwise and outwards from the centre. Light winds in the middle. Opposite direction to Northern Hemisphere.
key_facts: Closed isobars with highest pressure at the centre
Southern Hemisphere flow: anticlockwise around the high
Divergent flow at the surface, spreading outward
Very light winds near the centre
Opposite rotation direction to a Northern Hemisphere high
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: subsidence and subsidence inversions
concept: Subsidence in a high compresses and warms the sinking air, lowering its relative humidity and dissipating any cloud. It also produces a subsidence inversion — a layer where the sinking warmed air overlies cooler surface air. Below the inversion you often get trapped moisture, haze, smoke, and stratus. Above the inversion the air is clear and dry. That's why a high parked over NZ can give you beautiful clear upper air while the surface is fogged in.
exam_tip: Subsidence in a high → warming → drying → cloud dissipates → subsidence inversion. Below the inversion, pollutants and fog can be trapped. Above, clear and dry.
key_facts: Large-scale subsidence is the defining feature of a high
Sinking air warms adiabatically and dries out
Creates a subsidence inversion at the top of the surface layer
Pollutants, haze and fog can be trapped beneath the inversion
Above the inversion the air is clear and dry
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe anticyclones ('highs') with reference to: typical associated weather conditions
concept: Typical weather in an anticyclone: fine, clear or scattered cloud, light winds (especially near the centre), good visibility above any trapped haze, and settled conditions. In winter highs, you often get clear nights with strong radiation cooling, leading to morning fog and frost. In summer, hot days and calm evenings. The centre of a high is usually the easiest place to fly VFR and IFR — but don't be fooled by the surface haze or fog which can hide the calm upper-level picture.
exam_tip: High weather: fine, light winds, good visibility aloft, settled. Winter = morning fog/frost. Summer = hot calm. Beware surface fog/haze hiding the nice upper picture.
key_facts: Fine settled weather with light winds
Clear or scattered cloud aloft
Good visibility above any trapped haze
Winter: morning fog and frost possible
Summer: hot days, calm evenings
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the development of 'cold' highs
concept: Cold highs form over cold continental interiors in winter, where intense surface cooling produces dense cold air that piles up at the surface. Siberia and central Antarctica are the classic examples. They're shallow features — the high pressure only exists near the surface and disappears quickly aloft because the cold air is dense but thin. NZ doesn't get true cold highs, but cold air masses drifting up from Antarctica or southern Australia can bring similar short-lived features.
exam_tip: Cold high = shallow high over cold land in winter. Dense cold air piling up at the surface. Siberia is the textbook example. Shallow — disappears aloft.
key_facts: Form over cold land surfaces in winter
Driven by intense surface cooling
Dense cold air piles up at the surface
Shallow — confined to the lower atmosphere
Siberian high is the textbook example
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Discuss the hazards associated with anticyclones
concept: Highs get the reputation of being benign but they come with their own hazards. Radiation fog in winter — Hamilton and Whanganui are notorious for this. Frost and carb icing on still mornings. Trapped pollution and haze reducing visibility. Slow-moving systems that can keep aerodromes below minima for days. Extremely light winds making crosswinds at irrelevant runways a non-issue but also meaning you can't pick a particularly favourable runway. Icing can still occur in any residual stratocumulus layer. And a strong high stalling over NZ can lead to persistent westerlies along its northern edge, pumping weather across the country for days on end.
exam_tip: Highs aren't risk-free: fog, frost, haze, carb icing, trapped below-minima conditions, and persistent flows along their edges. Don't let a 'nice' synoptic chart fool you.
key_facts: Radiation fog in winter — common over NZ river valleys and plains
Frost and carburettor icing on still mornings
Trapped haze and pollution reducing visibility
Stagnation can keep below-minima conditions persistent
Strong flows along the northern edge of a stalled high
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: mid to high-latitude depressions ('lows')
concept: Mid to high-latitude depressions ('lows') are the main weather producers in NZ. They form as wave disturbances on the polar front between cold polar air and warmer subtropical air, and deepen as they move east in the westerly belt. A mature mid-latitude low has a warm sector between the warm and cold fronts, bringing widespread rain, strong winds, and vigorous weather. They're deeper, more energetic, and produce more rain than tropical depressions. Most of NZ's bad IFR weather comes from mid-latitude lows.
exam_tip: Mid-latitude depression = frontal low with warm and cold fronts, formed on the polar front, moves east in the westerlies. NZ's main weather producer.
key_facts: Form as wave disturbances on the polar front
Contain warm and cold fronts with a warm sector between
Deepen as they move east in the westerly belt
Produce widespread rain, cloud, wind, and poor visibility
Most of NZ's bad weather comes from mid-latitude lows
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: sub-tropical depressions
concept: Sub-tropical depressions form in the sub-tropics — roughly between 20°S and 35°S. They don't have the classic frontal structure of mid-latitude lows. Instead they're driven by convective processes, upper-level troughs, and warm sea temperatures. They can bring heavy rain and strong winds but lack the organised frontal weather of a mid-latitude low. NZ gets hit by them periodically, often when a tropical cyclone decays as it moves south into the sub-tropics.
exam_tip: Sub-tropical depression = low in the sub-tropics, no clear fronts, driven by convection and upper-level dynamics. Often the remnants of a former tropical cyclone.
key_facts: Form in the sub-tropics (20-35°S)
No classic frontal structure
Driven by convection, upper troughs, warm sea temperatures
Bring heavy rain and strong winds
Often decayed tropical cyclones
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of: tropical cyclones
concept: Tropical cyclones form over very warm tropical oceans (sea surface temperatures above about 27°C), away from the equator itself so there's enough Coriolis to get rotation going. They're intense, compact, warm-core systems with a calm eye surrounded by a wall of deep convection — hurricane-force winds, torrential rain, and devastating weather. NZ gets hit by decaying tropical cyclones that have moved south into the sub-tropics and extra-tropical waters, losing some of their intensity but gaining width and sometimes picking up frontal characteristics. Cyclone Gabrielle in 2023 was a notorious recent example.
exam_tip: Tropical cyclone = intense warm-core low over warm tropical oceans (SST > 27°C). Eye + eyewall structure. NZ sees decaying ones transitioning to extra-tropical systems.
key_facts: Form over tropical oceans with SST > 27°C
Need some distance from the equator for Coriolis
Intense warm-core systems with an eye and eyewall
Hurricane-force winds and torrential rain
NZ sees them decaying into extra-tropical systems (e.g., Cyclone Gabrielle 2023)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ

syllabus_desc: Define 'super-cooled water droplets'
concept: Supercooled water droplets are liquid water droplets that exist at temperatures below 0°C. Water doesn't have to freeze the moment it reaches 0°C — pure liquid droplets can remain liquid down to about -40°C before they freeze spontaneously. In clouds, droplets are often supercooled between 0°C and about -20°C. When a supercooled droplet hits something solid (like your airframe), it freezes instantly on impact. That instant freezing is the whole mechanism behind airframe icing.
exam_tip: Supercooled water droplets = liquid water below 0°C. Freeze instantly on impact. The cause of airframe icing. Most common between 0°C and -20°C.
key_facts: Liquid water existing below 0°C
Can remain liquid down to about -40°C in pure form
Most common in clouds between 0°C and -20°C
Freeze on impact with a solid surface
The fundamental cause of airframe icing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: clear (glaze) ice
concept: Clear (glaze) ice forms when large supercooled water droplets hit the airframe and spread out before freezing — the droplet flattens, runs back along the surface, and then freezes as a glassy, transparent layer. Because the ice doesn't freeze instantly, it can form on areas well behind the leading edge and follow the contours of the wing. Clear ice is the nastiest type: it's heavy, hard to detect, hard to remove, and aerodynamically disastrous because it deforms the wing profile. Most common in Cb and at temperatures from 0°C to about -10°C.
exam_tip: Clear (glaze) ice: large supercooled droplets, spread and freeze, transparent and heavy, hard to detect and remove. 0°C to -10°C, typical of Cb. The worst icing type.
key_facts: Forms from large supercooled water droplets
Droplets spread and run before freezing, giving a transparent glassy layer
Heavy, hard to detect, hard to remove
Deforms wing profile aerodynamically
Most common in Cb, 0°C to -10°C
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: rime (opaque) ice
concept: Rime (opaque) ice forms from small supercooled droplets that freeze almost instantly on contact, trapping air pockets between them. The result is an opaque, white, rough-textured layer that builds up on the leading edges only (because the droplets freeze before they can spread). It's lighter than clear ice, easier to remove, and less aerodynamically damaging because it mostly stays on the leading edge. Most common in stratiform clouds (As, Ns, Sc) and colder temperatures, typically -10°C to -20°C.
exam_tip: Rime ice: small droplets, freeze instantly, opaque rough white layer on leading edges. -10°C to -20°C, stratiform clouds. Lighter and less damaging than clear ice.
key_facts: Forms from small supercooled droplets that freeze instantly
Opaque, white, rough-textured
Builds on leading edges only
Lighter than clear ice and easier to remove
Typical of stratiform clouds, -10°C to -20°C
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: mixed ice
concept: Mixed ice is exactly what it sounds like — a combination of clear and rime forming when droplets of different sizes are present, or when conditions are transitioning. You get the weight and coverage of clear ice plus the rough texture of rime. It shares the worst features of both types and is common in mid-level clouds with mixed droplet sizes, typically between -5°C and -15°C.
exam_tip: Mixed ice: combination of clear and rime, mixed droplet sizes, -5°C to -15°C. Worst features of both types.
key_facts: Combination of clear and rime ice characteristics
Forms with mixed droplet sizes
Typical range -5°C to -15°C
Common in mid-level clouds
Heavy and rough — worst of both types
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: hoar frost
concept: Hoar frost is different from the other ice types — it forms by direct deposition of water vapour onto a cold surface (not by impact of liquid droplets). You'll see it on an aeroplane that's been sitting outside on a cold clear night — a layer of feathery white crystals on the wings and fuselage. Hoar frost needs to be cleaned off before flight because even a thin layer disrupts airflow over the wing and can dramatically reduce lift. It doesn't form in flight — it's a ground and pre-flight problem.
exam_tip: Hoar frost: forms by direct deposition of water vapour onto a cold surface (not from impact). Ground phenomenon. MUST be removed before flight — even thin frost destroys lift.
key_facts: Forms by direct deposition of water vapour on a cold surface
Not caused by impact of liquid droplets
Forms on parked aircraft on cold clear nights
Must be removed before flight — severely degrades lift
Not an in-flight icing hazard
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation process of: freezing rain
concept: Freezing rain is rain that falls from a warmer layer aloft through a colder layer below and hits your airframe as supercooled drops. The drops are large, the liquid water content is high, and they freeze on contact into thick clear ice extremely fast. It's the most aggressive icing hazard known — accumulation rates can be catastrophic in minutes. It also indicates a warm layer aloft, meaning an inversion, and a very hazardous situation overall. Avoid it — if you encounter freezing rain, get out of it fast, either down to the warm layer or back above it.
exam_tip: Freezing rain: rain from a warmer layer falling through a colder layer below. Large droplets, rapid clear ice accumulation, extremely dangerous. Indicates a warm layer aloft. Get out of it fast.
key_facts: Rain falling from a warmer layer through a colder layer below
Large supercooled droplets with high liquid water content
Most aggressive in-flight icing hazard
Indicates an inversion with warm air aloft
Escape down to the warm layer or climb above it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe: associated cloud types
concept: Associated cloud types: clear ice is typical of cumuliform cloud — Cb, TCu, Cu — because those clouds have the strongest updraughts and can support large supercooled droplets. Rime ice is typical of stratiform cloud — As, Ns, Sc — where updraughts are weak and only small droplets are supported. Mixed ice is typical of transitional or mid-level cloud where both droplet sizes coexist.
exam_tip: Clear ice → cumuliform (Cb, TCu, Cu). Rime ice → stratiform (As, Ns, Sc). Mixed ice → transitional mid-level cloud.
key_facts: Clear ice: cumuliform clouds (Cb, TCu, Cu)
Rime ice: stratiform clouds (As, Ns, Sc)
Mixed ice: transitional cloud types
Updraught strength determines droplet size
Cloud type is a strong indicator of likely ice type
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe: temperature ranges
concept: Temperature ranges: clear ice is most common from 0°C to about -10°C. Rime ice is most common from -10°C to about -20°C. Mixed ice falls in between, roughly -5°C to -15°C. Below about -20°C, airframe icing tends to reduce because most water is already frozen as ice crystals, and below about -40°C supercooled liquid essentially can't exist. So for practical in-flight icing, the main concern band is 0°C to -20°C.
exam_tip: Clear: 0°C to -10°C. Rime: -10°C to -20°C. Mixed: -5°C to -15°C. Below -20°C icing reduces; below -40°C it stops altogether.
key_facts: Clear ice most common 0°C to -10°C
Rime ice most common -10°C to -20°C
Mixed ice -5°C to -15°C
Icing risk reduces below -20°C as droplets freeze
Essentially no supercooled liquid below -40°C
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe: droplet size
concept: Droplet size: clear ice forms from large droplets (typical of strong convective updraughts in Cb). Rime ice forms from small droplets (typical of weak updraughts in stratus). Mixed ice involves a mix of droplet sizes. Large droplets have more water content per impact and freeze relatively slowly, allowing spread. Small droplets have less water and freeze instantly.
exam_tip: Large droplets → clear ice. Small droplets → rime ice. Mixed → both sizes present.
key_facts: Clear ice: large supercooled droplets
Rime ice: small supercooled droplets
Mixed ice: mix of droplet sizes
Large droplets have higher liquid water content
Small droplets freeze instantly; large ones spread before freezing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe: height range relative to the freezing level
concept: Height range relative to the freezing level: clear ice is usually found from the freezing level up to about 8,000-10,000 ft above the freezing level (in vigorous Cb it can extend even higher). Rime ice is usually from the freezing level up to about 15,000 ft above. Mixed ice falls in between. Above roughly 20,000 ft most cloud is ice-crystal only and icing stops.
exam_tip: Clear ice: freezing level to ~10,000 ft above. Rime ice: freezing level to ~15,000 ft above. Above ~20,000 ft, icing mostly stops (ice crystals).
key_facts: Clear ice: freezing level to about 10,000 ft above
Rime ice: freezing level to about 15,000 ft above
Mixed ice in between
Above 20,000 ft cloud becomes ice crystals and icing is minimal
Freezing level is the base of the icing-risk region
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to clear, rime and mixed ice, describe: enhancing factors
concept: Enhancing factors for airframe ice: high liquid water content (LWC) in the cloud, large droplet size, proximity to the freezing level, low OAT within the icing range, strong updraughts (which sustain supercooled liquid higher), and slow airspeed (which increases exposure time). Reducing any of these reduces ice accretion rate — which is why flight manuals sometimes recommend higher cruise speed in icing conditions.
exam_tip: Ice worsens with: high LWC, large droplets, near freezing level, strong updraughts, slow airspeed. Reduce any one and accretion slows.
key_facts: High liquid water content (LWC) in the cloud
Large droplet size
Temperature near the freezing level
Strong updraughts sustaining supercooled water
Slow airspeed (more exposure per distance flown)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the factors that influence the rate of ice accretion
concept: The rate of ice accretion depends on liquid water content (more water = more ice), droplet size (bigger = more efficient catch), airspeed (faster = more droplets per second), aircraft shape (smaller radius of curvature = more efficient catch), temperature (nearer freezing = more liquid water available), and exposure time (how long you stay in it). Add them all up and you can get accumulation rates of inches per minute in severe conditions — enough to overwhelm deicing systems or bring down an aircraft in under ten minutes.
exam_tip: Accretion rate = f(LWC, droplet size, airspeed, airfoil shape, temperature, exposure time). Severe conditions = inches per minute. Can overwhelm deicing.
key_facts: Liquid water content of the cloud
Droplet size
True airspeed (more droplets per second at higher speed)
Aircraft geometry (smaller leading-edge radius catches more)
Air temperature (best near freezing level)
Exposure time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the hazards of airframe icing to aircraft in flight
concept: Airframe ice is dangerous for several reasons at once. It increases weight (extra load). It distorts the shape of the wing, destroying laminar flow and reducing lift. It increases drag significantly. It can block control surfaces or cause control imbalance. It can block pitot and static ports, giving unreliable airspeed and altitude readings. It can choke engine intakes. And it can build up on propellers, causing imbalance and performance loss. Put it all together and you're fighting more weight and more drag with less lift, less thrust, and potentially unreliable instruments. Aircraft have been lost in under ten minutes in severe icing.
exam_tip: Ice hazards: added weight, lost lift, more drag, control issues, blocked pitot/static, engine intake issues, prop imbalance. More weight, more drag, less lift, less thrust — all at the same time.
key_facts: Added weight
Distorted wing shape = lost lift
Increased drag
Control surface blockage or imbalance
Blocked pitot/static ports and unreliable instruments
Engine intake blockage and prop imbalance
Can cause loss of control in under 10 minutes in severe conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: List the intensity classifications of icing
concept: The ICAO icing intensity classifications are: TRACE (just perceptible, ice accreting very slowly), LIGHT (accretion noticeable but flight can continue indefinitely without anti-icing if exposure is limited), MODERATE (rate of accretion such that even short encounters become potentially hazardous — anti-ice equipment needed or diversion), SEVERE (anti-ice equipment fails to control the ice or is not available — immediate diversion required). The categories are defined by accretion rate and by the effect on aircraft handling.
exam_tip: Four categories: Trace, Light, Moderate, Severe. Know the order and the rough definitions. Moderate needs anti-ice; Severe requires immediate escape.
key_facts: Trace — perceptible but very slow accretion
Light — continuous flight possible without anti-ice (short exposure)
Moderate — hazardous accretion; anti-ice or diversion needed
Severe — anti-ice cannot cope; immediate escape required
ICAO standard classifications
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of different intensity classifications of icing on aircraft
concept: Effect on aircraft by intensity: trace ice has minimal effect. Light ice causes some performance loss but the aircraft remains controllable. Moderate ice causes noticeable performance degradation — reduced climb, increased stall speed, some control sluggishness, and rapid accretion. Severe ice can cause rapid loss of control, stall at higher than normal speeds, and failure of control surfaces — flights have been lost within minutes of entering severe icing.
exam_tip: Trace = minimal. Light = some performance loss. Moderate = significant degradation, need anti-ice. Severe = rapid loss of control possible. Match intensity to aircraft effect.
key_facts: Trace: minimal effect
Light: some performance loss; controllable
Moderate: significant degradation, anti-ice needed
Severe: rapid loss of control possible
Classification correlates with time-to-hazard
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain methods of avoiding or mitigating airframe icing
concept: Avoiding or mitigating airframe icing: plan to stay out of known icing conditions if you don't have certified anti-ice. Use aircraft anti-ice and de-ice systems per the flight manual. Fly at altitudes below the freezing level where possible. Climb above icing-producing cloud layers if you have the performance. Descend to warmer air below the freezing level if safe. Change course to exit icing conditions as quickly as possible. Monitor accretion rates continually and don't wait to act. Avoid Cb at all costs. And know your aircraft's certification status — many light IFR aircraft are NOT certified for flight into known icing.
exam_tip: Avoidance strategies: don't enter icing without certification, use anti-ice, climb/descend out, reroute, avoid Cb. Early action beats late heroics.
key_facts: Avoid known icing if not certified for it
Use anti-ice and de-ice systems per the flight manual
Climb above or descend below icing layers
Route around icing-producing weather
Avoid Cb at all costs
Monitor accretion and act early
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the conditions required for the development of thunderstorms
concept: For a thunderstorm to develop you need three things: moisture (to fuel condensation and precipitation), instability (so that a parcel of lifted air will keep rising on its own), and a lifting mechanism (to get the air moving upward initially — could be surface heating, orographic lift, a front, or convergence). Remove any one and you won't get a thunderstorm. NZ sees all three regularly in summer, and in any season over the ranges where orographic lifting is available.
exam_tip: Three ingredients: moisture, instability, lifting mechanism. All three needed. Miss one and no Cb.
key_facts: Moisture source (for condensation and precipitation)
Conditional instability in the atmosphere
Lifting mechanism (heating, orographic, frontal, convergence)
All three are required simultaneously
NZ gets all three in summer regularly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: convective localised (stationary) thunderstorms
concept: Convective localised (stationary) thunderstorms form when a parcel of warm moist air is lifted (usually by surface heating) into an unstable atmosphere, and the resulting Cb develops in place. They're 'air mass' thunderstorms — not associated with any front or synoptic driver. They go through the classic cumulus → mature → dissipating stages in about 30-60 minutes, and they don't move far from where they form. Common on summer afternoons over inland NZ.
exam_tip: Localised/stationary convective thunderstorm = air-mass storm, triggered by surface heating, stays put, 30-60 min life cycle. Summer afternoons inland.
key_facts: Triggered by local surface heating
Forms in an unstable air mass without frontal driving
Life cycle of about 30-60 minutes
Doesn't move far from its origin
Common inland NZ on summer afternoons
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: convective traveling thunderstorms
concept: Convective travelling thunderstorms develop when the upper-level winds are strong enough to carry the storm downwind as it grows. Unlike stationary storms, they track across the landscape at the speed of the steering wind, and can last longer because new cells regenerate on the upwind edge. They can become multi-cell or supercell storms if conditions are favourable. NZ sees travelling storms in strong westerlies moving across the country.
exam_tip: Travelling convective storm = upper winds carry it along, can regenerate, lasts longer than stationary storms. Tracks downwind.
key_facts: Moves downwind at the steering wind speed
Upper-level wind shear drives the movement
Can regenerate cells on the upwind edge
May evolve into multi-cell or supercell storms
Longer-lived than stationary storms
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: orographic thunderstorms
concept: Orographic thunderstorms are triggered by the forced lifting of moist air up a mountain range — the range itself provides the lifting mechanism. NZ's Southern Alps are a classic generator of orographic storms in moist unstable westerlies. They can produce heavy rain localised to the ranges and tend to reform in the same places as long as the flow is feeding them.
exam_tip: Orographic thunderstorm = lifted by mountain slopes. NZ Southern Alps are a classic generator. Localised to the ranges.
key_facts: Triggered by forced lifting up a mountain slope
Moist unstable air needed
NZ Southern Alps produce them in westerly flows
Storms tend to reform over the same terrain
Produce heavy localised rain
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: nocturnal tropical thunderstorms
concept: Nocturnal tropical thunderstorms form overnight in tropical regions, driven by radiative cooling of the cloud tops (which increases instability aloft) rather than by daytime surface heating. They're common in the tropics over oceans and warm land surfaces. Not a typical feature of NZ weather but asked about in theory exams because of the worldwide context.
exam_tip: Nocturnal tropical thunderstorms = form at night in the tropics, driven by radiative cooling at cloud tops, not surface heating. Tropical-specific.
key_facts: Form overnight in tropical regions
Driven by radiative cooling of cloud tops
Not triggered by surface heating
Common over tropical oceans
Rare in temperate NZ but asked in exams
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: frontal and convergence-type thunderstorms
concept: Frontal and convergence-type thunderstorms are triggered by lifting at a front (cold or warm) or at a convergence line between two air masses. Cold fronts are particularly effective generators because the wedging cold air pushes the warm air up sharply. The storms can be violent and are embedded in a wider weather system. Convergence lines — where two wind flows meet — can also trigger storms along the line of meeting.
exam_tip: Frontal/convergence thunderstorms = lifting at a front or where winds converge. Cold fronts are particularly potent. Embedded in wider weather systems.
key_facts: Triggered by lifting at cold fronts, warm fronts, or convergence lines
Cold fronts are particularly effective
Embedded in wider weather systems
Can be organised into squall lines
Often severe
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: surface trough and upper trough thunderstorms
concept: Surface trough and upper trough thunderstorms form where low pressure troughs at the surface or aloft provide lifting and instability. Upper troughs are particularly important because they bring cold air aloft over warmer surface air, increasing instability substantially. Many NZ summer thunderstorms are triggered by upper-level troughs moving across the country.
exam_tip: Trough-induced thunderstorms = lifting and cooling from surface or upper troughs. Upper troughs are particularly effective at destabilising the atmosphere. Common NZ trigger.
key_facts: Triggered by surface or upper-level low-pressure troughs
Upper troughs bring cold air aloft, increasing instability
Surface troughs provide convergent lifting
Common trigger for NZ summer storms
Often generate widespread Cb areas
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics and development of: warm front embedded thunderstorms
concept: Warm front embedded thunderstorms are Cb hidden inside the widespread stratiform cloud mass of a warm front. They form when an unstable layer is present within the warm front cloud and the overall frontal lifting triggers convection in that layer. They're particularly dangerous because they're hidden — you can't see them visually inside the cloud mass, and they may not show up clearly on weather radar. Embedded Cb in warm fronts are a well-known IFR hazard.
exam_tip: Warm front embedded Cb = thunderstorms hidden in stratiform cloud of a warm front. Dangerous because they're invisible and can't be avoided visually.
key_facts: Cb hidden in the widespread cloud of a warm front
Form when an unstable layer is present within the frontal cloud
Cannot be seen visually from outside the cloud
May not show clearly on weather radar
A classic IFR hazard — always consider the possibility in warm frontal weather
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: turbulence
concept: Turbulence in and around thunderstorms is severe to extreme. Inside the Cb, updraughts and downdraughts can exceed 100 kt. Outside the storm, severe turbulence can extend 20 NM horizontally from the cloud itself and several thousand feet above the anvil. The standard recommendation is to stay at least 20 NM clear of severe Cb in light aircraft. Even the smooth-looking area around a Cb can contain damaging turbulence.
exam_tip: Cb turbulence = severe to extreme inside, severe up to 20 NM away and several thousand feet above the anvil. Stay well clear. 20 NM rule of thumb.
key_facts: Severe to extreme turbulence inside the Cb
Up- and downdraughts can exceed 100 kt
Severe turbulence extends up to 20 NM from the cloud
Several thousand feet above the anvil is also turbulent
Minimum 20 NM clearance from severe Cb
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: icing
concept: Icing in a Cb can be extreme — large supercooled droplets, high liquid water content, and rapid accretion rates. Clear ice is the usual product. Icing is worst between 0°C and about -20°C within the cloud but can extend higher in vigorous storms. A Cb is one of the most dangerous icing environments an aircraft can enter.
exam_tip: Cb icing = severe clear ice, 0°C to -20°C, large droplets, rapid accretion. One of the worst icing environments.
key_facts: Severe clear ice in Cb cores
Large supercooled droplets
High liquid water content
Most intense 0°C to -20°C
Rapid accretion rates
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: microbursts
concept: Microbursts are intense localised downdraughts that spread out at the surface when they hit. They can produce wind changes of 50-90 kt over a very short distance, making them extremely dangerous on approach or departure. A wet microburst is associated with a Cb outflow and brings rain; a dry microburst is associated with a virga shaft from Cb and can occur without visible rain. Small but deadly — a wind-shear encounter at low altitude can be unrecoverable.
exam_tip: Microburst = intense localised downdraught, spreads at surface, 50-90 kt wind change in seconds. Cb outflow related. Wet (with rain) or dry (virga). Deadly on approach/departure.
key_facts: Intense localised downdraught from a Cb
Produces sudden 50-90 kt wind changes at the surface
Wet microburst — with rain from the Cb
Dry microburst — virga only, can occur without visible rain
Extremely hazardous on approach and departure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: first gust (or gust front)
concept: The first gust (or gust front) is the leading edge of the cold downdraught air spreading out from a mature Cb. It marks a sudden wind change — the surface wind shifts direction and increases sharply — and it often arrives 10 minutes or more before the rain itself. The first gust is the classic warning sign that a storm is nearby and a cause of abrupt windshear on departure or approach. Treat every sudden wind change near a thunderstorm as a potentially hazardous gust front.
exam_tip: First gust / gust front = leading edge of cold outflow from a Cb. Sudden wind change, arrives before the rain. Classic pre-storm warning sign.
key_facts: Leading edge of cold Cb downdraught outflow
Produces sudden wind direction and speed change
Arrives before the rain (sometimes 10+ minutes ahead)
Pre-storm warning sign
Major hazard on approach and departure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: electrical phenomena
concept: Electrical phenomena around a Cb include lightning (cloud-to-ground, cloud-to-cloud, and intra-cloud), static discharge, and precipitation static. Lightning strikes on aircraft are usually survivable but can damage avionics, fuel systems, and structure. Static discharge from flying near or through charged cloud can create noise in the comms, navigation errors, and visible static discharges (St Elmo's fire). Stay well clear of active Cb for the lightning risk alone.
exam_tip: Cb electrical: lightning (cloud-ground, cloud-cloud, intra-cloud), static discharge, St Elmo's fire. Stay clear. Strikes can damage avionics.
key_facts: Lightning: cloud-to-ground, cloud-to-cloud, intra-cloud
Static discharge — radio interference and navigation errors
St Elmo's fire visible on leading edges in charged areas
Strikes can damage avionics, fuel systems and structure
Stay well clear of active Cb
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: tornadoes (if any)
concept: Tornadoes are rotating columns of air extending from the base of a Cb to the ground, producing extreme winds (up to 300+ kt in the biggest ones). NZ gets tornadoes occasionally — particularly in the Taranaki and Waikato regions from winter squall lines — but they're small and short-lived compared with the US midwest. The main lesson: powerful Cb can produce tornadoes anywhere, including NZ, and the area under a severe thunderstorm is not a place to be at low level.
exam_tip: Tornadoes = rotating columns from Cb base to ground. NZ gets small ones occasionally (Taranaki, Waikato, winter). Avoid severe Cb areas at low level.
key_facts: Rotating columns of air from Cb base to ground
Can exceed 300 kt wind in the most extreme cases
NZ sees small tornadoes occasionally, mostly in Taranaki and Waikato
Often associated with winter squall lines in NZ
Rare but extremely dangerous where they occur
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: hail
concept: Hail forms in Cb when water drops are carried into sub-freezing updraughts, freeze, and then grow by collecting more liquid water as they oscillate up and down in the storm. Hailstones can exceed golf-ball size in severe storms and cause catastrophic damage to airframes, leading edges, and windscreens. Large hail has been ejected from the anvil of a Cb and found in clear air many miles downwind — another reason to give big storms a wide berth.
exam_tip: Hail forms in Cb updraughts; can be ejected into clear air downwind of the anvil. Large hail causes catastrophic airframe damage. Another reason to stay well clear.
key_facts: Forms in Cb updraughts by repeated freezing and collection of water
Can exceed golf-ball size in severe storms
Causes catastrophic damage to airframes and windscreens
Can be ejected from the anvil into clear air downwind
A major hazard even outside the visible cloud
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With reference to flight in and around thunderstorms, describe the development, severity, and areas where the following are likely to be encountered: poor visibility
concept: Poor visibility in and around a Cb comes from heavy rain, hail, blowing debris, and the cloud itself. Visibility inside the storm core can be near zero. On approach and departure, the combination of heavy rain, dark cloud, and rapidly changing wind makes visual reference to the ground extremely difficult. Add lightning and it's a genuinely hostile environment to be anywhere near.
exam_tip: Cb visibility = heavy rain, hail, dark cloud, blowing debris, near-zero inside. Makes approach/departure very hard visually.
key_facts: Heavy rain and hail reduce visibility inside and near the Cb
Blowing debris in strong outflows
Cloud itself limits visual references
Near-zero visibility inside a Cb core
Particularly hazardous on approach and departure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of multi-cell thunderstorms
concept: Multi-cell thunderstorms consist of several individual Cb cells at different stages of their life cycle, clustered together. New cells form on the upwind (gust front) side while old cells dissipate on the downwind side, so the overall storm complex can persist for hours — much longer than a single-cell storm. They can cover large areas, produce heavy rain over wide regions, and contain severe weather (hail, microbursts, tornadoes) in the most vigorous cells. Common along cold fronts and squall lines.
exam_tip: Multi-cell storm = cluster of cells in different life-cycle stages, new cells on the upwind edge, old cells decaying downwind. Persists for hours. Common on cold fronts.
key_facts: Cluster of Cb cells in different life-cycle stages
New cells form on the upwind side
Old cells decay on the downwind side
Can persist for several hours
Cover large areas with widespread severe weather
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of radar to identify thunderstorms
concept: Weather radar detects thunderstorms by the radar reflection from precipitation inside the cloud — heavier precipitation returns a stronger echo. Colour-coded displays (green, yellow, red, magenta or similar) show increasing intensity. Radar is extremely useful for avoiding storms, but it has limitations: it only sees precipitation (not turbulence directly), it can be masked by intervening precipitation ('attenuation'), it may miss small or developing cells, and ground radar networks can have coverage gaps. Airborne radar is the gold standard for in-flight avoidance but requires interpretation skill.
exam_tip: Weather radar sees precipitation, not turbulence directly. Colour-coded intensity. Watch for attenuation behind heavy cells and gaps in coverage. Airborne radar is best in flight.
key_facts: Detects precipitation intensity by radar reflection
Colour-coded intensity displays
Only sees precipitation — not turbulence directly
Subject to attenuation behind heavy cells
Ground network can have coverage gaps
Airborne radar is the gold standard for in-flight avoidance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions that can be taken by pilots to avoid or minimise the effects of flying in the vicinity of thunderstorms
concept: Precautions around thunderstorms: don't fly within 20 NM of a severe Cb. Don't try to fly under a Cb at low altitude. Don't try to out-climb one — you almost certainly can't. Don't take off or land at an aerodrome with a nearby active Cb. Use radar (airborne or via ATC) to route around storms. Turn cockpit lighting bright if near lightning to protect night vision. Tighten seatbelts, secure loose items, slow to turbulence penetration speed if you have to fly through turbulent air. And if in doubt — delay, divert, or land. Thunderstorms have written the graveyard of many pilots who thought they'd push on through.
exam_tip: 20 NM clearance rule from severe Cb. Don't underfly. Don't outclimb. Route around. Slow to turbulence penetration speed. Delay or divert if in doubt.
key_facts: Stay at least 20 NM from severe Cb
Don't underfly or try to outclimb a Cb
Use airborne or ATC radar to route around
Turn cockpit lights bright near lightning
Slow to turbulence penetration speed if forced to fly through turbulence
Delay, divert or land if in any doubt
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on turbulence: atmospheric stability
concept: Atmospheric stability enhances turbulence when it's LOW — i.e., unstable air generates turbulence through convective mixing. Stable air is typically smooth because vertical motion is suppressed. So the rule is backward from what you might think: instability = turbulence, stability = smooth air. That's why hot unstable afternoons are rougher than cold stable mornings.
exam_tip: Instability enhances turbulence (convective mixing). Stable air is smooth. Sounds backwards — unstable is rough, stable is smooth.
key_facts: Unstable air generates convective turbulence
Stable air suppresses vertical motion and is generally smooth
Hot afternoons are rougher than cold mornings
The more unstable the profile, the more intense the turbulence
The enhancing factor is instability, not stability
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on turbulence: surface roughness
concept: Surface roughness increases low-level turbulence by creating mechanical mixing as the wind flows over obstacles. Rougher surfaces (forests, urban areas, mountains) produce more turbulence than smoother ones (open water, flat plains). The deeper the friction layer, the higher up the turbulence reaches.
exam_tip: Rougher surface = more mechanical turbulence = deeper turbulent layer. Mountains and urban areas > plains > sea.
key_facts: Rougher surfaces produce more mechanical turbulence
Mountains and urban areas are roughest
Sea and flat plains smoother
Deeper friction layer with more roughness
Combines with wind speed and stability
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on turbulence: wind speed/direction
concept: Stronger winds amplify turbulence because there's more energy available to stir the air. Doubling the wind speed roughly quadruples the kinetic energy. Wind direction also matters near terrain — a crosswind perpendicular to a ridge is much worse than one blowing parallel to it. For a mountain wave setup you specifically need a strong wind component perpendicular to the ridge.
exam_tip: Stronger wind = more turbulence. Direction matters too — cross-ridge flow much worse than along-ridge. Perpendicular-to-ridge component drives mountain waves.
key_facts: Stronger wind = more kinetic energy = more turbulence
Kinetic energy scales with the square of wind speed
Cross-ridge winds much worse than along-ridge
Perpendicular-to-ridge component needed for mountain waves
Combines with stability and surface roughness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the following enhancing factors on turbulence: vertical windshear
concept: Vertical windshear — a change in wind speed or direction with altitude — is a direct generator of turbulence. Where layers of air are moving at different speeds or directions, the shear zone between them becomes turbulent. Strong vertical shear is associated with clear air turbulence (CAT), mountain waves, thunderstorms, and jet streams. You can't see it, but you can feel it the moment you enter it.
exam_tip: Vertical windshear is a direct turbulence generator. Source of CAT, mountain wave turbulence, thunderstorm turbulence. Can't see it in clear air.
key_facts: Change in wind speed/direction with altitude
Generates turbulence at the shear boundary
Source of clear air turbulence (CAT)
Associated with jet streams, mountain waves, and fronts
Not visible — experienced as sudden rough air
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: surface friction
concept: Low-level windshear from surface friction: the basic friction-layer effect produces a wind that increases and changes direction with height within the first 2,000 ft or so. On climb-out or approach, an aircraft is passing through this layer and experiences shear as a result. Normally this is gradual and aircraft cope with it easily, but on windy days or over rough terrain the gradient can be sharp enough to be hazardous.
exam_tip: Surface friction windshear = the natural increase of wind with height through the friction layer. Usually gradual but can be sharp in strong winds or over rough terrain.
key_facts: Wind increases and changes direction with height through the friction layer
Friction layer typically extends to 2,000 ft AGL
Experienced by aircraft on climb-out and approach
Normally gradual
Sharp gradients over rough terrain or in strong winds
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: thunderstorms
concept: Thunderstorm windshear is the most dangerous low-level windshear an aircraft can encounter. It's generated by the downdraughts and gust front of a Cb — sudden changes of 30-90 kt in wind direction and speed over very short distances. On approach or departure, microburst windshear has caused multiple fatal accidents worldwide. The hazard is the speed of the change combined with low altitude, leaving little time or altitude to recover.
exam_tip: Thunderstorm windshear (microbursts) is the most dangerous low-level shear type. Sudden 30-90 kt changes. Fatal on approach/departure.
key_facts: Caused by Cb downdraughts and gust fronts
Sudden 30-90 kt wind changes over short distances
Most dangerous low-level windshear type
Cause of many fatal approach/departure accidents
Requires complete avoidance of Cb-affected airspace
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: temperature inversions
concept: Temperature inversions produce low-level windshear when the wind above the inversion is much stronger than the wind below it. The wind shift at the inversion height can be abrupt. Nocturnal surface inversions are a classic source — calm at the surface with a 40-kt wind 500 ft up. On early morning departures you can climb from dead calm into a strong wind within a few hundred feet.
exam_tip: Inversion windshear = strong wind above inversion, light wind below. Nocturnal surface inversions are the classic case. Sudden shift at inversion height.
key_facts: Inversion layer with strong wind above and light wind below
Nocturnal surface inversions are the classic source
Sudden wind change across the inversion height
Common on calm early mornings
Can be significant on climb-out
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: frontal activity
concept: Frontal windshear occurs at the boundary between two air masses in a front. Cold fronts produce sharper shear because the boundary is steeper — the wind can change direction by 90° or more and strength by 20-30 kt within a narrow band. Warm fronts have a shallower slope and gentler shear. The shear is most dangerous right at the surface passage of the front.
exam_tip: Frontal windshear = sharp at cold fronts, gentler at warm fronts. Cold front = sudden wind shift and strength change at passage.
key_facts: Wind shear at the boundary between air masses in a front
Cold fronts give sharper shear (steeper slope)
Warm fronts give gentler shear (shallow slope)
Most dangerous at the surface passage of the front
Can produce 20-30 kt strength and 90° direction changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the cause(s) and factors involved with the effects of low-level wind-shear due to: wake turbulence from fixed and rotary winged aircraft
concept: Wake turbulence from fixed-wing and rotary-wing aircraft produces a specific form of low-level windshear — counter-rotating vortices trailing from the wingtips of departing and arriving aircraft. It persists for 2-3 minutes and settles roughly 500-900 ft below the generating aircraft's flight path. Light aircraft following heavier ones into land need to stay above the heavier aircraft's flight path and land further down the runway. Helicopter rotor downwash creates similar hazardous turbulence. Standard separation minima exist to manage the risk.
exam_tip: Wake turbulence = wingtip vortices behind departing/arriving aircraft. Counter-rotating, persists 2-3 mins, sinks 500-900 ft. Follow ATC separation. Stay above the heavier aircraft's flight path.
key_facts: Counter-rotating wingtip vortices
Generated by all aircraft; worst behind heavy/large aircraft
Persists for 2-3 minutes
Sinks 500-900 ft below the generating aircraft's path
Helicopter rotor downwash creates similar turbulence
ATC wake turbulence separation minima exist for a reason
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe the techniques used to avoid or minimize the effects of low-level windshear
concept: Techniques to avoid or minimise low-level windshear: check TAFs and METARs for wind reports and ATIS windshear advisories. Listen for pilot reports on the frequency. Use longer runways if available. Fly stabilised approaches with extra speed margin if windshear is suspected. Go around early if the approach becomes unstable — don't try to recover a bad shear encounter low and slow. Delay departure through known shear. Most importantly, avoid any Cb or convective activity within 5 NM of the departure or arrival path. Recognising the environment early is the single biggest factor.
exam_tip: Windshear techniques: check weather and PIREPs, longer runways, stabilised approach with speed margin, go around early, delay if needed, avoid Cb near airfield. Recognition first.
key_facts: Check TAFs, METARs, ATIS for windshear advisories
Listen for pilot reports
Use longer runways when available
Fly stabilised approach with extra speed margin
Go around early if unstable
Avoid any Cb within 5 NM of departure/arrival path
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: light turbulence
concept: Light turbulence per ICAO: momentarily causes slight changes in altitude and/or attitude (pitch, roll, yaw) or causes slight rapid bumpiness. Food service and walking may be possible with some difficulty. Seatbelt is not essential but recommended.
exam_tip: Light turbulence = slight altitude/attitude changes or bumpiness. Food service possible. Belts recommended but not required.
key_facts: Slight momentary altitude or attitude changes
Slight rapid bumpiness
Food service possible with difficulty
Walking possible
Seatbelts recommended
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: moderate turbulence
concept: Moderate turbulence per ICAO: causes changes in altitude and/or attitude but the aircraft remains in positive control at all times. Usually causes variations in indicated airspeed. Occupants feel definite strain against seat belts. Unsecured objects are dislodged. Food service and walking are difficult.
exam_tip: Moderate turbulence = definite altitude/attitude changes but positive control maintained. Strain against seatbelts. Loose items move. Food service difficult.
key_facts: Definite altitude or attitude changes
Positive control always maintained
Variations in indicated airspeed
Occupants feel strain against seatbelts
Unsecured objects dislodged
Food service and walking difficult
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Describe, in accordance with the ICAO definitions, the characteristics of: severe turbulence
concept: Severe turbulence per ICAO: causes large abrupt changes in altitude and/or attitude. Usually causes large variations in indicated airspeed. The aircraft may be momentarily out of control. Occupants are forced violently against seat belts. Unsecured objects are tossed about. Food service and walking are impossible. This is the level where structural damage becomes possible in extreme cases.
exam_tip: Severe turbulence = large abrupt changes, momentary loss of control possible, occupants thrown against belts, loose items tossed around. Possible structural damage in extremes.
key_facts: Large abrupt altitude or attitude changes
Large airspeed variations
Momentary loss of control possible
Occupants violently thrown against seatbelts
Unsecured objects tossed about
Structural damage possible in extreme cases
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the methods by which the aviation community is advised of volcanic eruptions within the New Zealand FIR
concept: Volcanic eruption advisories in NZ are issued through several channels. The Volcanic Ash Advisory Centre (VAAC) in Wellington issues Volcanic Ash Advisories (VAAs) covering the NZ FIR and the southwest Pacific. SIGMETs are issued by MetService for volcanic ash affecting NZ airspace. NOTAMs are issued for temporary airspace restrictions or hazardous areas. ATIS broadcasts may include volcanic ash information for affected aerodromes. Operators also get direct alerts via AFTN and email. For any flight over or near NZ volcanic areas (Ruapehu, Taranaki, Tongariro, White Island), check the latest information before departure.
exam_tip: Volcanic ash advisories: VAAC Wellington issues VAAs, MetService issues SIGMETs, NOTAMs for airspace restrictions, ATIS for aerodrome info. Check before any flight near active volcanoes.
key_facts: VAAC Wellington issues Volcanic Ash Advisories (VAAs)
MetService issues SIGMETs for volcanic ash
NOTAMs for airspace restrictions and hazards
ATIS broadcasts include volcanic ash info for affected aerodromes
Direct alerts via AFTN to operators
Critical for flights near Ruapehu, Taranaki, Tongariro, White Island
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards to aviation of volcanic ash encountered: in flight
concept: Volcanic ash in flight is extremely dangerous. The ash is made of glassy, abrasive silicate particles that get sucked into jet engines, melt in the combustion chambers, and re-solidify on turbine blades — causing engine flame-out. It also sandblasts cockpit windscreens to near-zero visibility, erodes leading edges, damages pitot/static systems, and contaminates environmental control systems. Multiple commercial jets have lost all engines after flying into ash clouds (BA009 over Indonesia in 1982 is the famous case). Avoidance is the only safe strategy — stay 20 NM clear of any visible ash, and even further if downwind.
exam_tip: Volcanic ash in flight = engine flameout, windscreen damage, sandblasting, pitot-static blockage. Historic multi-engine failures (BA009). Stay well clear — avoidance is the only option.
key_facts: Glassy silicate particles ingested by engines
Melts in combustion chambers and re-solidifies on turbine blades
Causes engine flameout
Sandblasts windscreens, erodes leading edges
Blocks pitot-static systems
BA009 over Indonesia (1982) is the famous case
Stay at least 20 NM clear, further if downwind
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the hazards to aviation of volcanic ash encountered: during the take-off and landing phases on an ash contaminated runway
concept: Volcanic ash on a runway during take-off or landing creates several problems at once. It reduces friction, lengthening landing distance and braking. It can be stirred up by prop wash or jet blast and ingested into engines on the ground. It's slippery when wet. And a contaminated runway may require closure until it's cleaned. Reverse thrust should be avoided to minimise ash ingestion. Any take-off or landing on an ash-contaminated surface should be treated as a contaminated runway operation with appropriate performance penalties.
exam_tip: Ash on runway: reduced friction, ingestion risk, slippery when wet. Avoid reverse thrust. Treat as contaminated runway. May require closure.
key_facts: Reduces runway friction and braking
Can be ingested into engines from prop wash or jet blast
Slippery when wet
Runway may need closure and cleaning
Avoid use of reverse thrust
Treat as a contaminated runway operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: dust storms
concept: Dust storms (or sandstorms) develop in arid regions when strong surface winds lift dust or sand into the air and carry it over long distances. They can reduce visibility to near zero, damage aircraft surfaces, contaminate engines, and cause navigation problems by obscuring ground references. Not a common NZ hazard but relevant in Australian and overseas operations. Hazards include the low visibility itself, abrasive damage, and possible IMC-equivalent conditions at low level.
exam_tip: Dust/sandstorms = visibility near zero, abrasive damage, navigation problems. Rare in NZ but possible in Australian operations. IMC-equivalent at low level.
key_facts: Strong surface winds lifting dust/sand in arid regions
Visibility can be reduced to near zero
Causes abrasive damage and engine contamination
Long-distance transport possible
IMC-equivalent at low level
Common in Australia, rare in NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: blowing surface snow (blizzards)
concept: Blowing surface snow (blizzards) occurs when strong winds lift loose snow from the surface into the air, sharply reducing visibility. In the Southern Alps in winter this is a genuine hazard — visibility can drop from 10 km to under 200 m in minutes as a squall moves through. Blizzards also contribute to disorientation, drifting, and whiteout conditions. A combined hazard with low ceilings, cold temperatures, and ice.
exam_tip: Blizzards = strong wind + loose snow = sharp vis reduction. Southern Alps in winter. Combines with low ceiling, cold, icing. Can cause whiteout.
key_facts: Strong surface winds lifting loose snow
Sharp and sudden visibility reduction
Hazardous in NZ Southern Alps in winter
Contributes to disorientation and whiteout
Combines with low ceilings, cold, and icing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: Explain the development of, and the hazards associated with, flight in the following conditions: whiteout (visual illusion type)
concept: Whiteout is a visual illusion caused by the combination of overcast sky, uniform snow-covered surface, and absence of shadows or contrast. The horizon disappears, depth perception is lost, and the pilot has no visual reference to judge altitude or attitude. It's a leading cause of accidents in polar and high-latitude operations and in NZ alpine skifield operations. The safest action is to treat whiteout conditions as IMC and rely on instruments — never trust visual cues in a whiteout.
exam_tip: Whiteout = loss of horizon and depth perception from uniform snow + overcast. Treat as IMC. Never trust visual cues. Major cause of alpine flying accidents.
key_facts: Caused by uniform snow surface and overcast sky
Loss of horizon, contrast, and depth perception
Leading cause of polar and alpine flight accidents
NZ Southern Alps a known risk area in winter
Treat as IMC and rely on instruments
Never trust visual cues in whiteout
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, AFT Meteorology, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, CAA GAP Weather and Winter Flying

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify: areas of stable and unstable air
concept: On satellite imagery you can identify stable versus unstable air by looking at the cloud texture and pattern. Stable air shows up as smooth, uniform stratiform cloud sheets with no bumpy texture — think of a grey blanket covering the area. Unstable air shows up as lumpy, textured, cellular cloud — individual cumulus or cumulonimbus cells with bright tops (in IR imagery) and clear gaps between. The cellular 'popcorn' pattern is the classic signature of unstable convection. In visible imagery, stable layers look flat and uniform; unstable clouds cast shadows and show clear three-dimensional structure.
exam_tip: Stable air = smooth uniform cloud sheets. Unstable air = lumpy, cellular, 'popcorn' textured cloud. Cellular structure is the unstable signature.
key_facts: Stable air: smooth, uniform stratiform cloud sheets
Unstable air: lumpy, cellular, cumuliform cloud patterns
Cellular 'popcorn' pattern = clear unstable signature
Visible imagery shows 3D shadows from unstable cloud
Infrared imagery shows bright tops for cold Cb
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify: the processes causing each significant area or mass of cloud
concept: Identifying the processes causing cloud masses on satellite imagery is really about pattern recognition. Frontal cloud bands are long, narrow, and often curved in a comma or spiral shape around a low centre. Orographic cloud sits over and just downwind of mountain ranges — you'll see a clear line following the topography. Convective cloud is cellular and lumpy, often appearing in clusters or lines in unstable air. Fog and low stratus shows up as smooth, uniform cloud sheets filling valleys or coastal plains. Once you've looked at a few dozen NZ satellite images you start recognising the processes at a glance.
exam_tip: Pattern recognition: frontal = long curved band, orographic = over ranges, convective = cellular, fog/low stratus = smooth valley/coastal sheet. Practice with real MetService images.
key_facts: Fronts: long curved cloud bands, often comma-shaped
Orographic cloud: sits over and downwind of mountain ranges
Convective cloud: cellular and lumpy, often clustered
Fog/low stratus: smooth sheets in valleys and coastal plains
Pattern recognition improves rapidly with practice
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, using given examples of satellite imagery, identify: likely cloud types and weather associated with each significant area of cloud
concept: Likely cloud types and weather from satellite imagery follow from identifying the processes. Deep convective cloud — bright and textured in IR — means Cb, likely heavy showers, possible thunderstorms, turbulence, icing. Long frontal bands mean layered cloud sequence (Ci, Cs, As, Ns) and continuous rain. Orographic cloud over ranges means low ceilings, rain, icing, and mountain wave activity downwind. Smooth low stratus sheets means below-minima conditions in the affected region but often good weather just a few hundred feet above. For IFR planning this is a very quick way to get a visual summary of the day's weather.
exam_tip: Cloud pattern → weather expected: Cb texture = thunderstorms, frontal band = widespread rain, orographic cap = low ceilings over ranges, low stratus = fog/below minima.
key_facts: Deep convective tops → Cb, thunderstorms, icing, turbulence
Frontal bands → continuous stratiform rain
Orographic caps → low ceilings and icing over ranges
Low stratus sheets → fog and below-minima conditions at the surface
Quick visual summary of the day's hazards
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: precipitation types and intensity causing the radar echo
concept: On weather radar, different precipitation types and intensities show distinct echoes. Intensity is colour-coded — green is light, yellow moderate, red heavy, magenta extreme. Heavy rain from Cb shows up as tight red/magenta cores with sharp gradients to green at the edges. Stratiform rain from Ns shows up as widespread light to moderate echo with smooth, uniform returns. Hail returns are especially intense and often show distinctive bright cores within Cb. Snow returns more weakly than rain for the same precipitation rate, so snow in cold air can be underestimated. Mixed precipitation gives messy returns — a good reason to always cross-check with other sources.
exam_tip: Radar: green=light, yellow=moderate, red=heavy, magenta=extreme. Tight red cores = Cb. Widespread smooth green/yellow = frontal rain. Snow returns weaker than rain.
key_facts: Colour-coded intensity: green, yellow, red, magenta
Cb = tight intense cores with sharp gradients
Stratiform rain = widespread, smooth, uniform returns
Hail = extreme intensity returns within Cb
Snow returns more weakly than rain for the same rate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: likely cloud types associated with the precipitation echo
concept: Cloud type from radar echoes: intense, tight echoes with sharp boundaries point to Cb and TCu (strong convection). Widespread, smoother echoes with gradual boundaries point to stratiform cloud (Ns, As) — the classic warm front picture. Mixed echoes (scattered intense cores within widespread lighter returns) point to embedded Cb within frontal cloud — a particular IFR hazard. Weak scattered echoes from below the freezing level in cold weather may indicate snow from Sc or As. Radar doesn't see non-precipitating cloud at all, so absence of echo doesn't mean absence of cloud.
exam_tip: Radar echo type → cloud type: intense tight = Cb/TCu, smooth widespread = Ns/As, mixed = embedded Cb (IFR hazard). No echo ≠ no cloud — radar only sees precipitation.
key_facts: Intense, tight echoes → Cb, TCu (convective cloud)
Widespread smooth echoes → Ns, As (stratiform cloud)
Mixed intense cores in lighter returns → embedded Cb in frontal cloud
Weak echoes in cold air → snow from Sc/As
No echo does not mean no cloud — radar only sees precipitation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

syllabus_desc: With respect to NZ IFR operations, interpret radar imagery in terms of: speed of movement and timing of radar echoes, and the expected impact at given locations
concept: Movement speed and timing of radar echoes tells you how quickly weather will arrive at your location. Loop successive radar images (most modern displays do this automatically) to estimate the direction and speed of the echo movement, then extrapolate to your destination or route. For example, a 40 kt west-to-east cold front echo 80 NM west of Wellington will arrive in roughly two hours. Be aware that individual cells within a wider system can move differently from the system as a whole, and that new cells may develop ahead of what you see. For IFR planning this is a powerful tool — it lets you time departures and diversions around moving weather.
exam_tip: Loop the radar, estimate speed and direction, extrapolate to your destination. Allow for new cells developing ahead of the main system. Powerful timing tool for IFR planning.
key_facts: Use radar loops to estimate echo movement speed and direction
Extrapolate to route or destination for arrival timing
Individual cells may move differently from the system as a whole
New cells may develop ahead of the main echo
Key tool for timing IFR departures and diversions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, MetService Aviation Weather in NZ, AFT Meteorology

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Explain how the partial pressure of oxygen changes as altitude increases
concept: Atmospheric pressure drops as you climb, and although the percentage of oxygen in the air stays the same (about 21%), the partial pressure of oxygen drops in proportion to the total pressure. At sea level the partial pressure of oxygen is about 159 hPa, which is more than enough to saturate your blood through the lungs. By 10,000 ft it's down to around 110 hPa and your blood saturation starts to noticeably drop. By 18,000 ft it's roughly half the sea-level value, and by 35,000 ft it's so low that even pressure-breathing pure oxygen barely keeps you conscious. The lesson is simple — it's not the percentage of oxygen that matters, it's the partial pressure pushing it across the lung membrane into your blood. That's why a quick climb from Ardmore to FL150 in an unpressurised aircraft will gradually make you stupid even though the air around you is still 21% oxygen.
exam_tip: Partial pressure of oxygen drops in proportion to total atmospheric pressure. The percentage stays at 21%, but the pressure pushing it into your blood falls. That's why high altitude is hypoxic.
key_facts: Air is 21% oxygen at all altitudes you'll fly
Partial pressure of oxygen drops as total pressure drops
Sea level pO2 ~159 hPa, halved by ~18,000 ft
Lower pO2 means less oxygen pushed across lung membrane
Hypoxia is caused by low partial pressure, not low percentage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the primary physiological and behavioural consequences of hypoxia for flight crew and passengers
concept: Hypoxia hits the brain before anything else. The early symptoms are subtle and that's exactly what makes it dangerous — slowed thinking, impaired judgement, mild euphoria, tunnel vision, slight headache, fingertip and lip cyanosis (bluish tinge), and a feeling of being a bit drunk. Behaviourally pilots become careless, miss radio calls, fixate on things, fail to scan, and may become uncharacteristically aggressive or giggly. Coordination goes next — fine motor skills first, then gross movements. Eventually you lose consciousness without ever realising you were in trouble. The classic accident profile is a perfectly competent pilot who quietly stops being competent and never knows it. Passengers are vulnerable too, particularly the elderly, children, and anyone with a respiratory or cardiac problem.
exam_tip: Hypoxia symptoms: slowed thinking, poor judgement, euphoria, tunnel vision, headache, cyanosis, then unconsciousness. The killer is that you don't notice it happening to you.
key_facts: Brain is the first organ affected by hypoxia
Early symptoms: impaired judgement, euphoria, tunnel vision, headache
Behavioural effects: carelessness, fixation, missed calls, mood changes
Coordination degrades from fine to gross motor skills
Insidious onset — pilot doesn't recognise their own impairment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: List the main factors influencing variation in hypoxia onset (tolerance) between individuals
concept: Hypoxia tolerance varies a lot from one person to the next, and even from one day to the next in the same person. The main factors are physical fitness (fitter pilots cope a bit longer), age (older pilots are more susceptible), smoking status (smokers carry carbon monoxide in their blood and have less spare oxygen capacity), alcohol consumption (lowers tolerance significantly even the morning after), illness or fatigue (both reduce tolerance), recent meals (digestion uses oxygen), and any pre-existing respiratory or cardiac conditions. Acclimatisation matters too — someone who lives in Queenstown at 1100 ft has a slight head start on someone who lives at sea level in Tauranga. None of this gives you a free pass to fly higher unsupplemented, but it explains why two pilots in the same cockpit at FL120 may feel quite different.
