exam_tip: Tolerance factors: fitness, age, smoking, alcohol, illness, fatigue, recent meals, acclimatisation. Smokers and the unfit are more susceptible. Don't assume yesterday's tolerance is today's.
key_facts: Fitness improves hypoxia tolerance slightly
Age, smoking, alcohol and illness reduce tolerance
Smokers carry carbon monoxide that reduces oxygen capacity
Acclimatisation to altitude builds modest tolerance over time
Tolerance varies day to day in the same individual
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: State the factors that affect the likelihood of suffering from hypoxia
concept: The main factor that determines whether you'll get hypoxia is altitude — the higher you go, the lower the partial pressure of oxygen and the more likely you are to be affected. After altitude comes time of exposure (the longer you sit at altitude, the more your body's oxygen reserves deplete), rate of ascent (a fast climb gives you less time to notice symptoms), physical workload (working harder uses more oxygen), ambient temperature (cold increases oxygen demand), and any of the individual factors covered above (smoking, fitness, illness, alcohol). For NZ ops the practical takeaway is that anything above 10,000 ft for any meaningful length of time needs supplemental oxygen, and the rules in CAR Part 91 require it above set altitudes.
exam_tip: Likelihood factors: altitude, time at altitude, rate of ascent, workload, temperature, individual fitness/health. Altitude is the dominant factor. NZ rules require supplemental oxygen above defined limits in CAR Part 91.
key_facts: Altitude is the primary factor
Time of exposure increases the risk
Faster climbs reduce warning time
Workload and cold both increase oxygen demand
CAR Part 91 sets supplemental oxygen requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe how hypoxia can be treated
concept: Treating hypoxia is straightforward in principle — get more oxygen into the blood. In practice that means immediately donning supplemental oxygen at 100% concentration and descending to a lower altitude (10,000 ft or below for a clean recovery). If oxygen isn't available, descend immediately and aggressively. Recovery is rapid once oxygen is restored, though the pilot may feel a bit shaky and headachy for a while. After landing, evaluate why it happened — mask seal, regulator failure, oxygen system fault, exceeding cabin altitude limits, etc. Don't fly again until you've worked out what went wrong. For passengers, the same principles apply: get them on oxygen and get the cabin altitude down.
exam_tip: Treatment: 100% oxygen + immediate descent. Recovery is rapid. After landing, find the root cause before flying again.
key_facts: Don 100% oxygen immediately
Descend to 10,000 ft or below
Recovery is rapid once oxygen restored
Pilot may feel residual headache and shakiness
Investigate root cause before further flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Define the concept of 'time of useful consciousness'
concept: Time of useful consciousness (TUC), sometimes called effective performance time, is the time between losing your normal oxygen supply at altitude and the point at which you can no longer take useful action — for example, donning a mask, calling ATC, or starting a descent. After TUC expires you're not necessarily unconscious, but you're no longer capable of doing anything useful to save yourself. TUC depends on altitude, rate of decompression (a rapid decompression is much worse than a slow one because it actively forces oxygen out of your lungs), workload, and individual factors. Knowing your TUC at the altitudes you operate at is what tells you how much margin you have if something goes wrong with the oxygen system.
exam_tip: TUC = time you can still take useful action after losing your oxygen supply. Depends on altitude, decompression rate, workload. Rapid decompression cuts TUC roughly in half.
key_facts: TUC is time available to take useful corrective action
Ends before unconsciousness, not at unconsciousness
Decreases sharply with altitude
Rapid decompression roughly halves TUC
Higher workload reduces TUC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: 18,000ft
concept: At 18,000 ft, the typical time of useful consciousness is around 20 to 30 minutes for a pilot at moderate workload. That sounds like a long time, but the trap is that you may not realise anything is wrong for the first 10 to 15 minutes of that — the impairment creeps in gradually. By the time you notice something feels off, your judgement is already compromised. 18,000 ft isn't a routine NZ PPL altitude, but it's well within reach of any turbocharged single, and it's the altitude band where insidious hypoxia accidents tend to start.
exam_tip: TUC at 18,000 ft is approximately 20–30 minutes. Long enough to be sneaky — impairment starts long before TUC ends.
key_facts: Approximate TUC at 18,000 ft: 20–30 minutes
Insidious onset — impairment well before TUC ends
Within reach of any turbocharged piston
Most NZ hypoxia incidents start in this altitude band
Time given is for moderate workload
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: 25,000ft
concept: At 25,000 ft, TUC drops to around 3 to 5 minutes. That's a dramatic reduction from 18,000 ft and reflects how quickly oxygen partial pressure falls in the upper troposphere. At this altitude you've got just enough time to recognise the problem, don the mask, set 100% oxygen and start a descent — but only if you're already alert and the mask is within reach. Any delay or fumbling and you're past your TUC. NZ PPLs won't routinely operate here, but anyone moving on to CPL or instrument flight in turbocharged or turbine aircraft will, and the numbers need to be in your head before you ever get there.
exam_tip: TUC at 25,000 ft is approximately 3–5 minutes. Just enough to mask up and start a descent — no margin for fumbling.
key_facts: Approximate TUC at 25,000 ft: 3–5 minutes
Dramatic reduction compared to 18,000 ft
Just enough time for mask + descent if pre-briefed
Common turbocharged single-engine cruise altitude
No margin for delay or distraction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: State the approximate time of useful consciousness at: 35,000ft
concept: At 35,000 ft, TUC is down to roughly 30 to 60 seconds, and that's after a slow decompression. After a rapid decompression (sudden cabin pressure loss), TUC at 35,000 ft can drop to as little as 15 to 20 seconds, because the explosive decompression actively forces oxygen out of your lungs. This is why airline crews have quick-don oxygen masks within arm's reach and practise donning them in seconds. If you're ever flying at this altitude in a pressurised aircraft and the cabin lets go, the only thing standing between you and unconsciousness is whether you can get the mask on before your seconds run out.
exam_tip: TUC at 35,000 ft is approximately 30–60 seconds, halved to 15–20 seconds after a rapid decompression. This is why airline crews drill quick-don masks.
key_facts: Approximate TUC at 35,000 ft: 30–60 seconds slow decompression
Rapid decompression cuts that to 15–20 seconds
Explosive decompression forces oxygen out of lungs
Quick-don masks designed to be on within 5 seconds
Routine cruise altitude for jets — not NZ PPL territory but worth knowing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Explain oxygen paradox
concept: Oxygen paradox is the counter-intuitive worsening of symptoms that can happen in the first few seconds after you start breathing supplemental oxygen following hypoxia. You'd expect to feel better immediately, but instead you may feel briefly dizzy, faint, or even pass out for a moment. The reason is that your body has been compensating for low oxygen by hyperventilating (blowing off CO2) and dilating blood vessels. When oxygen is suddenly restored, the blood vessels constrict and blood pressure drops momentarily before everything resets. The lesson is to expect this — if you suddenly feel worse just after putting on the mask, don't pull it off thinking it isn't working. Hold on for 30 seconds and you'll be fine.
exam_tip: Oxygen paradox = brief worsening of symptoms when oxygen is first restored after hypoxia. Don't take the mask off — wait it out for 30 seconds.
key_facts: Symptoms briefly worsen when oxygen restored
Caused by sudden vasoconstriction and BP drop
Lasts 15–30 seconds
Pilot may feel dizzy or faint just after donning mask
Critical not to remove the mask thinking it's failed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe how barotrauma can be prevented
concept: Barotrauma is injury caused by trapped gas expanding or contracting as cabin pressure changes. The classic spots are the middle ear (ear block), the sinuses (sinus block), the gut (gas pains during climb), and teeth with poor fillings (toothache during ascent). Prevention is mostly about not flying with a head cold or blocked sinuses, equalising actively during descent (Valsalva manoeuvre, swallowing, yawning), keeping descent rates moderate, avoiding gas-producing foods before flight, and getting any dodgy fillings checked. If a passenger has a cold, advise them to consider a decongestant or postpone the flight. NZ pilots flying in and out of Queenstown or Wanaka regularly handle steep descents — that's where ear and sinus blocks tend to bite.
exam_tip: Barotrauma prevention: don't fly with a cold, equalise actively, slow descent rates, watch the diet, dental check. Common spots: ears, sinuses, gut, teeth.
key_facts: Affects middle ear, sinuses, gut, teeth
Equalise with Valsalva, swallowing, yawning
Avoid flying with a cold or blocked sinuses
Moderate descent rates reduce risk
Decongestants can help passengers but not recommended for crew
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: State the approximate required times between diving at various depths and flying
concept: After diving you must wait before flying because dissolved nitrogen in your tissues needs time to come out of solution gradually — flying too soon can trigger decompression sickness (the bends) at altitudes that wouldn't normally bother you. The standard guidance is at least 12 hours after a single no-decompression dive, 24 hours after multiple dives, repetitive dives, or any dive requiring decompression stops. PADI and most recreational dive bodies use these as minimums. For commercial divers and technical divers the wait is longer. Cabin altitudes as low as 8,000 ft can trigger problems if you've still got significant nitrogen on board, so even an unpressurised flight to 6,000 ft after a morning dive at the Poor Knights is a real risk.
exam_tip: Wait 12 hours after single dive, 24 hours after multiple/repetitive/decompression dives. Cabin altitudes as low as 8,000 ft can trigger DCS after diving.
key_facts: 12 hours after a single no-decompression dive
24 hours after multiple, repetitive, or decompression dives
Even low cabin altitudes can provoke DCS
Nitrogen needs time to off-gas at sea level
PADI standard adopted internationally
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe methods of cockpit/flight deck lighting and problems associated with each
concept: Cockpit lighting at night is a balancing act between seeing the instruments and preserving night vision. White light is bright and clear but destroys dark adaptation — your eyes take 20 to 30 minutes to fully re-adapt to darkness after a flash of white light. Red light preserves dark adaptation well because the rod cells in your retina (which give you night vision) are insensitive to red, but red makes red ink on charts disappear and causes some colour distortion. Blue-green or low-intensity white is the modern compromise — easier to read than red, and dimmable enough to preserve most night vision. Whatever the colour, use the lowest intensity that lets you read the instruments and chart, and avoid flicking torches or flash beams around the cockpit at night.
exam_tip: Red light preserves night vision but kills red chart markings. White light destroys dark adaptation. Modern aircraft use dimmable blue-green or low white as a compromise. Always use the minimum brightness that works.
key_facts: Red light preserves dark adaptation but distorts colours
White light destroys dark adaptation for 20–30 min
Blue-green is the modern compromise
Use minimum effective brightness
Avoid sweeping torches around the cockpit at night
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the requirements for using corrective lenses
concept: If you need glasses or contacts to see properly, the medical certificate will say so and you must wear them while exercising the privileges of your licence. CAANZ requires a spare set of corrective lenses to be carried on every flight if your medical depends on them. Reading glasses (for charts and instruments) are a separate consideration from distance glasses — many pilots over 40 end up with bifocal or progressive lenses for exactly this reason. Contact lenses are permitted but check with your DAME, especially for monovision (one eye distance, one eye near), which can affect depth perception. If your prescription changes significantly you should let your DAME know.
exam_tip: If your medical says you need glasses, you wear them and carry a spare. Bifocals or progressives common for pilots over 40. Contact lenses fine but check with DAME.
key_facts: Glasses required if listed on medical certificate
Spare set must be carried on every flight
Bifocals or progressives common for older pilots
Contact lenses permitted with DAME approval
Notify DAME of significant prescription changes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Explain the visual illusions associated with sector whiteout
concept: Sector whiteout is a visual illusion that happens when you're flying over snow or sea ice under an overcast sky. The diffuse light from the cloud above is scattered by the white surface below until there's no shadow, no horizon, no texture, no contrast — just an even white field in every direction. You lose all visual cues for attitude, height and distance. It's not the same as flying into cloud — visibility is technically unlimited — but you're effectively in IMC and have to fly on instruments. The danger is that pilots often don't recognise what's happened and try to keep flying visually. NZ alpine ops in winter, particularly in the Southern Alps and on the West Coast glaciers, are classic whiteout territory.
exam_tip: Sector whiteout = featureless white field over snow/ice under overcast. No horizon, no texture, no depth cues. Effectively IMC. Common in NZ alpine and glacier ops.
key_facts: Caused by overcast above + snow/ice surface below
All visual cues for attitude and depth disappear
Visibility may still be unlimited
Pilot must transition to instruments immediately
Major hazard for NZ alpine/glacier flying
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the methods of avoiding and/or coping with sector whiteout
concept: Avoiding sector whiteout means planning around the conditions — don't go low over featureless snow under an overcast, brief yourself on the conditions before takeoff, and have an instrument-flying option if you must operate in marginal alpine conditions. If you find yourself in whiteout, the only safe action is to immediately transition to instruments, climb if obstacles allow, and fly out of the affected area on instruments back towards visual ground references. Don't try to keep flying visually — pilots who do this often end up flying into terrain. Stay current on instrument flying even as a VFR pilot if you operate in alpine areas, and treat any forecast of low cloud over snow as a possible whiteout day.
exam_tip: Avoid by planning around the conditions. Cope by transitioning to instruments immediately, climbing, and flying out to visual references. Don't try to keep VFR — that's how you hit terrain.
key_facts: Avoid by careful planning around alpine weather
Recognise the conditions early — overcast + snow
Transition to instruments at the first sign of whiteout
Climb if terrain permits, then fly to visual references
Maintain instrument currency even as VFR pilot in alpine areas
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Specify the various levels of noise in decibels at which various grades of hearing protection are required
concept: Hearing protection requirements scale with noise exposure. At around 80 dB (a busy office or quiet GA cockpit) hearing protection is recommended for prolonged exposure. At 85 dB (the OSH action threshold in many jurisdictions) protection becomes mandatory for sustained exposure. From 90 dB upward (the inside of most piston cockpits in cruise) you need passive headsets or earplugs as a minimum. From around 100 dB (light helicopter cabins, propellers nearby) you want active noise-cancelling headsets or doubled-up protection. From 110 dB up (running radial engines, ramp environments near jets) you need both earplugs AND a headset or earmuffs. The numbers matter because hearing damage from noise is cumulative and permanent.
exam_tip: Rough scale: 80 dB recommended, 85 dB action threshold, 90 dB headset needed, 100 dB ANR or doubled, 110 dB earplugs + headset both. Damage is cumulative and permanent.
key_facts: 80 dB: recommended protection
85 dB: action threshold (mandatory in most workplaces)
90 dB+: passive headset or plugs needed
100 dB+: ANR headset or doubled protection
110 dB+: earplugs AND headset
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Specify noise levels at which hearing damage may occur
concept: Hearing damage starts to become a real risk around 85 dB for prolonged exposure (8 hours), and the safe exposure time roughly halves for every 3 dB increase. So 88 dB = 4 hours, 91 dB = 2 hours, 94 dB = 1 hour, and so on. Above 115 dB even brief exposure is damaging, and a single exposure to 140 dB or higher can cause immediate permanent damage. A typical piston GA cockpit runs around 90–100 dB in cruise, well above the safe limits, which is why headsets aren't optional. Helicopters and turbine aircraft are usually quieter inside but can have much louder ramp environments outside.
exam_tip: 85 dB for 8 hours is the rough safe limit. Each +3 dB halves the safe exposure time. Above 115 dB damage is rapid; above 140 dB damage is instant. GA cockpits typically 90–100 dB.
key_facts: 85 dB / 8 hours is the standard safe limit
Safe exposure halves for every +3 dB
115 dB causes rapid damage
140 dB causes instant permanent damage
GA piston cockpits typically run 90–100 dB
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe what is meant by the action threshold for hearing protection
concept: The action threshold for hearing protection is the noise level at which an employer or operator is required to take active steps to protect workers' hearing — providing protection, monitoring exposure, and offering hearing testing. In most jurisdictions including New Zealand the action threshold is 85 dB averaged over an 8-hour day. Below this level, protection is recommended but not legally mandated. At or above it, the operator is required to act. For aviation operators this means most cockpit and ramp environments fall above the action threshold, so headsets, earplugs and audiometric testing become standard parts of the safety system.
exam_tip: Action threshold = 85 dB over 8 hours. Above this, protection is mandatory and the operator must act. Below it, protection is recommended only.
key_facts: Action threshold is 85 dB averaged over 8 hours
Triggers mandatory protection and monitoring
NZ adopts the same threshold as most international standards
Most cockpit and ramp environments exceed it
Drives audiometric testing programmes for crews
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the factors which affect an individual's susceptibility to disorientation
concept: Susceptibility to spatial disorientation varies between individuals based on a few factors. Experience and currency are the biggest — pilots who fly regularly on instruments are far more resistant to disorientation than those who don't. Fatigue, illness, alcohol, and stress all dramatically increase susceptibility. Inner ear infections, head colds and recent head trauma can affect the vestibular system directly. Age plays a role — older pilots may take longer to recover from disorientating manoeuvres. Personality factors like complacency and overconfidence push pilots into situations they can't handle. Even something as simple as a recent meal or dehydration can affect tolerance. The key takeaway is that disorientation can happen to any pilot, but currency and good health lower the risk.
exam_tip: Susceptibility factors: experience/currency, fatigue, illness, alcohol, stress, inner ear problems, age, complacency, dehydration. Currency on instruments is the biggest protective factor.
key_facts: Currency on instruments is the strongest protective factor
Fatigue, illness, alcohol all increase susceptibility
Inner ear infections directly affect vestibular system
Age slows recovery from disorientating inputs
Complacency leads pilots into situations beyond their capability
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of gastrointestinal problems
concept: Gastrointestinal problems in flight present as cramps, nausea, urgent need for the toilet, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, sweating, and a general feeling of illness. The danger isn't just the discomfort — pilot incapacitation from a severe gastro episode is a recognised cause of accidents. Symptoms can come on fast and the pilot may go from feeling 'a bit off' to severely incapacitated in minutes. The classic scenario is food poisoning from a meal eaten before flight. NZ guidance is unambiguous — if you're feeling gastrointestinal symptoms before flight, don't fly; if they come on in flight, declare an issue, get on the ground at the nearest suitable aerodrome, and don't try to push through.
exam_tip: GI symptoms: cramps, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, sweating, abdominal pain. Onset can be rapid. Don't fly if symptoms present; land if they appear in flight.
key_facts: Symptoms include cramps, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, sweating
Rapid onset is common
Recognised cause of pilot incapacitation accidents
Don't fly if symptoms present pre-flight
Land at nearest suitable aerodrome if onset in flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Identify the primary causes of food poisoning
concept: Food poisoning in pilots usually traces back to one of the same handful of culprits as in the general population — poorly handled poultry (salmonella, campylobacter), undercooked seafood, raw or lightly cooked eggs, dairy products that have been temperature-abused, reheated rice (Bacillus cereus), and food prepared by people with poor hand hygiene. The aviation-specific advice is that crew members on the same flight should ideally eat different meals from different sources to reduce the chance that both pilots are taken out by the same dodgy chicken sandwich. Avoid risky foods (raw seafood, undercooked meats, salads of unknown provenance) before and during flying duties, and stay well-hydrated with bottled or known-safe water.
exam_tip: Common causes: poultry, seafood, eggs, dairy, reheated rice, poor hygiene. Crew should not eat the same meals from the same source. Avoid risky foods before flight.
key_facts: Poultry and seafood are common culprits (salmonella, campylobacter)
Reheated rice can carry Bacillus cereus
Crew should eat different meals from different sources
Avoid raw or undercooked items before flight
Hand hygiene of food preparers is a major factor
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: compressed gases
concept: Compressed gases in the aviation environment include oxygen cylinders, nitrogen for tyres and shock struts, and various refrigerants and CO2 systems. The hazards come from two directions — the gas itself and the pressure in the cylinder. A ruptured high-pressure cylinder is essentially a missile and can punch holes in aircraft and people. The gases themselves can displace oxygen from the air (any inert gas can asphyxiate you in a confined space), or cause cold burns from rapid expansion at the valve, or in the case of oxygen, support combustion violently. Symptoms of asphyxiation creep in like hypoxia — confusion, dizziness, eventual unconsciousness. Treatment is to get the person to fresh air immediately, apply oxygen if available, and call medical help. Cold burns are treated by warming the affected area gently, never with hot water.
exam_tip: Compressed gases: cylinder rupture, asphyxiation in confined spaces, cold burns from expansion, oxygen supports combustion. Treat asphyxiation with fresh air; treat cold burns with gentle warming.
key_facts: Ruptured high-pressure cylinders can become missiles
Inert gases can asphyxiate in confined spaces
Rapid expansion at the valve causes cold burns
Oxygen supports violent combustion
Treatment: fresh air for asphyxiation, gentle warming for cold burns
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: liquid oxygen
concept: Liquid oxygen (LOX) is oxygen stored at extremely low temperatures (around −183°C) as a liquid for compact storage. It's used in some military and high-altitude operations. The hazards are severe — direct skin contact causes immediate severe cold burns (frostbite-like injury), the vapour above the liquid is enough to support violent combustion of materials that wouldn't normally burn (greases, oils, even some metals), and any organic material soaked in LOX becomes a powerful explosive. Symptoms of contact are immediate intense pain and tissue damage that resembles a burn. Treatment is to immerse the affected area in tepid (not hot) water and seek immediate medical attention. Spilled LOX should be allowed to evaporate in a well-ventilated area away from any ignition source or organic material.
exam_tip: LOX = liquid oxygen at −183°C. Causes severe cold burns on contact, makes combustibles explosive, violently supports combustion. Treat burns with tepid water, get medical help immediately.
key_facts: Stored at approximately −183°C
Immediate severe cold burns on skin contact
Soaked organic material becomes explosive
Supports violent combustion of normally non-flammable materials
Treatment: tepid water immersion + immediate medical care
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms, effects and immediate treatments for the following hazards present in the aviation environment: de-icing fluids
concept: De-icing fluids used on aircraft (Type I, II, III, IV) are mostly glycol-based — propylene or ethylene glycol with water and additives. Skin contact usually just causes mild irritation, but eye contact causes more serious irritation and the fluid can damage the cornea if not flushed out promptly. Inhalation of mist from spray application can irritate the airways, and ingestion (ethylene glycol particularly) is highly toxic — it metabolises to compounds that damage kidneys and cause metabolic acidosis. Symptoms of significant exposure include eye/skin irritation, headache, nausea, dizziness. Treatment is to flush eyes with copious water for at least 15 minutes, wash skin thoroughly, move to fresh air for inhalation, and seek medical attention immediately for any ingestion. Ramp staff need PPE during de-icing operations.
exam_tip: De-icing fluids: glycol-based, irritating to skin and eyes, toxic if ingested (especially ethylene glycol). Flush eyes 15 min, wash skin, fresh air for inhalation, medical attention for ingestion.
key_facts: Glycol-based — propylene or ethylene
Skin and eye irritation common
Inhalation of spray mist irritates airways
Ingestion is highly toxic — kidney damage
Ramp staff require appropriate PPE
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Identify and give examples of physical, environmental, task-related, organisational and psychological stressors
concept: Stressors in aviation come in five flavours. Physical stressors are things like heat, cold, noise, vibration, G-forces, dehydration, and fatigue — anything bothering your body. Environmental stressors include altitude, low humidity, turbulence, weather, glare, and lighting conditions. Task-related stressors are workload spikes, time pressure, complex procedures, equipment failures, and difficult ATC instructions. Organisational stressors come from the company side — rosters, commercial pressure, management style, training shortfalls, and the company safety culture (or lack of it). Psychological stressors are personal — relationship problems, financial worries, family illness, bereavement, anxiety, and the everyday baggage you bring to the flight deck. Recognising which category a stressor falls into helps you decide whether you can fix it or whether you need to manage your response to it.
exam_tip: Five categories: physical, environmental, task-related, organisational, psychological. Memorise the categories with one example of each. Identify which type so you can decide whether to fix it or manage your response.
key_facts: Physical: heat, cold, noise, vibration, fatigue, G-force
Environmental: altitude, humidity, turbulence, glare
Task-related: workload, time pressure, equipment failures
Organisational: rosters, commercial pressure, safety culture
Psychological: personal worries, relationships, anxiety, finances
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of stress on attention, motivation and performance
concept: Stress affects attention, motivation and performance in fairly predictable ways. Mild stress sharpens attention and improves performance — this is the 'inverted U' or Yerkes-Dodson curve, and it's why pilots often perform best when slightly pressured. As stress increases past the optimum, attention narrows (tunnelling), peripheral cues are missed, working memory degrades, motivation initially climbs but then crashes when the situation feels hopeless, and performance drops sharply. At very high stress levels you get fixation, regression to overlearned behaviours (sometimes the wrong ones), freezing, or panic. The curve is individual — some pilots thrive at higher stress levels than others — but the basic shape applies to everyone. Recognising your own position on the curve is part of self-management.
exam_tip: Yerkes-Dodson inverted U: mild stress improves performance, excess stress crashes it. Effects: tunnelling, missed cues, fixation, regression, freezing or panic at the top end.
key_facts: Inverted U / Yerkes-Dodson curve relates stress to performance
Mild stress sharpens attention and motivation
Excess stress narrows attention (tunnelling)
Working memory degrades under high stress
Extreme stress causes fixation, regression, freezing or panic
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the stages of sleep
concept: Sleep happens in cycles of about 90 minutes, each cycle moving through several stages. Stage 1 is light dozing — easy to wake from, lasts a few minutes. Stage 2 is true sleep but still relatively light, with most of total sleep time spent here over a night. Stages 3 and 4 are deep slow-wave sleep — physically restorative, the stages where you really recover from physical fatigue, and the hardest to wake from. Then comes REM (rapid eye movement) sleep, where most dreaming happens — mentally restorative, important for memory consolidation, and characterised by paradoxical brain activity with muscle paralysis. Each cycle includes all stages, with REM periods getting longer as the night goes on. A full healthy night's sleep is about 4–5 complete cycles totalling 7–9 hours.
exam_tip: Sleep cycle is ~90 min: Stage 1 dozing → Stage 2 light sleep → Stages 3/4 deep slow-wave (physical recovery) → REM (mental recovery, dreaming). 4–5 cycles per night, 7–9 hours total.
key_facts: Sleep cycle is approximately 90 minutes
Stages 1–2 are light sleep
Stages 3–4 are deep slow-wave sleep — physical recovery
REM sleep is mentally restorative and includes most dreaming
4–5 cycles totalling 7–9 hours is a healthy night
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe the mechanism of sleep regulation
concept: Sleep is regulated by two interacting processes. The first is the circadian rhythm — your internal body clock, roughly 24 hours long, driven by exposure to light and darkness via the suprachiasmatic nucleus in the brain. It's what makes you feel sleepy at night and alert during the day, and what makes early morning starts and late night flights so difficult. The second is the homeostatic drive — sleep pressure that builds up the longer you've been awake. The two processes normally line up so you feel maximum sleepiness in the early hours of the morning. They get out of sync when you cross time zones, work shifts, or stay up late, and that's when sleep problems and fatigue accidents start. Light exposure (sunlight or even bright artificial light) is the strongest reset signal for the circadian clock.
exam_tip: Two systems: circadian rhythm (24-hour body clock, light-driven) and homeostatic drive (sleep pressure that builds with wakefulness). They normally align; jet lag and shift work pull them apart.
key_facts: Circadian rhythm is the 24-hour body clock
Driven by light/dark via suprachiasmatic nucleus
Homeostatic drive builds with hours awake
Two systems normally align for night-time sleep
Jet lag and shift work desynchronise the systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with sleep at abnormal times of the day
concept: Sleeping at the wrong time of day creates problems. Daytime sleep tends to be lighter, shorter, and less restorative because your circadian system is telling your body it should be awake — light, noise, and warm temperatures all work against you. You typically get less REM and less slow-wave sleep, so you wake feeling unrefreshed even after a full duration. Early-morning starts (3–5am wake-ups for a dawn departure) are especially brutal because they cut into the most restorative part of the sleep cycle. Multiple successive disrupted sleeps build sleep debt rapidly. The classic pilot scenario is a 0500 report after a 2200 dinner — you might lie down for 5 hours but you'll only get 3 or 4 hours of poor-quality sleep.
exam_tip: Daytime sleep = lighter, shorter, less REM and slow-wave, less restorative. Early-morning starts cut the most restorative sleep. Sleep debt builds quickly.
key_facts: Daytime sleep is lighter and shorter than night sleep
Less REM and slow-wave sleep means less recovery
Light, noise and temperature all work against daytime sleep
Early starts (3–5am) cut the most restorative sleep
Sleep debt accumulates quickly with disrupted sleep patterns
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by sleep debt
concept: Sleep debt is the cumulative shortfall between the sleep you need (for most adults, 7–9 hours per night) and the sleep you actually get. Lose an hour per night for a week and you've built up 7 hours of debt — equivalent to a complete missed night's sleep, with measurable effects on reaction time, judgement and mood. The debt doesn't go away on its own — you need extra recovery sleep over several nights to clear it, and even then, deep sleep stages get priority and other stages may stay shortchanged. Pilots running on chronic sleep debt are demonstrably slower, more error-prone, and worse at recognising their own impairment — much like alcohol intoxication. The only fix is enough sleep, regularly. There are no shortcuts, no matter what coffee adverts say.
exam_tip: Sleep debt = cumulative shortfall vs needed sleep. Builds up across nights, doesn't go away on its own, takes several nights of recovery to clear. Effects mimic alcohol intoxication.
key_facts: Cumulative shortfall against the 7–9 hour need
Builds up across consecutive nights of poor sleep
Recovery requires several nights of full sleep
Effects on reaction and judgement mimic alcohol intoxication
Pilots with sleep debt underestimate their own impairment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe methods of managing fatigue
concept: Managing fatigue is partly individual and partly organisational. As an individual you control your sleep hygiene — regular bedtime, dark and cool sleeping environment, no screens before sleep, limited caffeine and alcohol, exercise during the day, and protecting sleep periods from interruptions. Strategic naps before duty (20 minutes for a power nap or a full 90-minute cycle) help recovery. Caffeine helps acutely but doesn't replace sleep. On the organisational side, fatigue risk management systems set duty time limits, minimum rest periods, and maximum consecutive early starts or night duties. NZ Part 121 has prescriptive flight and duty time rules and Part 91 operators are also expected to manage fatigue. Self-assessment honesty is critical — if you're too tired to fly safely, you say so.
exam_tip: Individual: sleep hygiene, naps, limited caffeine/alcohol, exercise. Organisational: FRMS, FDP limits, rest periods. Honest self-assessment is essential. NZ rules in Part 91/121.
key_facts: Sleep hygiene: regular schedule, dark/cool environment, no screens
Strategic naps (20 min or 90 min) before duty aid recovery
Caffeine helps acutely but does not replace sleep
Fatigue risk management systems set duty/rest limits
Honest self-assessment is the critical individual safeguard
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe methods by which age-related changes in memory and speed of information processing can be moderated by older pilots
concept: Age-related changes in memory and information processing speed are normal and can be managed rather than reversed. Practical strategies include: writing things down (don't rely on memory for clearances or briefings), using checklists rigorously, maintaining currency through regular flying (use it or lose it applies strongly), allocating more planning time to compensate for slower processing, simplifying procedures where possible, and using cockpit organisation (pre-set frequencies, prepared paperwork) to reduce real-time workload. Continued mental and physical exercise, good sleep, social engagement, and managing health conditions (blood pressure, cholesterol, diabetes) all help preserve cognitive function. The DAME medical assessment also tracks for any signs of cognitive decline. Older pilots are not unsafe pilots — but they typically need to work a bit harder and be a bit more honest with themselves.
exam_tip: Manage age-related changes by: writing things down, rigorous checklist use, currency, more planning time, cockpit organisation. Stay mentally and physically active. DAME tracks cognitive health.
key_facts: Write things down — don't rely on memory
Rigorous checklist discipline
Maintain currency — use it or lose it
Allow more planning time
Mental and physical activity preserve cognitive function
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

topic: 53.201 -- Physiology and the Effects of Flight
syllabus_desc: Describe what changes would indicate early dementia or age-related cognitive impairment in another pilot
concept: Early signs of dementia or significant age-related cognitive impairment in another pilot include: forgetting recent events or briefings repeatedly, getting lost on familiar routes or in familiar aerodromes, repeated lapses in reading checklists, struggling with numbers or basic mental arithmetic that used to be easy, withdrawal from previously-enjoyed activities, mood changes (apathy, irritability, depression), confusion about time or place, repeating questions or stories, difficulty following multi-step instructions, and personality changes noticed by family or colleagues. The challenge is that many of these can have other causes (tiredness, stress, illness, depression), so a single incident isn't diagnostic — but a pattern of changes over weeks or months is a warning. If you spot it in a colleague, the kindest and safest thing is to have a quiet honest conversation and encourage a medical review. CAANZ requires any condition affecting safety to be reported to the DAME.
exam_tip: Early signs: memory lapses, repeated questions, getting lost, checklist errors, withdrawal, mood/personality changes. Pattern over time matters more than one bad day. Encourage colleague to see DAME.
key_facts: Repeated memory lapses about recent events
Getting lost on familiar routes
Withdrawal and mood/personality changes
Difficulty with numbers or multi-step instructions
Pattern over time is more significant than isolated incidents
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of mental workload
concept: Mental workload is the amount of cognitive effort you're putting in at any moment to deal with what's in front of you. It's a function of the difficulty of the task, the time you have to do it, your skill level, and how many things you're juggling at once. Mental workload isn't the same as physical workload — you can be sitting still in a quiet cockpit and still be mentally maxed out (think hand-flying a partial-panel ILS to minima in turbulence). The opposite is also true — workload that drops too low (a quiet cruise on autopilot) brings its own problems with attention and complacency. Effective IFR pilots learn to read their own workload and shed tasks when it climbs too high, by delegating to the autopilot, asking ATC for delays or vectors, or saying 'standby' instead of trying to handle everything at once.
exam_tip: Mental workload = cognitive effort needed for the task. Depends on task difficulty, time pressure, skill level, and number of competing tasks. High and low workload both have hazards.
key_facts: Cognitive effort to manage current tasks
Function of task difficulty, time pressure, skill, and multitasking load
Different from physical workload
Both very high and very low workload are problems
Managed by task shedding, automation, and asking for time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of overload
concept: Overload happens when the demands of the situation exceed your ability to handle them — your mental workload tips over the edge and you can't keep up. The classic signs are tunnel vision (focusing on one thing while ignoring others), missed radio calls, dropped checklist items, fixation, slow or erratic responses, breathing changes, and a feeling of falling behind the aircraft. Once a pilot is overloaded, performance drops sharply and errors multiply. The really dangerous bit is that overloaded pilots often don't recognise it — they just keep trying harder at the wrong things while the bigger picture falls apart. Recognising the early signs in yourself and your colleagues is one of the most important CRM skills you can develop.
exam_tip: Overload = workload exceeds capacity. Signs: tunnel vision, missed calls, dropped checklists, fixation, falling behind. Pilots in overload often don't recognise it themselves.
key_facts: Demand exceeds available capacity
Signs: tunnel vision, missed calls, fixation, falling behind
Performance drops sharply and errors multiply
Pilot often does not recognise their own overload
Early recognition is a key CRM skill
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe methods of managing potential overload
concept: Managing potential overload starts before the workload spikes. Plan ahead — brief approaches well before top of descent, set up frequencies and bugs in the cruise, keep paperwork organised. In flight, use the autopilot to reduce manual flying load when things get busy. Prioritise ruthlessly using aviate, navigate, communicate. Buy yourself time by asking ATC for delays, vectors, or holding rather than trying to do everything at once. Delegate to other crew members where you have them. Recognise the early signs of overload in yourself (rushed feeling, missed items, tunnel vision) and act on them — say 'standby', shed non-essential tasks, take a breath. Practice handling high-workload scenarios in the sim or with an instructor so the experience isn't entirely new in the real aircraft.
exam_tip: Manage overload by: planning ahead, using automation, prioritising aviate/navigate/communicate, asking for time, delegating, recognising early signs and shedding tasks. Practice in the sim.
key_facts: Plan ahead — brief and set up before workload spikes
Use autopilot to offload manual flying
Prioritise: aviate, navigate, communicate
Buy time from ATC with delays or vectors
Recognise early signs and shed non-essential tasks
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe and compare skill, rule and knowledge-based behaviours
concept: Rasmussen's framework describes three levels of behaviour. Skill-based is automatic and unconscious — things like keeping the wings level, or moving the throttle, that you do without thinking. Rule-based is following learned procedures or rules ('if this, then that') — like running an approach checklist or recognising a situation that calls for a particular memory item. Knowledge-based is conscious problem-solving from first principles — what you do when something happens that doesn't fit any rule you know, like a novel system failure with multiple cascading effects. Skill-based is fastest and least demanding, knowledge-based is slowest and most demanding. Experienced pilots operate mostly skill- and rule-based; the goal of training is to convert knowledge-based work into rule- and skill-based as you build experience.
exam_tip: Rasmussen: skill-based (automatic), rule-based (if-then procedures), knowledge-based (first-principles problem solving). Training moves novel tasks from knowledge → rule → skill.
key_facts: Skill-based = automatic, unconscious actions
Rule-based = applying learned if-then procedures
Knowledge-based = problem solving from first principles
Skill-based is fastest and least demanding
Knowledge-based is slowest and most error-prone
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the process of acquiring a skill
concept: Acquiring a skill is a four-stage process. First is unconscious incompetence — you don't even know what you don't know, and you may have no idea you're doing things badly. Then conscious incompetence — you've started learning and you can see clearly how much you can't do (this is the hard, frustrating phase). Then conscious competence — you can do the task but only by concentrating hard on every step. Finally unconscious competence — the skill has become automatic and you can do it without consciously thinking about it. The progression takes practice with feedback, and along the way you'll have setbacks. Once a skill is unconscious it lives in your muscle memory and procedural memory, but you have to keep using it or it decays. This is why currency and recency rules exist.
exam_tip: Four stages: unconscious incompetence → conscious incompetence → conscious competence → unconscious competence. Requires practice with feedback. Skills decay without use.
key_facts: Stage 1: unconscious incompetence
Stage 2: conscious incompetence (frustrating phase)
Stage 3: conscious competence (effortful)
Stage 4: unconscious competence (automatic)
Skills decay without regular practice
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe failures of skill, rule and knowledge-based behaviours
concept: Each level of behaviour fails in characteristic ways. Skill-based failures are typically slips and lapses — you knew what you wanted to do, but your hand or attention wandered. Selecting the wrong frequency, missing a checklist item, dialling 1200 instead of 1300 in the heading bug. They happen most often during routine tasks when attention is elsewhere. Rule-based failures are mistakes — applying the wrong rule, or applying the right rule wrongly. Misdiagnosing a situation and running the wrong drill. Knowledge-based failures are reasoning errors — getting the analysis wrong when you're trying to solve a novel problem. These are the worst kind because under stress your knowledge-based reasoning is often impaired exactly when you need it most. The key to avoiding failures is to know which level you're operating at.
exam_tip: Skill-based failures = slips and lapses (right plan, wrong execution). Rule-based failures = mistakes (wrong rule). Knowledge-based failures = reasoning errors (wrong analysis). Each fails differently.
key_facts: Skill-based: slips and lapses (right intent, wrong action)
Rule-based: applying the wrong rule or right rule wrongly
Knowledge-based: reasoning errors under novel conditions
Skill-based failures common during routine tasks with distraction
Knowledge-based failures worst under stress
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain confirmation bias
concept: Confirmation bias is the tendency to look for information that supports what you already believe and to ignore or downplay information that contradicts it. In aviation it's a serial killer. The classic case is a pilot who has decided the weather at the destination is fine, sees a deteriorating METAR, and rationalises it away ('that must be a temporary cell, the next one will be better'). Or a pilot who thinks the engine sound is normal, then ignores rising oil temperature because it doesn't fit the story. The defence is to actively look for evidence that contradicts your current plan and take it seriously when you find it. CRM training spends a lot of time on this because it's so insidious.
exam_tip: Confirmation bias = seeking evidence that supports your existing belief and ignoring contradictory evidence. Defend by actively looking for what could prove you wrong.
key_facts: Tendency to favour information confirming existing beliefs
Causes pilots to ignore deteriorating conditions or warnings
Common cause of weather-related and decision accidents
Defence: actively seek disconfirming evidence
Major topic in CRM training
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on perception: expectation
concept: Expectation strongly shapes perception — you tend to see and hear what you expect to see and hear, not necessarily what's actually there. The classic example is a clearance readback where the pilot reads back what they expected ATC to say rather than what was actually said. Or seeing a runway when you're tense about finding it (and getting fooled by a road or parallel strip). Or hearing your own callsign in someone else's call. Expectation isn't always wrong — it lets you process information faster — but when it's wrong, you may not even notice the mismatch. The defence is to deliberately listen for what's actually said, look for what's actually there, and verify against independent information when something doesn't fit.
exam_tip: Expectation makes you perceive what you expect, not what's there. Causes wrong readbacks, mistaken visual identification, hearing your callsign when it wasn't called. Verify against independent sources.
key_facts: Expectation biases what you see and hear
Common cause of incorrect readbacks
Can cause mistaken visual identification of runways or features
Pilots may not notice the mismatch
Defence: deliberate listening and independent verification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on perception: experience
concept: Experience also shapes perception, mostly for the better — experienced pilots see patterns, threats and opportunities that beginners simply don't notice. The textbook example is an experienced instructor who can tell from a student's first turn what's about to go wrong. But experience can also lead pilots astray — if every previous flight to a destination has been fine, you may downplay warning signs because they don't fit your experience of that place. Expert pilots make better decisions on average but they can also become complacent or overconfident. The trick is to use experience to recognise patterns while staying open to the possibility that this flight, this day, is different.
exam_tip: Experience helps you see patterns and threats faster but can lead to complacency and overconfidence. Use it to recognise patterns while staying open to new threats.
key_facts: Experience improves pattern recognition and threat detection
Experts perceive things novices miss
Experience can also lead to complacency
Familiarity bias: 'it's always been fine before'
Best pilots use experience without becoming overconfident
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation of mental models
concept: A mental model is the picture you carry in your head of what's happening — where you are, what the aircraft is doing, what's going to happen next, and what the threats are. Building one starts with gathering information from instruments, ATC, weather, charts and your own senses, then organising it into a coherent picture, then continuously updating it as new information comes in. A good mental model lets you anticipate, plan, and react quickly because you understand what's going on. A bad or outdated mental model leads to surprise and bad decisions. The job of every IFR pilot is to keep their mental model accurate — and to know the difference between the model and reality. When you find yourself surprised, that's a sign your model is wrong, and you need to rebuild it from scratch rather than try to patch it.
exam_tip: Mental model = your picture of what's happening. Build by gathering info, organising, and continuously updating. Surprise = your model is wrong; rebuild rather than patch.
key_facts: Internal picture of current and future situation
Built from instruments, ATC, charts, weather and senses
Continuously updated as new information arrives
Good models enable anticipation and quick response
Surprise indicates the model is wrong and needs rebuilding
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the special perceptual problems associated with transitioning from IMC to VMC off an instrument approach
concept: Transitioning from IMC to VMC at the end of an instrument approach has its own perceptual traps. Your eyes have been focused on the panel for the last 20 minutes — accommodation is locked in close, and your night/dim adaptation may be different from outside conditions. When you look up and try to acquire the runway, depth perception can be poor, distances misjudged, and you may not see the runway environment until it's closer than you think. Visual illusions like black-hole approach (no terrain features around the runway), runway slope illusions, and width illusions all become possible. There's also a workload spike — you have to maintain your instrument scan as a backup until you're confident the visual picture is accurate. Practice the transition deliberately, plan for the workload, and be ready to go around if the visual picture isn't what you expected.
exam_tip: IMC-to-VMC transition: accommodation lag, depth perception issues, visual illusions (black hole, slope, width), workload spike. Keep instrument scan as backup. Be ready to go around.
key_facts: Eye accommodation is locked close after instrument scan
Depth perception can be poor when looking up
Black-hole, slope and width illusions are common at this point
Workload spikes during the transition
Maintain instrument scan as backup until visual confirmed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between crew resource management (CRM) and the building of situational awareness by pilots
concept: Crew resource management (CRM) is the structured use of all available resources — people, equipment, information — to achieve safe and efficient flight. Situational awareness (SA) is your accurate perception of what's happening around you and what's likely to happen next. CRM directly supports SA by getting more information into the picture: cross-checks, briefings, calls, queries, sharing observations, and using the second pilot or ATC as an extra set of eyes. Without CRM, SA depends entirely on one person's observations, biases and attention. With good CRM, SA is built and verified by multiple people who can catch each other's errors. Single-pilot IFR is the hardest because there's no second pilot to share the SA-building load — that's why single-pilot IFR pilots use the autopilot, ATC, and structured personal habits to compensate.
exam_tip: CRM uses all available resources to build and maintain situational awareness. Multiple people cross-checking each other reduces errors. Single-pilot IFR has to compensate using automation and ATC.
key_facts: CRM uses people, equipment and information together
Situational awareness is the picture of current and future state
CRM supports SA by adding cross-checks and verification
Multiple people catch each other's errors
Single-pilot IFR compensates with automation and ATC support
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify risk assessment techniques
concept: Risk assessment techniques in aviation range from informal mental checks to structured tools. Common techniques include the PAVE checklist (Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures), the IMSAFE personal check (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Eating), the 5P model (Plan, Plane, Pilot, Passengers, Programming), risk matrices that score likelihood against consequence to give a risk score, formal hazard logs maintained as part of an SMS, and Threat and Error Management (TEM) which identifies threats before flight and tracks how they're being managed. The goal of any technique is to make implicit risks explicit so you can decide whether to accept, mitigate, transfer or avoid them. Good operators use multiple techniques layered together rather than relying on one.
exam_tip: Common techniques: PAVE, IMSAFE, 5P, risk matrices, hazard logs, TEM. The goal is to make implicit risks explicit. Good operators layer multiple techniques.
key_facts: PAVE: Pilot, Aircraft, enVironment, External pressures
IMSAFE: Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Eating
5P: Plan, Plane, Pilot, Passengers, Programming
Risk matrices score likelihood vs consequence
TEM: Threat and Error Management
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify risk levels that compromise safety
concept: Risk levels that compromise safety are those where the combination of likelihood and severity passes a threshold the operator (or pilot) considers acceptable. On a typical risk matrix, a risk score in the red zone (high likelihood + severe consequence) is unacceptable and requires either elimination of the hazard or a stop to the activity. Yellow-zone risks need active mitigation before proceeding. Green-zone risks are tolerable as is, with monitoring. The key idea is that 'safety' isn't a binary but a continuum, and the operator decides where the line sits — typically that line is set conservatively so there's margin for the things you didn't think of. A pilot who finds themselves arguing why a yellow or red risk is actually fine is usually the pilot in the next accident report.
exam_tip: Risk levels: green (tolerable, monitor), yellow (mitigate before proceeding), red (unacceptable, stop or eliminate hazard). Safety is a continuum, not a binary. Conservative thresholds give margin.
key_facts: Risk = likelihood × consequence
Green: tolerable with monitoring
Yellow: requires active mitigation
Red: unacceptable, must be eliminated or activity stopped
Conservative thresholds protect against unknown unknowns
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify situations where time pressure compromises safety or increases risk levels
concept: Time pressure compromises safety in lots of recognisable scenarios — pushing on through deteriorating weather to make a scheduled arrival, rushing a pre-flight inspection because of a tight turnaround, skipping briefings to save five minutes, accepting a clearance you haven't fully understood because you don't want to ask for a repeat, or descending faster than briefed to make an approach window. Time pressure can be commercial (the company expects an on-time arrival), personal (a meeting, a family event, a dinner reservation), or self-imposed (you committed to a friend you'd be there). It pushes pilots to skip steps, accept higher risk, and rationalise away warning signs. The single most useful question to ask yourself when you feel rushed is: 'what would I do if I had unlimited time?' — and then do that.
exam_tip: Time pressure causes skipped steps, rushed inspections, deteriorating weather acceptance, missed briefings. Sources: commercial, personal, self-imposed. Ask: 'what would I do with unlimited time?'
key_facts: Pressure to push on through bad weather
Rushed pre-flight inspections and briefings
Acceptance of misunderstood clearances
Sources: commercial, personal, self-imposed
Ask yourself what you'd do with unlimited time
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Define cognitive dissonance
concept: Cognitive dissonance is the mental discomfort you feel when you hold two conflicting beliefs or when your actions contradict your beliefs. Pilots resolve this discomfort either by changing their beliefs (rare and hard) or by rationalising their actions to match the situation. The classic example is a pilot who believes 'I'm a safe pilot' but who's also pressing on into deteriorating weather. The dissonance is resolved by rationalising: 'this isn't really that bad', 'I've flown in worse', 'it'll improve in 20 minutes'. The rationalisation feels true at the time but it isn't — it's the brain's way of relieving the discomfort, not assessing the actual risk. Recognising cognitive dissonance in yourself is hard but important — when you find yourself constructing arguments for why a marginal decision is actually fine, that's the dissonance talking, and that's when you should take the dissonance seriously and reconsider.
exam_tip: Cognitive dissonance = mental discomfort from conflicting beliefs or beliefs vs actions. Pilots usually resolve it by rationalising rather than changing behaviour. Watch for it when justifying marginal decisions.
key_facts: Mental discomfort from holding conflicting beliefs
Or from actions that contradict beliefs
Usually resolved by rationalisation rather than behaviour change
Common in continued VFR into IMC scenarios
Recognising it in yourself is the defence
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the following personality traits and explain their effect on group decision making: introversion
concept: Introversion is a personality trait characterised by drawing energy from solitude, preferring deeper one-on-one conversations to large groups, processing thoughts internally before speaking, and being more reserved in unfamiliar social situations. Introverts are not shy or antisocial — they just have a different relationship with social stimulation. In a group decision-making setting, introverts may take longer to contribute, may be reluctant to speak up against a dominant voice, and may prefer to think through options privately before voicing them. The risk in a cockpit is that an introverted FO with important safety information may not assert it firmly enough against an extroverted captain. Good CRM training teaches both sides — captains to actively invite input, FOs to speak up clearly even when it's uncomfortable.
exam_tip: Introversion = energy from solitude, internal processing, reserved in groups. Risk: introverted crew may not assert safety concerns strongly enough. CRM teaches both invitation and assertion.
key_facts: Energy drawn from solitude rather than social stimulation
Internal processing before speaking
Not the same as shyness or social anxiety
May be reluctant to assert against dominant voices
CRM addresses with assertion training and invitation skills
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the following personality traits and explain their effect on group decision making: extraversion
concept: Extraversion is the opposite — drawing energy from social interaction, thinking out loud, comfortable in groups and unfamiliar situations, and willing to assert oneself in conversation. Extraverts contribute readily to group discussions, but they may dominate conversations, talk over quieter colleagues, decide quickly without fully processing input, and underweight ideas that come from less assertive crew members. In the cockpit an extraverted captain may steamroll a quieter FO and miss important information. Good CRM teaches extraverts to actively pause, invite input, and listen carefully — and to be wary of their own tendency to fill silence with their own voice.
exam_tip: Extraversion = energy from interaction, talkative, assertive, decisive. Risk: may steamroll quieter crew and miss important input. CRM teaches active listening and inviting input.
key_facts: Energy drawn from social interaction
Thinks out loud, comfortable asserting in groups
Decisive but may decide before processing all input
May dominate conversation and miss quieter voices
CRM teaches active listening and pausing for input
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the following personality traits and explain their effect on group decision making: anxiety
concept: Anxiety as a personality trait describes individuals who tend to worry, anticipate problems, feel uncertainty intensely, and have heightened arousal in challenging situations. A moderate amount of trait anxiety can actually be useful in aviation — anxious pilots tend to plan more thoroughly and check more carefully. But high trait anxiety becomes a problem in group decision-making because anxious pilots may second-guess themselves, defer too readily to others, or fail to assert their concerns. They may also become risk-averse to the point of indecision, or conversely, may make rushed decisions to escape the discomfort of uncertainty. Good CRM acknowledges that everyone has different baseline anxiety levels and teaches techniques to manage anxious moments without letting them dominate the decision process.
exam_tip: Anxiety trait = tendency to worry, anticipate problems, feel uncertainty strongly. Moderate anxiety useful (more checking); high anxiety harmful (indecision, deference, rushed decisions).
key_facts: Tendency to worry and anticipate problems
Heightened arousal under uncertainty
Moderate anxiety supports thorough planning
High anxiety leads to indecision or rushed escape decisions
CRM teaches techniques for managing anxious moments
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe a basic model of communications
concept: A basic communication model has a sender, a message, an encoding process (turning thoughts into words), a transmission medium (voice, radio, written), a decoding process (turning words back into thoughts), a receiver, and feedback (the receiver acknowledges what they understood). At every step there's potential for error — the sender encodes badly, the medium introduces noise, the receiver decodes incorrectly, or the feedback loop is missed. The whole point of standard phraseology, readbacks, and structured briefings in aviation is to make this loop more reliable by reducing the variability at each step. When the loop breaks down — sender unclear, medium noisy, receiver distracted, feedback missed — communication errors happen, and most communication-related accidents trace back to one of these failures.
exam_tip: Communication model: sender → encoding → medium → decoding → receiver → feedback. Errors can occur at any step. Standard phraseology and readbacks make the loop more reliable.
key_facts: Components: sender, message, encoding, medium, decoding, receiver, feedback
Errors possible at every step
Feedback loop closes the communication
Standard phraseology reduces variability
Most communication accidents trace to a broken step in the loop
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the barriers to effective communication
concept: Barriers to effective communication include language differences (English as a second language, regional accents, jargon), physical noise (cockpit noise, radio static, headset issues), psychological factors (stress, fatigue, distraction, emotional state), assumptions (sender or receiver assumes the other knows something they don't), workload spikes (no spare capacity to listen), interruptions, hierarchy and authority gradients (FO hesitant to speak up to captain), expectation (hearing what you expect rather than what's said), cultural differences in communication style, and pure information overload. Each of these can break down the model in different ways. In the cockpit, multiple barriers often stack up at once during exactly the moments when communication matters most.
exam_tip: Barriers: language, noise, stress/fatigue, assumptions, workload, hierarchy, expectation, culture, overload. Multiple barriers often stack up at the worst moments.
key_facts: Language differences (ESL, accents, jargon)
Physical noise in cockpit and radio
Stress, fatigue and distraction
Hierarchy and authority gradients
Assumptions and expectations distort messages
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify techniques to reduce communication barriers
concept: Techniques to reduce communication barriers start with using standard phraseology so words have the same meaning to everyone. Beyond that: speak clearly and at a measured pace, use the receiver's name or callsign to focus their attention, structure messages logically (most important information first), confirm receipt with a readback, ask for repeats when anything is unclear, reduce background noise where possible, use a sterile cockpit during critical phases, address authority gradients explicitly through assertion training, and check understanding by asking the receiver to restate the key points in their own words. In multi-crew cockpits, clear assignment of who's flying and who's communicating prevents stepped-on transmissions. Above all, treat communication as a closed loop, not a broadcast.
exam_tip: Reduce barriers: standard phraseology, clear measured speech, named addressing, logical structure, readbacks, requesting repeats, sterile cockpit, assertion training, closed-loop confirmation.
key_facts: Standard phraseology gives words common meaning
Speak clearly at measured pace
Closed-loop communication with readbacks
Sterile cockpit reduces distractions in critical phases
Address authority gradients through assertion training
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: read backs
concept: Read backs close the communication loop — the receiver repeats the key elements of the message back to the sender so that any error can be caught immediately. ATC clearances are read back in full (route, altitude, frequency, squawk where relevant) so that controller and pilot have the same picture. The discipline matters — a quick mumbled readback that omits the altitude is worse than useless because it gives a false sense of agreement. NZ AIP and CAR Part 91 specify what must be read back. The most common readback errors are altitude and heading — exactly the parameters that cause most errors of misunderstanding — which is why both controllers and pilots are trained to listen carefully for the readback and challenge anything that doesn't match.
exam_tip: Readbacks close the communication loop. Read back altitude, heading, frequency, route, squawk in full. Sloppy readbacks are worse than none — they give false confidence.
key_facts: Receiver repeats key elements back to sender
Catches errors before they propagate
Required by NZ AIP and CAR Part 91 for key clearances
Most common errors: altitude and heading
Sloppy or partial readbacks are worse than useless
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: standard phraseology
concept: Standard phraseology is the use of agreed words and phrases with fixed meanings so that everyone in the system understands the same thing by them. ICAO publishes the standard phraseology used internationally and NZ AIP specifies the local variations. Examples: 'cleared to land', 'line up and wait', 'maintain', 'climb', 'descend', 'standby'. Each has a precise meaning. The reason it matters is that natural language is ambiguous — 'taking off' might mean anything to a non-aviation listener, but in the cockpit it has a very specific meaning that the famous Tenerife disaster showed has to be unambiguous. Use the standard words even when they feel stilted; don't paraphrase, don't improvise.
exam_tip: Standard phraseology = agreed words with fixed meanings. ICAO international standards plus NZ AIP local variants. Don't paraphrase. Tenerife is the classic example of why it matters.
key_facts: Agreed words with precise fixed meanings
ICAO publishes international standards
NZ AIP specifies local variations
Don't paraphrase or improvise
Tenerife disaster traces to non-standard phraseology
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: standard calls
concept: Standard calls are pre-defined verbal callouts that crew members make at fixed points in the flight — examples include '80 knots' on takeoff, 'V1', 'rotate', 'positive rate', 'gear up', '1,000 to go', 'one thousand', 'minimums', 'go around'. They keep both crew members in the same mental model and trigger expected responses from the other crew member. In single-pilot operations the calls are often made aloud to yourself to reinforce the action and create a structured mental rhythm.
