The discipline of making the calls every time, even when the situation feels routine, is what builds the habit that protects you when the situation isn't routine. Companies publish standard call lists in their operations manual.
exam_tip: Standard calls = predefined verbal callouts at fixed points. Examples: V1, rotate, gear up, 1000 to go, minimums, go around. Keep crew in same mental model. Make them every time.
key_facts: Predefined verbal callouts at fixed flight points
Keep both crew in the same mental model
Trigger expected responses from other crew
Used by single pilots aloud to themselves
Build habit that protects in non-routine situations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: cross-checks
concept: Cross-checks are deliberate verifications where one crew member checks something another crew member did, or checks one source of information against another. Examples: PF flies the heading, PM checks the heading bug matches the cleared heading; one pilot reads the checklist, the other confirms each item; pilot loads a flight plan into the GPS, copilot verifies it against the paper flight plan. Cross-checks catch slips and lapses that the original actor wouldn't catch on their own. They're effective because the second person doesn't share the assumptions and biases of the first. Single-pilot operators do something similar by deliberately verifying their own work against an independent source — paper plan against GPS, written altitude against altimeter, clock against ETA.
exam_tip: Cross-checks = one crew member or independent source verifies another's action. Catches slips and lapses by removing shared assumptions. Single-pilots verify against independent sources.
key_facts: One crew checks another's actions
Or one source is verified against another
Catches slips and lapses the original actor would miss
Effective because second checker doesn't share assumptions
Single-pilot equivalent: verify against independent sources
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: document verification checks
concept: Document verification checks confirm that the information you're using matches what's actually current and correct — flight plan vs filed plan, weight and balance vs actual loading, charts in date, NOTAMs reviewed, performance figures recalculated for actual conditions, fuel quantity vs planned figure, and so on. The point is to catch out-of-date or wrong information before it causes a problem in flight. The classic accident example is a chart that's a revision out of date and shows the wrong frequency, or a weight calculation done from yesterday's load sheet. Make these checks part of every pre-flight, and double-check anything that involves numbers transferred from one document to another — that's where errors creep in.
exam_tip: Document verification = confirm flight documents are current, correct and consistent. Charts in date, NOTAMs reviewed, W&B vs actual load, performance for actual conditions, fuel matches plan.
key_facts: Verify documents are current and correct
Check W&B against actual load
Confirm charts and AIP supplements in date
Review NOTAMs
Particularly check numbers transferred between documents
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: display and control setting checks
concept: Display and control setting checks verify that the systems are set the way you intended. Examples: altimeter set to current QNH, heading bug on cleared heading, transponder code matches cleared squawk, autopilot mode matches the phase of flight, navigation source selected correctly (GPS vs VOR vs ILS), comm frequencies set to active and standby, fuel selector on correct tank, mixture and prop settings matching the procedure. The check has two parts: did I do what I meant to do, and did the system actually receive it? It's surprisingly common for a pilot to twist the heading bug and the system to register the wrong value, or to think the autopilot is in altitude hold when it's actually in vertical speed mode. Touch, look, verify.
exam_tip: Display/control setting checks = verify systems are set the way you intended. QNH, heading bug, squawk, autopilot mode, nav source, fuel selector. Touch, look, verify.
key_facts: Verify systems are set as intended
Check QNH, heading bug, squawk, autopilot mode
Confirm nav source and frequency selection
Catches mode confusion and silent setting errors
Touch the control, look at the display, verify the result
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the following strategies used to reduce communication errors in aviation: sterile cockpit policies
concept: Sterile cockpit policies restrict non-essential conversation and activities during critical phases of flight — typically below 10,000 ft on climb and descent, during taxi, takeoff, approach and landing. The rule is that nothing is discussed except things directly related to the operation of the aircraft. The reasons are obvious: workload is highest in these phases, distractions cause errors, and most accidents happen close to the ground. The rule was introduced after several accidents where flight crews missed approach calls or runway incursions because they were chatting about non-flight matters. NZ Part 121 operators have explicit sterile cockpit procedures; smaller operators often adopt similar discipline informally. Single pilots do the same by mentally focusing on the operation and avoiding non-essential mental tangents during critical phases.
exam_tip: Sterile cockpit = no non-essential conversation or activity below 10,000 ft or during critical phases. Reduces distraction errors. Mandatory under Part 121 and good practice elsewhere.
key_facts: No non-essential talk or activity below ~10,000 ft
Applies during taxi, takeoff, climb, descent, approach, landing
Introduced after accidents involving cockpit distraction
NZ Part 121 has explicit sterile cockpit procedures
Single pilots apply the principle individually
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe and identify examples of overt/active and latent threats
concept: Threats to aviation safety come in two flavours. Overt or active threats are immediate, obvious and right in front of you — a thunderstorm on the radar, a system failure annunciator, an ATC call you didn't expect, a bird crossing the windscreen. They demand immediate action and you can see them coming. Latent threats are hidden in the background, lying in wait until conditions activate them — a procedure that's been written ambiguously, a maintenance shortcut taken weeks ago, a training gap, an organisational pressure to push schedules, a fatiguing roster pattern. Latent threats are dangerous because nobody notices them until they combine with an active situation to cause an accident. James Reason's swiss cheese model is built on this distinction. Good safety management surfaces and addresses both kinds.
exam_tip: Overt/active threats = immediate, visible, demand action now. Latent threats = hidden in the background, waiting to combine with other factors. Reason model is built on this distinction.
key_facts: Overt/active threats: immediate and visible
Latent threats: hidden in procedures, training, culture
Latent threats activate when combined with active conditions
Both are addressed by safety management systems
Reason swiss cheese model formalises the distinction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify methods and means for detecting error in the aviation system
concept: Errors get caught by a combination of system features and human attention. Methods include: cross-checks between crew members, automated alerting (TCAS, GPWS, configuration warnings), checklist discipline, ATC monitoring and challenges, standard callouts that trigger expected responses, post-flight debriefs that review what went well and what didn't, hazard reporting systems that surface near-miss errors, FOQA / flight data monitoring programmes that detect parameter exceedances, line operations safety audits (LOSA), and structured incident investigation processes. The principle is that errors will happen no matter how good the training is, so the system has to be designed to catch them before they cause harm.
exam_tip: Detection methods: crew cross-checks, automated alerts (TCAS, GPWS), checklists, ATC, callouts, debriefs, hazard reports, FOQA, LOSA, incident investigation. Errors will happen — system catches them.
key_facts: Cross-checks between crew members
Automated alerts (TCAS, GPWS, configuration warnings)
Checklist discipline and standard callouts
ATC monitoring as external safety net
Hazard reporting and FOQA programmes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe error avoidance techniques
concept: Error avoidance techniques are the things you do to stop errors happening in the first place. Stay rested and current. Plan thoroughly and brief everything before you need it. Use checklists, don't skip them, don't rush them, and verify each item rather than ticking them off. Maintain situational awareness by deliberately scanning, asking questions, and updating your mental model. Manage workload — don't try to do everything at the busiest moment. Use automation appropriately. Apply standard procedures and resist shortcuts. Speak up when something doesn't feel right. Take a moment when surprised rather than reacting. Keep your training and currency up. None of this guarantees no errors — but it pushes the rate down dramatically.
exam_tip: Error avoidance: rest, currency, planning, checklists, SA, workload management, automation, SOPs, speaking up, pausing when surprised. None of it eliminates errors but the rate drops dramatically.
key_facts: Rest, currency and good health as foundation
Thorough planning and briefing
Disciplined checklist use
Active situational awareness maintenance
Workload management and automation use
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic elements and features of the Reason Model
concept: James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model explains how accidents happen in complex systems. Every defence in the system is imperfect — like a slice of Swiss cheese, it has holes. Holes come from latent failures (poor design, gaps in training, organisational pressures) and active failures (slips, lapses, mistakes by frontline operators). Most of the time, when one slice has a hole, the next slice covers it. An accident happens only when the holes line up — when latent failures, active failures, missed defences and unfortunate circumstances all coincide and the hazard punches all the way through. The implication is huge: you can't prevent accidents just by punishing the person at the sharp end of the active failure. You have to fix the latent holes too — the procedures, the training, the culture, the fatigue patterns. Reason's model is the foundation of modern safety management.
exam_tip: Reason model: defences are slices of Swiss cheese with holes. Latent + active failures align when holes line up. Fix latent holes in the system, not just the person at the sharp end.
key_facts: System defences are imperfect — holes from latent and active failures
Accident occurs when holes align across slices
Latent failures: design, training, culture, organisational pressures
Active failures: slips, lapses, mistakes by frontline operators
Foundation of modern SMS thinking
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe and identify examples of an active failure/error
concept: An active failure or error is an error or unsafe act committed at the sharp end by the person operating the system — a pilot, a controller, a mechanic — and it has an immediate or near-immediate effect on safety. Examples: a pilot setting the wrong altitude in the autopilot, a controller issuing the wrong heading, a mechanic mis-torquing a bolt during installation. Active failures are the visible bit of an accident chain — they're what investigators see first because they're closest in time and place to the event. But they're rarely the whole story. The same active failure happens many times every day across the system without causing an accident, because the other defences usually catch it. The accident happens when those other defences are also weakened.
exam_tip: Active failure = unsafe act at the sharp end with immediate effect. Visible, close in time and place to the accident. Examples: wrong altitude bug, wrong heading clearance, mis-torqued bolt.
key_facts: Unsafe act by frontline operator
Immediate or near-immediate effect
Visible to investigators
Close in time and place to accident
Rarely the whole story — other defences usually catch them
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe and identify examples of a latent failure/error
concept: A latent failure is a flaw in the system that exists for a long time before it contributes to an accident — and may never contribute to one. Examples: a procedure written ambiguously, a training programme that doesn't cover a specific failure mode, a roster pattern that creates chronic fatigue, a maintenance shortcut tolerated by management, a culture that discourages questioning, a chart that's been published with a typo, a piece of equipment that behaves unexpectedly under certain rare conditions. Latent failures aren't unsafe acts — they're conditions that make unsafe acts more likely or harder to catch. They sit in the background until an active failure or unusual condition activates them. Investigating an accident properly means tracing the active failure back through the latent ones to the underlying organisational and cultural conditions.
exam_tip: Latent failure = flaw in the system that exists in the background, makes accidents more likely, may never cause one alone. Examples: bad procedures, training gaps, fatigue rosters, weak culture.
key_facts: System flaw existing in the background
Not an unsafe act — a condition that allows them
Examples: procedures, training, rosters, culture, equipment quirks
May exist for years without causing harm
Activated by unusual conditions or active failures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify and describe slips, lapses, mistakes and violations
concept: Reason's taxonomy of unsafe acts has four types. Slips are when you intend the right action but execute the wrong one — flicking the gear lever instead of the flap lever, dialling the wrong frequency. Lapses are memory failures — forgetting to perform a step, missing a checklist item, failing to switch tanks at the planned time. Mistakes are when you do exactly what you intended, but what you intended was wrong — applying the wrong rule to the situation, or making a bad decision based on incorrect understanding. Violations are deliberate departures from procedures — knowingly skipping a checklist, descending below minima, exceeding limits — and they range from minor 'routine violations' through to serious sabotage. Slips and lapses are skill-based failures, mistakes are rule- or knowledge-based, violations are motivational. Each type needs different defences.
exam_tip: Slip = wrong action despite right intent. Lapse = memory failure. Mistake = wrong intent applied correctly. Violation = deliberate departure from procedure. Each has different causes and defences.
key_facts: Slip: right intent, wrong execution (skill-based)
Lapse: memory failure (skill-based)
Mistake: wrong plan applied correctly (rule/knowledge-based)
Violation: deliberate departure from procedure (motivational)
Each category needs different defences
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Identify the attributes of at-risk behaviour
concept: At-risk behaviour is any habit or pattern of action that increases the chance of an accident, often without the person realising or caring. The attributes include: complacency about familiar tasks (skipping checklists because 'I know this aircraft'), overconfidence about personal skill ('I've flown in worse'), shortcut-taking under time pressure, normalisation of deviance (small departures from procedure that nobody calls out, gradually becoming standard practice), pressing on into deteriorating conditions, ignoring or rationalising warnings, and poor self-monitoring. At-risk behaviour is often comfortable and feels efficient — that's exactly why it spreads. Recognising it in yourself and your colleagues, and gently calling it out, is part of a healthy safety culture.
exam_tip: At-risk behaviour: complacency, overconfidence, shortcuts, normalisation of deviance, pressing on, ignoring warnings, poor self-monitoring. Comfortable and efficient — that's why it spreads.
key_facts: Complacency about familiar tasks
Overconfidence about personal skill
Shortcut-taking under time pressure
Normalisation of deviance over time
Often comfortable — which is why it spreads
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the concepts of risk creep and risk tolerance and their application within an aviation organisation
concept: Risk creep is the gradual drift of acceptable risk over time as small departures from standards go uncorrected and become the new normal. A pilot who once never descended below 1,500 ft on a sightseeing flight may, after months of going to 1,200 with no consequences, find themselves at 1,000 — and then 800 — without ever consciously deciding to. Each step felt small, but the cumulative drift is dramatic. Risk tolerance is the level of risk an individual or organisation is willing to accept. In a healthy aviation organisation, risk tolerance is set deliberately, communicated clearly, monitored against actual practice, and re-affirmed regularly. Risk creep happens when no one is watching the gap between stated tolerance and actual practice. The fix is active monitoring — flight data monitoring, line audits, peer feedback, hazard reporting — and the willingness to call out small deviations before they become big ones.
exam_tip: Risk creep = gradual drift from standard, each step small, cumulative drift dramatic. Risk tolerance = level of risk accepted. Risk creep happens when nobody monitors the gap. Active monitoring is the fix.
key_facts: Risk creep: gradual drift from standards over time
Each step feels small; cumulative effect is large
Risk tolerance: level of risk an org or individual accepts
Healthy orgs set tolerance deliberately and monitor actively
Fix: FDM, audits, peer feedback, hazard reporting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: failure to monitor
concept: Advanced cockpit automation makes the pilot a system manager rather than a hands-on flyer for most of the time. One predictable effect is failure to monitor — the autopilot is doing the flying so well that the pilot stops paying close attention to what it's actually doing. The classic accident is the pilot who didn't notice the autopilot had reverted to a different mode, or had captured the wrong altitude, or had quietly disconnected. Failure to monitor isn't laziness — it's a natural consequence of being asked to watch an automated system that almost always behaves correctly. Defending against it requires deliberate scanning habits, structured callouts ('mode change confirmed', 'altitude capture'), and a cultural expectation that the pilot is always actively cross-checking the automation rather than just watching it.
exam_tip: Failure to monitor: pilot stops actively watching the automation because it usually works. Defended by deliberate scan habits, callouts, and a culture of active cross-checking.
key_facts: Pilot becomes a system manager, not active flyer
Natural to drift into passive watching
Common cause of automation-related accidents
Defended by deliberate scanning and callouts
Culture must expect active cross-checking, not passive observation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: boredom and complacency
concept: Boredom and complacency are the second predictable consequence of high automation. When the autopilot is flying smoothly through hour after hour of cruise, the pilot's arousal level drops, attention wanders, and complacency sets in. 'It's been fine for the last three hours, why would it suddenly stop being fine?' is the dangerous internal voice. Bored pilots miss subtle cues, take longer to notice problems, and are slower to react when something does go wrong. The defences are to plan deliberate engagement during cruise (cross-checking nav, monitoring fuel and engine trends, doing position reports, briefing the next phase), to take micro-breaks to stay alert, and to recognise that complacency feels like calmness — that's what makes it dangerous.
exam_tip: Boredom and complacency: arousal drops in long automated cruise, attention wanders, response times slow. Defend with deliberate engagement, monitoring tasks, and recognising the trap.
key_facts: Long automated cruise reduces arousal
Complacency sets in after periods of trouble-free operation
Pilots miss subtle cues and react slowly
Defend with deliberate cruise tasks and monitoring
Complacency feels like calmness — that's why it's dangerous
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: loss of proficiency
concept: Loss of proficiency is the slow erosion of hand-flying skills that happens when you let the autopilot do everything. If you only hand-fly the first 1,000 feet of climb and the last 1,000 feet of approach, the manual flying skills you spent years building gradually decay. When the autopilot fails — and it will, eventually — you may find yourself rusty exactly when you need the skill most. The same applies to mental skills like raw-data navigation, manual fuel calculations, and instrument scanning without the FD. The fix is to deliberately hand-fly portions of every flight when conditions allow — practise raw-data approaches, fly without the autopilot in the climb and descent, do mental calculations even when the FMS is doing them. Currency in manual skills has to be deliberately maintained.
exam_tip: Loss of proficiency: hand-flying and mental skills decay with disuse. Defend by deliberately hand-flying parts of every flight and practising raw-data and manual calculations.
key_facts: Hand-flying skills decay without practice
Mental navigation and calculation skills also decay
Raw-data instrument scan particularly affected
Defend by deliberate hand-flying on routine flights
Practice raw-data approaches in good conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of advanced cockpit automation, including: problems associated with equipment failure
concept: Problems associated with equipment failure in automated cockpits are often more dangerous than failures in less automated aircraft, for a couple of reasons. First, the pilot is rusty (loss of proficiency, see above). Second, the automation may have been hiding the problem until it can no longer cope, so the failure presents in a confusing or partial way. Third, automation failures often happen in the worst possible mode — the system disconnects without obvious annunciation, or it reverts to a mode the pilot didn't expect, or it gives misleading indications. Fourth, the workload spike from suddenly having to take over manual flying plus diagnose the failure plus manage the aircraft can overwhelm the pilot. Defending against this means knowing the failure modes of your automation, practising manual reversion in the simulator, and trusting your basic instrument scan when things get confusing.
exam_tip: Automation failures: pilot is rusty, failure may be confusing or partial, workload spikes. Know the failure modes, practise manual reversion in the sim, trust your basic scan.
key_facts: Pilot may be rusty when the automation fails
Failures often partial or confusingly annunciated
Mode reversions may not be obvious
Workload spikes during diagnosis and manual takeover
Practise manual reversion in the simulator
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of mode awareness in setting up and operating automated systems
concept: Mode awareness is knowing exactly which mode the autopilot, flight director and FMS are in at any moment, and what they will do next. Modern automation has many modes — heading hold, NAV, LNAV, VNAV, altitude hold, vertical speed, FLCH, approach, GS, GA, etc. — and they interact in subtle ways. Mode confusion happens when the pilot thinks the system is in one mode but it's actually in another. Famous examples include the Strasbourg A320 accident (pilot thought they had selected -3.3 degree flight path, system was in vertical speed mode at -3,300 fpm) and several altitude busts and CFIT incidents. The defences are to verbally confirm mode changes ('VNAV armed', 'GS captured'), to scan the FMA (flight mode annunciator) every time you make a mode change, to brief the expected automation behaviour for each phase, and to react with suspicion any time the aircraft does something you didn't expect.
exam_tip: Mode awareness = knowing exactly which mode the automation is in. Mode confusion has caused major accidents (Strasbourg A320). Defend by verbal callouts, FMA scan, and expecting the unexpected.
key_facts: Knowing the current and next mode of the automation
Modern automation has many interacting modes
Mode confusion has caused major fatal accidents
Confirm mode changes verbally and scan the FMA
Investigate any unexpected aircraft behaviour immediately
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe elements of coping behaviour associated with automatic cockpits
concept: Coping behaviour in automated cockpits is the set of habits pilots develop to stay engaged and effective when the system is doing most of the work. Good coping behaviours include: deliberate cross-checking of the automation against raw data and against your own expectations, asking 'what is it doing now and why?', verbalising mode changes, planning ahead so the next phase is briefed before it arrives, taking responsibility for the flight rather than delegating it to the box, hand-flying sections to maintain proficiency, and being willing to disconnect the automation when it's doing something unexpected rather than wrestling with it. Bad coping behaviours include passive watching, blind trust in the automation, fixation on the FMS while the aircraft does its own thing, and trying to reprogramme rather than disconnect when problems arise. The mantra 'aviate, navigate, communicate, automate' — in that order — is a useful summary.
exam_tip: Good coping: deliberate cross-checks, mode callouts, planning ahead, hand-flying sometimes, disconnecting when in doubt. Bad coping: passive watching, blind trust, fixation, reprogramming during emergencies. 'Aviate, navigate, communicate, automate.'
key_facts: Deliberate cross-checking against raw data
Verbalising mode changes and planned actions
Maintaining hand-flying proficiency
Disconnecting rather than wrestling with the automation
'Aviate, navigate, communicate, automate' mantra
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: size
concept: The size of a control matters because it has to be large enough to operate accurately, especially with gloves on or in turbulence. Critical controls — landing gear, flap, throttle quadrant — are deliberately made larger than secondary controls so the pilot can find and operate them quickly without looking. Tiny controls invite errors because the hand can't precisely hit them, especially when the aircraft is bouncing around. Equally, controls that are too large for their function take up valuable panel space and can be confused with neighbouring controls. The size has to suit both the importance of the function and the realistic operating environment.
exam_tip: Control size matters for accurate operation, especially with gloves or in turbulence. Critical controls are deliberately larger so they can be found and operated quickly without looking.
key_facts: Must be large enough to operate accurately under stress
Critical controls (gear, flap, throttle) deliberately larger
Too small invites operating errors
Too large wastes panel space and causes confusion
Sized for the realistic operating environment, not the showroom
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: shape/recognition by touch
concept: Shape and recognition by touch is the principle that controls with different functions should feel different so the pilot can identify them without looking. The classic example is the gear handle (shaped like a small wheel) and the flap handle (shaped like a small flap section) — you can tell them apart in the dark, in the rain, with cold hands. This wasn't always the case; early aircraft had identical handles for gear and flap and gear-up landings on the runway were common. The rule was made standard after that pattern of accidents. Modern aircraft extend the principle to other critical controls — fuel cutoffs, fire handles, emergency landing gear extension — each with a distinctive shape and texture.
exam_tip: Shape coding lets pilots identify controls by touch alone — gear handle is wheel-shaped, flap handle is flap-shaped. The standard came in after a pattern of gear-up landings.
key_facts: Different functions should have different shapes/textures
Gear handle = wheel shape; flap handle = flap shape
Standardised after early accidents involving identical handles
Allows identification without looking
Extended to fuel cutoffs, fire handles, emergency extensions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: location
concept: Control location matters because the pilot's hands have to find them quickly and consistently, often without looking. Throttles in the centre console, flap handle on the central pedestal, gear handle on the panel below the PFD, transponder and comm panels in standard locations — these conventions exist so a pilot moving between aircraft types can still find the basic controls in roughly the same place. Critical controls should be placed where they can be reached without releasing the primary flight controls. Less critical controls go in less accessible spots. Bad location design leads to slow operation, missed actions in emergencies, and inadvertent activation of the wrong control when reaching for the right one.
exam_tip: Location should make critical controls easy to find and reach without looking or releasing primary flight controls. Standard locations across types help pilots transition between aircraft.
key_facts: Critical controls reachable without releasing primary controls
Standard locations across aircraft aid type transitions
Less critical controls placed in less accessible spots
Bad location causes slow operation in emergencies
Inadvertent activation risk if controls clustered too tightly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: layout and the uniformity of spatial arrangement
concept: Layout and uniformity of spatial arrangement is about grouping related controls together and arranging them in a logical, repeatable pattern. Engine instruments cluster together. Comm radios stack one above the other. Switches for related systems sit on the same panel. The 'T' arrangement of primary flight instruments (airspeed, attitude, altimeter, heading) is the classic example — it's been standard for decades because pilots' eyes move predictably across it. When layouts change between aircraft types, pilots transitioning have to consciously re-learn where things are, and that re-learning is a source of errors. Good layout design respects the way human eyes and hands actually work and stays consistent within a fleet.
exam_tip: Layout: group related controls, use logical patterns, keep arrangements consistent. The basic T of primary flight instruments is the classic example. Layout changes between types cause errors.
key_facts: Group related controls together logically
Standard 'T' of primary flight instruments
Consistent layouts across a fleet reduce errors
Layout changes between types require re-learning
Designed around how human eyes and hands actually move
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: direction of movement
concept: Direction of movement of a control should match the expected effect — what ergonomists call stimulus-response compatibility. Push the throttle forward to go faster, pull it back to slow down. Pull the yoke back to pitch up. Move the heading bug clockwise to turn right. When this matches you don't have to think — your hand goes the right way automatically, even under stress. When it doesn't match (a few aircraft have non-standard conventions on certain switches), pilots make slip errors, especially under high workload. Standardising direction of movement across the industry has eliminated many sources of confusion that existed in early aviation.
exam_tip: Direction of movement should match expected effect (stimulus-response compatibility). Throttle forward = faster, yoke back = pitch up. Mismatches cause slip errors under stress.
key_facts: Direction of movement should match expected effect
Stimulus-response compatibility principle
Throttle forward = faster, yoke back = pitch up
Mismatches cause slip errors under high workload
Standardisation has eliminated many early confusions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of the following in control design: visibility
concept: Visibility of a control means you can see it clearly when you need to look at it — it's not blocked by your hand, the yoke, your knee, the sun, or the glare of cockpit lighting. A control that's only visible from a specific seat position, or that's hidden behind another control most of the time, will be operated wrongly when you can't see it. Visibility also includes the indication associated with the control — gear position lights, flap position indicator, switch labelling — so you can verify the control was actually moved to the position you intended. Designs that hide critical controls or their indicators cause errors that good ergonomic principles would prevent.
exam_tip: Visibility: control and its indicator must be clearly visible from the normal seat position. Hidden controls or indicators cause verification errors.
key_facts: Control must be visible from normal seat position
Not blocked by hand, yoke, knee, glare
Indicator showing position must also be visible
Verification depends on seeing both the control and the result
Hidden controls cause errors that good design would prevent
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe common errors in display interpretation
concept: Common errors in display interpretation include misreading a number (5 vs 3 vs 8 on small digital displays), confusing two similar-looking instruments (HSI vs ADI on glass cockpits at a glance), reading the wrong scale (knots vs Mach, ft vs m), misreading a needle (transposing the hundreds and thousands hand on a three-pointer altimeter), looking at the wrong display entirely (reading the standby instead of the primary), and missing a small digital indication that's overwhelmed by surrounding information. Other errors come from the format itself — round dial vs tape vs digital all have different error modes. Good display design uses unambiguous formats, clear labelling, consistent units, and visual prominence that matches the importance of the information.
exam_tip: Display errors: misread numbers, confused instruments, wrong scale, misread needles, looking at wrong display. Three-pointer altimeter is a classic. Good design uses clear formats and prominence.
key_facts: Misreading digits or transposing numbers
Confusing similar instruments (HSI vs ADI)
Reading wrong scale or wrong units
Three-pointer altimeter known for misreading errors
Looking at standby instead of primary display
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe potential errors in the interpretation of the artificial horizon
concept: The artificial horizon (AI/ADI) is critical and has known interpretation traps. The biggest is somatogravic illusion combined with leans — when the body's vestibular sense disagrees with the AI, the pilot may not believe the instrument and tilt the aircraft to match their (wrong) body sense. Other errors include misreading bank angle from the bank pointer (especially on older analogue AIs where the pointer is small), confusion about which direction the horizon line is moving (the aircraft symbol stays fixed, the horizon moves — but it can look the opposite under stress), pitch attitude misread on small displays, and full failures where the AI is still showing 'something' but it's not correct. The defence is to cross-check the AI against turn coordinator, altimeter, VSI and ASI continuously rather than fixating on the AI alone.
exam_tip: Artificial horizon errors: somatogravic illusion + leans (body sense overrides instrument), bank angle misread, pitch misread on small displays, partial failures. Cross-check against TC, alt, VSI, ASI continuously.
key_facts: Somatogravic illusion + leans makes pilots disbelieve the AI
Bank angle errors on small/analogue AIs
Pitch attitude errors on small displays
Partial failures may show plausible but wrong attitudes
Cross-check against TC, altimeter, VSI and ASI continuously
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the presentation and misinterpretation of alerts
concept: Alerts are designed to grab attention and prompt the right action, but they can fail in several ways. Too many alerts at once cause information overload — the pilot can't tell which is the most important and may freeze. Alerts that are too quiet or visually subtle get missed, especially in high noise/vibration. Alerts that are too loud or persistent become irritating and pilots may suppress them without first understanding what they're for. Crying wolf — repeated false alerts — trains pilots to ignore the alert when it eventually means something. Misinterpretation happens when alert symbology or wording is unclear. Modern alerting systems try to prioritise alerts by criticality (warning > caution > advisory) and use different colours and tones for different urgency levels — but the underlying problem of attention management never fully goes away.
exam_tip: Alert problems: overload, too quiet, too loud/irritating, false alarms training pilots to ignore, misinterpretation. Modern systems prioritise by criticality (warning/caution/advisory) with distinct colours and tones.
key_facts: Too many alerts at once cause overload
Subtle alerts may be missed in noisy cockpit
Persistent alerts get suppressed without understanding
False alerts train pilots to ignore real ones
Modern systems use criticality levels with distinct cues
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the design and use of checklists and manuals
concept: Checklists and manuals have their own design problems. Checklists that are too long encourage pilots to rush or skip items. Checklists with poorly worded items invite misinterpretation. Order matters — items in the wrong sequence can lead to missing something critical. Manuals that are dense, badly indexed, or full of cross-references are slow to use in flight. Layout, font size, paper quality, lighting and the physical handling of paper documents all affect usability. Electronic checklists solve some problems (always current, easy to update) but introduce others (display failures, distraction from the screen). Good design is short, clear, sequenced, clearly labelled, and easy to use one-handed in turbulence under poor lighting.
exam_tip: Checklist/manual problems: too long, poorly worded, wrong sequence, dense layout, hard to find. Good design: short, clear, sequenced, one-handed-friendly. Electronic versions solve some problems but introduce others.
key_facts: Too-long checklists encourage rushing or skipping
Poor wording invites misinterpretation
Order/sequence affects safety
Dense manuals slow in-flight reference
Electronic checklists solve some issues, create others
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes

syllabus_desc: Describe problems associated with the design and use of maps and charts
concept: Maps and charts have known design and use problems. Cluttered charts with too much information make critical features hard to find. Tiny fonts on terminal charts under poor cockpit lighting cause misreading. Symbols that look similar (different VOR types, different airspace categories) can be confused. Out-of-date charts cause dangerous errors when the user doesn't realise the publication has changed. Folding paper charts is slow and awkward in a cramped cockpit. Electronic charts on EFBs solve some problems (always current, zoomable, geo-referenced) but introduce others (battery failure, screen glare, fingertip selection errors, distraction). Good map design uses clear symbology, prioritises essential information, and is legible under realistic cockpit conditions.
exam_tip: Map/chart problems: clutter, tiny fonts, similar symbols, out-of-date publications, awkward folding. EFBs solve some problems but introduce battery/glare/fat-finger issues. Verify currency before flight.
key_facts: Clutter hides critical features
Tiny fonts misread under poor cockpit lighting
Similar symbols confused under stress
Out-of-date charts dangerous if user unaware
EFBs solve some problems, introduce battery/glare/touch errors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-17 Rev 17, CAA Good Aviation Practice — Human Factors, AFT Human Factors course notes
